Materials_origin.Acemetacin_and_indomethacin: Gift sample from Delta Pharma, Egypt
Materials_origin.Span_60_cholesterol_dicetylphosphate_ste: Sigma Chemical Co., St. Louis, MO, USA
Materials_origin.Maltodextrin_and_Avicel: Gift samples from Egyptian International Pharmaceutical Industries Co., EPICO, Egypt
Materials_origin.Chloroform_and_other_chemicals: El-Nasr Pharmaceuticals Chemical Co., Cairo, Egypt

Experimental_Design_for_Optimization.Preparation_method: Slurry method using maltodextrin as carrier. Lipid mixture (250 μmol) and AC (60 mg) dissolved in chloroform/diethyl ether (1:1 v/v), added to maltodextrin (1:1 ratio with surfactant), solvent evaporated under reduced pressure (70 rpm, 60°C±2°C).
Experimental_Design_for_Optimization.Variables: Molar ratios of Span 60 and cholesterol, inclusion of positive (stearylamine) or negative (dicetylphosphate) charge, drug loading constant (60 mg AC).
Experimental_Design_for_Optimization.Optimization_criteria: Entrapment efficiency (%) and in vitro release rate.
Experimental_Design_for_Optimization.Best_formulation: Span 60/Chol/SA (70:20:10) with EE% 85.94% and slowest release.

Pre_compression_parameters.Note: Flowability of proniosomal powders equal or better than pure maltodextrin. No per-batch pre-compression data reported.

Composition_of_independent_variables.Independent_variables: Molar ratios of Span 60, cholesterol, stearylamine (SA), dicetylphosphate (DCP)
Composition_of_independent_variables.Fixed_parameters: Total lipid 250 μmol, AC 60 mg, maltodextrin:surfactant mass ratio 1:1

Proniosome_preparation.Conditions: 70 rpm, 60°C±2°C, reduced pressure, then vacuum desiccator overnight at room temperature

Tablet_compression.Device: Single-punch tablet machine (Korsch Frogerais, type AO, Berlin, Germany)

Observations: Negatively charged (DCP) showed fastest release, neutral intermediate, positively charged (SA) slowest release due to electrostatic attraction with AC.
In_vitro_release_from_proniosome_derived.Factors_influencing: Cholesterol concentration, presence of charged lipids (SA or DCP), pH (only pH 7.4 tested for niosome release)

Fig3_release_data.Description: Effect of Chol concentration on in vitro release of AC from Span 60 proniosome-derived niosomes (PBS pH 7.4, 37°C). Approximated from Figure 3.

Fig4_release_data.Description: Effect of charged lipid on in vitro release of AC from Span 60 proniosome-derived niosomes (PBS pH 7.4, 37°C). Approximated from Figure 4.

Fig5_release_data.Description: In vitro release of AC from proniosomes powder and tablet in comparison to AC plain tablet. Conditions: first 2h in HCl pH 1.2, then PBS pH 7.4, 37°C, USP basket 50 rpm. Approximated from Figure 5. Plain tablet reached 100% at 8h; proniosome powder 75% at 8h, 80% at 24h; proniosome tablet 58% at 8h, 60% at 24h.

In_vitro_dissolution_from_tablets_and_po.Method: USP XXIII apparatus I (rotating basket), 50 rpm, 37°C, first 2 h in HCl pH 1.2 then PBS pH 7.4 up to 24 h
In_vitro_dissolution_from_tablets_and_po.Factors_influencing: pH (slow release in acid, rapid in alkaline), disintegration time (tablet slower than powder), sustained release effect of proniosomes.

Release_Properties.Release_kinetics: Not modeled (no zero-order, first-order, Hixson-Crowell, Korsmeyer-Peppas reported).

In_vivo_pharmacokinetics.Animal_model: Male rabbits (1.5-2 kg), single oral dose 30 mg AC/kg
In_vivo_pharmacokinetics.Groups: AC plain tablet, AC proniosome powder (70:20:10), AC proniosome-loaded tablet (70:20:10)
In_vivo_pharmacokinetics.Analyte: AC and its active metabolite indomethacin (IND) in plasma by HPLC
In_vivo_pharmacokinetics.Statistical_analysis: One-way ANOVA followed by LSD post hoc test (P<0.05 considered significant).

data.Compression_method: Direct compression, single-punch tablet machine (Korsch Frogerais, type AO, Berlin, Germany), flat-faced 10 mm punches

data.Formulation_per_tablet: AC proniosomes (Span 60/Chol/SA 70:20:10) equivalent to 60 mg AC, Avicel® 70 mg, total 300 mg
data.Compression_method: Direct compression, same machine and punch

Materials_origin.Neratinib: Mesochem Technology Co., Ltd. Beijing, China

article_title: Development of Bilayer Tablets with Modified Release of Selected Incompatible Drugs

article_metadata.title: Development of Bilayer Tablets with Modified Release of Selected Incompatible Drugs
article_metadata.objective: Develop bilayer tablets with conventional release TMS and slow-release SVT to co-formulate incompatible drugs safely in one tablet.

key_outcomes.summary: TMS layer achieved ≥85% release by 45 min; SVT layer achieved ~80% by 8 h with no SVT release in first hour at pH 1.2.
key_outcomes.best_formulation_note: 003_F4 gave the highest TMS 1 h release and acceptable SVT control; maps to 003_T4/003_TMS4/003_SVT4 in this schema.

APIs.telmisartan: Gift sample, Medley Pharmaceuticals Ltd., Daman, India
APIs.simvastatin: Gift sample, Lincoln Pharmaceuticals Ltd., Ahmedabad, India

excipients.croscarmellose_sodium: SD Fine Chem. Ltd., Mumbai, India
excipients.HPMC_K15M: SD Fine Chem. Ltd., Mumbai, India
excipients.PVP_K30: Central Drug House (P) Ltd., Mumbai, India
excipients.aerosil: Listed in TMS layer; commercial source not specified

experimental_design_for_optimization.design_type: Eight trial formulations per layer (F1–F8) varying superdisintegrant in TMS layer; binder type/level, HPMC/Carbopol, croscarmellose/DCP in SVT layer; combined to bilayer tablets F1–F8.
response_variables: Granule flow indices (bulk/tapped density, Hausner ratio, Carr’s index, angle of repose)
Tablet hardness, friability, thickness, weight variation, layer separation, disintegration
Dissolution profiles in 0.1M HCl (1 h) then pH 6.8 buffer (7 h)
Release kinetics fits (zero, first order, Higuchi, Peppas) and ANOVA

analysis_methods.statistics: Two-way ANOVA with Bonferroni post-tests; p < 0.0001 threshold

FTIR.compatibility_findings: Layers individually show preserved API characteristic peaks; physical mixture of layers shows reduced intensity/shifted/disappeared peaks, evidencing incompatibility when blended.

equations.tapped_density: \(Tapped density = \frac{Weight of granules}{Final volume after tapping}\)

granulation.sieving: #80 pre-sift; wet mass through #16; dried mass through #22
granulation.binders: SVT layer: starch paste (2–5% w/v) or starch powder

compression.press: 10-station rotary press (M26 A12, Karnavati Engineering Ltd., Ahmedabad)

testing_equipment.dissolution: USP II (DS 8000), 900 mL, 100 rpm, 37 ± 0.5°C; 0.1M HCl 1 h then pH 6.8

method_overview.sequence: 0.1M HCl (pH 1.2) for 1 h then phosphate buffer (pH 6.8) for 7 h at 100 rpm, 37 ± 0.5°C
method_overview.findings_summary: No SVT release in first hour; TMS shows rapid release; SVT shows controlled release to ~80% by 8 h depending on batch.

TMS_release_highlights.note: TMS release increased with higher croscarmellose; 003_F4 (003_TMS4) achieved 93.84 ± 0.99% at 1 h.
TMS_release_highlights.criterion: Conventional release target >80% in 45 min satisfied.

release_mechanism_interpretation.model_fit: SVT release best fits first-order and Higuchi models across batches.
release_mechanism_interpretation.mechanism: Diffusion-controlled release with swelling; Peppas n between 0.5 and 1 indicates Fickian diffusion with swelling.

two_way_anova.significance: Significant differences among SVT dissolution profiles F1–F8; polymer composition significantly affects release.

disintegration.TMS_layer: 4–15 min depending on batch and croscarmellose level
disintegration.SVT_layer: Not fully disintegrated within 2 h for all batches

section_locator.abstract: Study aim, design summary, FTIR compatibility statement, and headline dissolution/outcomes.
section_locator.introduction: Rationale for bilayer approach with TMS/SVT, incompatibility context, therapeutic logic.
section_locator.materials: API and excipient sources; instruments listed.
section_locator.methods: Granulation, drying, compression, instruments, test conditions, equations; Tables 1–2 compositions.
section_locator.results_discussion: Preformulation (melting point, UV λmax), FTIR assignments (Table 3), micromeritic data (Tables 4–5), tablet tests (Tables 6–8), dissolution and kinetics (Figures 4–5, Table 9), ANOVA (Table 10).
section_locator.conclusion: Best batch summary and mechanism statement; compliance benefits.

Materials_origin.acarbose: Ranbaxy Research Laboratories Ltd., Gurgaon, India [file:1]
Materials_origin.fenugreek_gum: Emerald Seed Products Ltd., Saskatchewan, Canada [file:1]
Materials_origin.talc: S.D. Fine Chemicals Ltd., Mumbai, India [file:1]
Materials_origin.magnesium_stearate: S.D. Fine Chemicals Ltd., Mumbai, India [file:1]

Experimental_design_for_optimization.approach: Matrix tablets with different ratios of gums; direct compression for tablet formation; factorial design for evaluating release control [file:1]
Experimental_design_for_optimization.animal_model: Male Wistar rats, high-fructose diet induced Type II diabetes; 5 groups: control, diabetic, IR treated, CR treated, placebo [file:1]

ingredients_identification_characterizat.preparation: Direct compression of physical mixtures, geometric addition, blending, and compression into tablets using single-punch tableting machine [file:1]
ingredients_identification_characterizat.results: Tablets meet IP requirements. Drug content: 99.01-100.70%. Validation RP-HPLC (λ=210 nm); FTIR: no interaction between drug and polymers (bands: 3371, 1652, 1418, 1152 cm⁻¹) [file:1]
ingredients_identification_characterizat.absorbance: UV spectrophotometer used at λmax 210 nm for drug determination [file:1]

Fourier_transform_infrared_spectral_meas.findings: No significant interaction between acarbose and gums; major peaks for OH, CC, CO and water stretching confirmed [file:1]

Tablet_fabrications_and_evaluation.equipment: Single-station tablet machine (Modern Engineering, New Delhi), biconcave die punch 9.7 mm diameter [file:1]

process_parameters.pressure: Not specified, but using biconcave punch set [file:1]

Tablet_fabrications_and_evaluation.batch_evaluations: Physical parameters (weight variation, thickness, hardness, friability, drug content) as per pharmacopoeial standards [file:1]

stability.004_AF40: No significant change after 6 months; f2=83 (>50); shelf life 1.47 years [file:1]

swelling_index.004_AF40\u2013004_AF60: Swelling proportional to fenugreek gum concentration, max at 12h, with gel formation; ALBG/AB: initial swelling, then erosion or fragmentation [file:1]

in_vitro.release: See release profiles; kinetic models applied (first order, Higuchi, Korsmeyer-Peppas, Hixson Crowell)
in_vitro.swelling_water_uptake: 004_AF40–004_AF60: lag ~30min, max swelling at 12h; others erode quicker [file:1]

in_vivo.animal_model: Wistar rats; 004_AF40 selected; significant decrease in postprandial blood glucose and triglycerides; placebo (fenugreek gum) effective alone [file:1]
in_vivo.body_weight: Reduction in weight gain in sustained release group; 004_AF40 body weight decreased from 84.33±3.35 to 66.78±5.78 g in 5 days [file:1]
in_vivo.side_effects: Occurrence of diarrhea lowered with sustained release formulation; food intake increased (004_AF40 group ate 80–95g in 72h) [file:1]

Experimental_data.statistics: Significance stated as p<0.001 for glucose, triglycerides, body weight [file:1]

Formulations: IR tablets (paracetamol STADA 500 mg, STADA Arzneimittel AG, Germany)
Hard capsules (ben-u-ron 500 mg, bene-Arzneimittel GmbH, Germany)
Soft capsules (APAPcaps 500 mg, US Pharmacia Sp. z o.o., Poland)
Modified release bi-layer (ER) tablets (Osteomol 665 mg, Pharmacor Pty Ltd., Australia)

Experimental Design for Optimization.Dissolution Model: Novel in vitro dissolution method using USP apparatus 3 (reciprocating cylinder) to compare four commercially available acetaminophen formulations under single and overdose conditions.
Experimental Groups: Overdose (10, 20, and 50 tablets/capsules for IR; 10 and 20 for ER)

ingredients identification.Preparation: Tablets and capsules were purchased commercially. IR tablets were crushed and pulverized for solubility testing.
ingredients identification.Equation: Standard calibration curve (Concentration vs Absorbance) was used for HPLC quantification (detection at 240 nm, R² > 0.999).
ingredients identification.Absorbance: Peak detection for acetaminophen at 240 nm in HPLC analysis.

Pre-compression parameters of acetaminop.Hausner ratio: Not explicitly stated for this study, but typically calculated as tapped/bulk density

Osteomol 665 mg ER tablets.Excipients: Not detailed in this article, but typical ER formulations include hydrophilic matrix polymers (e.g., HPMC), fillers, binders, and lubricants.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectral Meas.Method: Not performed in this study. (Other studies confirm FTIR is used for compatibility, showing no interaction between acetaminophen and excipients.)

Parameters.Drug content in tablet: Labeled as 665 mg acetaminophen per ER tablet.

Process Parameters.Compression process: Not detailed for commercial tablets in this study.
Equipment used: USP apparatus 2 (PTDT 820D, Pharma Test Apparatebau AG, Germany) for preliminary dissolution
USP apparatus 3 (RRT 10 Caleva, ERWEKA, Germany) for main dissolution studies

Release Properties (formulations for ext.Release mechanism: Initial burst (IR fraction) followed by sustained release (ER fraction); release slowed at higher doses due to clumping and limited media access.
Factors influencing release: Dose (higher dose = slower, less extensive release due to clumping)
GI pH (media changes simulate GI tract)
Physical obstruction (clumping, pharmacobezoar formation at high dose)
Release Properties (formulations for ext.Release kinetics: Not explicitly modeled in this article; sustained release profile observed.

Tablet Properties (for extended release.Disintegration time: Outer layer disintegrates rapidly; core persists for several hours

Key results: Single ER tablet: nearly complete release over 6 hours
Higher doses: significant reduction in release due to clumping
Results from in vitro studies (Summary): Single ER tablet: 50.6 ± 1.5% release in 10 min (FaSSGF), 67.3 ± 1.5% at 60 min, 92.2 ± 2.0% at 6 hrs
10 ER tablets: 25.1 ± 5.6% at 60 min, 60.7 ± 12% at 6 hrs
20 ER tablets: 7.4 ± 0.8% at 60 min, 25.3 ± 12.7% at 6 hrs
Performance metrics: Percent released at each time point (mean ± SD, n=3)
Similarity factor f2 (FDA guideline: f2 ≥ 50 indicates similar profiles)
Experimental Data.Statistical analysis: Data presented as mean ± SD; ANOVA and Holm-Sidak post-hoc tests applied; similarity factor f2 calculated for dissolution profile comparison.
Experimental Data.Zero and first order release: Not specifically reported; release profile suggests non-linear (complex) kinetics at high dose

Polyethylene_oxide.PEO_750: Polyox WSR 750, MW = 3x10^5, manufactured by Dow Chemical (Philadelphia, USA), distributed by Colorcon (Kent, UK)
Polyethylene_oxide.PEO_303: Polyox WSR 303, MW = 7x10^6, manufactured by Dow Chemical (Philadelphia, USA), distributed by Colorcon (Kent, UK)

Materials_origin.Lactose: α-lactose monohydrate, donated by DMV International, Netherlands

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Materials were individually weighed and mixed for 10 minutes (except magnesium stearate) using a TURBULA blender at 100 rpm. Magnesium stearate was then mixed with the final mixture for an additional 2 minutes.
ingredients_identification.results: The study identified that smaller PEO particle sizes and higher molecular weight (PEO 303) resulted in harder tablets and slower drug release. Larger particle sizes and lower molecular weight (PEO 750) led to faster release and higher porosity.
ingredients_identification.absorbance: UV spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) at 271 nm for theophylline.

data.Fourier_Transform_Infrared_Spectral_Meas: Not available in the provided text.

Tablet_Fabrications.Process: A specific amount (350 mg) of the blended powder containing 1% magnesium stearate was compressed using an ENERPA C Hydraulic Press RC-53 (USA), fitted with 10-mm round and flat-faced punches.

Experimental_Data.key_results: Smaller PEO particle sizes produce harder tablets with slower drug release. The effect of particle size is more pronounced at lower polymer concentrations (20%) and with higher molecular weight PEO (303).

data.Release_Profile: Slowest release among A series; 88.0% DE, 1.42 h MDT.
data.Release_Mechanism: Primarily erosion (n=1.11 > 1, indicating super case-II transport).
data.Factors_Influencing_Release: Small particle size facilitates rapid gel formation, slowing release.
Medium particle size provides moderate gel formation.
Larger particle size delays gel formation, increasing release rate.

data.Release_Profile: Fastest release among A series; 96.4% DE, 0.44 h MDT.
data.Release_Mechanism: Primarily diffusion (data too quick to calculate n, indicating burst release).
data.Factors_Influencing_Release: Large particle size leads to slow hydration and high porosity, causing burst release.

data.Release_Profile: Slowest release among B series; 56.0% DE, 5.20 h MDT.
data.Release_Mechanism: Anomalous transport (n=0.82, mixed diffusion and erosion).
data.Factors_Influencing_Release: High MW and small particle size create strong, thick gel layer.
Medium particle size with high MW still provides good control.
Larger particles hydrate slower, increasing release rate.

data.Release_Profile: Fastest release among B series; 79.0% DE, 2.49 h MDT.
data.Factors_Influencing_Release: Large particles hydrate slowly, but high MW still provides some control.

data.Release_Profile: Slower release than 006_C4; 80.0% DE, 1.47 h MDT.
data.Factors_Influencing_Release: High polymer concentration reduces impact of particle size on release rate.

data.Release_Profile: Faster release than 006_C1; 88.0% DE, 1.39 h MDT.
data.Factors_Influencing_Release: Release profile similar to 006_C1 (f2 > 50) at this high concentration.

data.Release_Profile: Slowest release overall; 41.0% DE, 6.20 h MDT.
data.Factors_Influencing_Release: High MW and concentration, and small particle size yield best control.

data.Release_Profile: Faster release than 006_D1; 58.0% DE, 5.00 h MDT.
data.Factors_Influencing_Release: Particle size effect is significant even at high concentration for high MW PEO (f2 < 50 vs 006_D1).

article_sections.abstract: Present; includes aims, formulations (atenolol/atorvastatin ODTs 008_F1–008_F6; metformin tablets 008_F1–008_F3), evaluation tests, and key findings on disintegration and release.
article_sections.introduction: Present; covers roles of excipients, Plantago ovata arabinoxylan, and rationale for ODTs of atenolol/atorvastatin and metformin tablets.
article_sections.materials: Present under Materials and Methods; lists API sources, excipient sources, and isolation of arabinoxylan.
article_sections.methods: Present; includes arabinoxylan isolation, tablet preparation (direct compression for ODTs; wet granulation for metformin), FTIR, pre- and post-compression tests, dissolution conditions and UV wavelengths.
article_sections.results_and_discussion: Present; reports FTIR compatibility, flow indices, tablet properties, wetting/absorption, and dissolution outcomes with figures and tables.
article_sections.conclusion: Implied in discussion; arabinoxylan functions as an effective binder and superdisintegrant, outperforming husk and SSG in several tests.

materials_origin.apis: Atenolol, atorvastatin, and metformin obtained as gifts from Warrick Pharmaceuticals, Islamabad, Pakistan.
materials_origin.arabinoxylan_source: Ispaghula (Plantago ovata) husk purchased from local market in Sargodha, Pakistan.
materials_origin.other_excipients: Sodium starch glycolate, talc, microcrystalline cellulose, saccharin sodium, starch, magnesium stearate; analytical grade.

experimental_design_for_optimization.design_type: Comparative formulations; no formal factorial/RSM optimization reported.
experimental_design_for_optimization.independent_factors: Type and level of superdisintegrant in ODTs (arabinoxylan 12/9/6 mg; ispaghula husk 12 mg; sodium starch glycolate 12 mg); binder type in metformin (starch vs arabinoxylan; arabinoxylan also as superdisintegrant).
experimental_design_for_optimization.dependent_responses: Flow indices, hardness, thickness, diameter, friability, weight variation, disintegration time, wetting time, water absorption ratio, dissolution profile.

arabinoxylan_isolation.procedure: 100 g husk soaked overnight in 5 L water; pH adjusted to 12 with 2.5% NaOH; husk removed; gel coagulated with conc. acetic acid to pH 3; washed to constant pH and freeze-dried ~1 week; yield ~45% gel.
arabinoxylan_isolation.reference_method: Method of Saghir et al. (2008) for arabinoxylan isolation referenced by authors.

uv_vis_assay_conditions.notes: Samples filtered/diluted; quantified during dissolution testing in pH 6.8 buffer.

equations_and_measurements.tapped_density: ρ_tapped = M / Vf (USP Method I; final volume after tapping).

008_F4.note: Direct compression ODT; husk as superdisintegrant.

008_F5.note: Direct compression ODT; SSG as superdisintegrant.

008_F6.note: Formulation listed in table; no superdisintegrant specified; no evaluation data reported in result tables.

008_M1.note: Wet granulation; starch as binder, SSG as superdisintegrant.

008_M2.note: Wet granulation; arabinoxylan as binder, SSG as superdisintegrant.

008_M3.note: Wet granulation; arabinoxylan as binder and superdisintegrant.

008_F1.notes: Passable flow per USP scale (as discussed)

008_F2.notes: Passable flow per USP scale (as discussed)

008_F3.notes: Passable flow per USP scale (as discussed)

008_F6.notes: No precompression table entry provided for 008_F6

ftir.method: KBr pellet method; 2 mg sample in 200 mg KBr, pressed at 150 kg/cm^2; spectra recorded for pure atenolol, atorvastatin, metformin and their physical mixtures with arabinoxylan.
ftir.result: No band shifts or additional peaks observed, indicating compatibility between arabinoxylan and APIs.

fabrication.atenolol_atorvastatin: Direct compression; all ingredients passed through mesh 60, blended by tumbling; compressed with 8 mm round flat punches on 10‑station rotary tablet machine.
fabrication.metformin: Wet granulation; sieved (1.0 mm), mixed 10 min; water granulated; granulated through 2.5 mm (25 mm likely typo?) screen; oven-dried at 40°C for 1 h; resized to 1 mm; 1% w/w superdisintegrant and 2% w/w lubricant added; compression.

equipment_and_conditions.weight_variation: 20 tablets; Shimadzu electronic balance; acceptance ranges noted (±7.5 mg for ODTs; 5% for metformin).
equipment_and_conditions.hardness: Digital hardness tester (Pharma Test, Germany); mean of three tablets.
equipment_and_conditions.thickness_diameter: Digital apparatus (Pharma Test, Germany); vertical placement; read from screen.
equipment_and_conditions.friability: Roche friabilator (Pharma Test, Germany); 25 rpm, 4 min; weight loss calculated; spec <1%.
equipment_and_conditions.disintegration: Pharma Test disintegration apparatus; buffer pH 6.8 at 37±2°C; time to complete disintegration recorded.
equipment_and_conditions.wetting_absorption: Modified Doijad method; eosin dye; wetting time to surface coloration; AR = (Wa − Wb)/Wb.
equipment_and_conditions.dissolution_odt: USP II paddle, 50 rpm, 37°C, pH 6.8 phosphate buffer; samples every 3 min; UV at 243 nm (atenolol) and 225 nm (atorvastatin).
equipment_and_conditions.dissolution_metformin: USP II paddle, 50 rpm, pH 6.8; samples every 5 min; UV at 233 nm.

008_F1.stability_moisture_uptake: Stable at 75% RH for 2 weeks (qualitative statement)

008_F4.note: Husk showed lower swelling index than arabinoxylan

008_F5.note: Despite higher AR, disintegration was slower with SSG

swelling_and_water_uptake.floating_or_lag_time: Not applicable; no floating system reported

release_properties.dissolution_conditions_common: USP II, 50 rpm, 37°C, pH 6.8; ODT sampling every 3 min (atenolol at 243 nm; atorvastatin at 225 nm); metformin sampling every 5 min at 233 nm.

008_F2.profile_summary: Not individually quantified; see figure; rapid IR profile

008_F3.profile_summary: Not individually quantified; see figure; rapid IR profile

008_F2.profile_summary: Not individually quantified; see figure; rapid IR profile

008_F3.profile_summary: Not individually quantified; see figure; rapid IR profile

release_properties.factors_influencing_release: Medium pH 6.8 and rapid disintegration/swelling behavior; no enzyme effects studied.
release_properties.kinetic_models_fitted: Zero/first-order, Higuchi, Korsmeyer–Peppas not reported/fitted in the paper.

experimental_data_summary.in_vitro_only: All data are in vitro; no in vivo studies reported.
experimental_data_summary.statistics: Results presented as means with standard deviations where shown; no inferential statistics reported.
key_findings: FTIR confirmed compatibility of arabinoxylan with atenolol, atorvastatin, and metformin (no shifts/additional peaks).
Arabinoxylan ODT 008_F1 showed minimum disintegration time of 4 s and low wetting time (17 s) with high swelling ratio (1.946).
Husk (008_F4) and SSG (008_F5) ODTs disintegrated slower (30 s and 35 s, respectively) despite acceptable mechanical properties.
ODT dissolution: 008_F1 and 008_F4 achieved ≈98–100% release by 18 min; 008_F5 required 24 min (atenolol) and 21 min (atorvastatin).
Metformin tablets: 008_M3 (AX as binder and superdisintegrant) disintegrated fastest (2.5 min) and released ≈98% by 18 min.
experimental_data_summary.zero_and_first_order_release: Not analyzed/reported; only empirical release profiles provided

compression.device: 10‑station rotary tablet press; 8 mm round flat punches (ODTs).

granulation_drying.metformin: Wet granulation with purified water; oven drying at 40°C for 1 h before resizing.

test_conditions.dissolution: USP II, 50 rpm, 37°C, pH 6.8; sampling intervals per product type.

notes_and_limitations.f6_data_gap: 008_F6 composition listed but no pre/post-compression or dissolution data reported in tables.
notes_and_limitations.drug_content_assay: Drug content (%) per tablet not reported.
notes_and_limitations.density_of_formulation: No tablet solid density; only powder bulk/tapped densities reported.
notes_and_limitations.consolidation_index: Reported as Carr’s index in precompression tables.
notes_and_limitations.kinetic_modeling: No zero/first-order/Higuchi/Peppas analysis in the paper.

abstract: Study aim, formulation sets (009_F1–009_F4 polymer ratios; 009_F5–009_F9 SLS levels), dissolution overview, release models, and key conclusion (009_F6 best) [attached_file:1]
introduction: Clinical rationale for sustained release carvedilol, solubility context, and objective of using HPMC matrices with SLS [attached_file:1]
materials_and_methods: Materials origin (vendors), tablet preparation (direct compression), compression device/force/time/punch, blend and tablet tests, UV-Vis calibration (241 nm, absorbance table), dissolution setup (USP II, 0.1 N HCl pH 1.2, 37±0.05°C, 100 rpm, 24 h), release models and equations, MDT equation [attached_file:1]
results_and_discussion: Pre-compression (reported as ranges), tablet properties (reported as ranges), 009_F1–009_F4 and 009_F5–009_F9 dissolution tables and figures, kinetic parameters (009_F5–009_F9), MDT and fractional dissolution times, mechanism interpretation (diffusion + erosion) and selection of 009_F6 [attached_file:1]
conclusion: Summary of successful once-daily sustained-release matrix development and best formulation note [attached_file:1]

materials_origin.api: Carvedilol phosphate (gift sample), Beximco Pharmaceuticals Limited, Bangladesh [attached_file:1]
excipients: Methocel K4M CR, Methocel K15M CR, Avicel PH101 (Dow Chemical’s Asia Pvt., India) [attached_file:1]
Sodium lauryl sulfate (Sigma-Aldrich Chemie GmbH, Germany) [attached_file:1]
materials_origin.solvents_and_grade: Reagent-grade chemicals; distilled water used throughout [attached_file:1]

experimental_design_for_optimization.overview: Two-stage optimization: 009_F1–009_F4 varied HPMC K4M/K15M ratio to sustain 24 h; 009_F5–009_F9 fixed polymer ratio and varied SLS (1.0–2.0%) to enhance dissolution of poorly soluble carvedilol [attached_file:1]

sets.name: 009_F1–009_F4
sets.factor: HPMC K15M level while K4M fixed (matrix polymer ratio) [attached_file:1]
sets.objective: Optimize polymer composition to avoid burst and sustain release up to 24 h [attached_file:1]

sets.factor: SLS percentage with fixed polymers (K4M 45 mg, K15M 33 mg) [attached_file:1]
sets.objective: Increase dissolution rate due to poor aqueous solubility while matching theoretical once-daily profile [attached_file:1]

ingredients_identification_uv_vis.instrument_and_wavelength: Double-beam UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) at 241 nm [attached_file:1]
equations_used_in_methods: Korsmeyer–Peppas: \( \frac{M_t}{M_\alpha} = k t^{n} \) with mechanism by n [attached_file:1]

Hardness and thickness via Dr. Schleuniger Pharmatron Tablet Tester (Model 6D) [attached_file:1]

dissolution_conditions.assay: UV-Vis at 241 nm using calibration curve [attached_file:1]

mechanism_interpretation.F1_to_F4: Not optimized for target profile; mechanism not reported [attached_file:1]

contains: Study aim, overview of three formulations (F-1: 40% guar gum, F-2: 50% guar gum, F-3: control without guar), 50 mg curcumin per tablet, wet granulation method, evaluation metrics (hardness, friability, content uniformity), in vitro mouth-to-colon release design, and key result that F-1 released 91.1% at 24 h with rat caecal contents vs 82.1% for F-2
abstract.notes: Summarizes formulation strategy and comparative release outcomes in simulated GI conditions with/without rat caecal contents

contains: Rationale for colon-targeted delivery, advantages of colon as a site, curcumin background and therapeutic relevance, and justification for using degradable polysaccharides such as guar gum for colon targeting
introduction.notes: Frames need for matrix systems that protect in stomach/small intestine and release in colon

contains: Chemicals and sources (guar gum and curcumin), characterization of guar gum (particle traits, angle of repose, densities, pH, ash, LOD, solubility), and formulation components/percentages (Table 2)
materials.notes: Vendor information and excipient profiles; guar gum quality parameters in Table 1

contains: Wet granulation process steps, sieving sizes, drying conditions, tableting equipment, evaluation tests (weight variation, hardness, friability, content uniformity), dissolution media sequence, USP Apparatus 1 settings, UV analysis at 421 nm, and rat caecal content preparation for microbiota-susceptibility testing
methods.notes: Full preparation and test conditions to replicate in vitro mouth-to-colon release and microbiota-mediated degradation

contains: Physical evaluation (weight, hardness, friability, content uniformity in Table 3), dissolution profiles without rat caecal contents (Table 4), dissolution profiles with rat caecal contents (Table 5), and interpretation that guar gum protects in upper GI and enhances colonic release via enzymatic degradation
results_and_discussion.notes: Comparative performance across F-1/F-2/F-3 and effect of rat caecal enzymes on release

contains: Guar gum matrices protect curcumin in stomach/small intestine and enable colonic release, potentially reducing dose while increasing bioavailability for colon cancer therapy
conclusion.notes: Positions guar gum as a promising carrier for colon-specific curcumin delivery

actives_and_excipients: Curcumin and guar gum purchased from National Chemical, Vadodara, Gujarat, India

guar_gum_characterization.particle_characters: White to off-white powder, nearly neutral flavor, odor, color
guar_gum_characterization.solubility: Soluble in hot and cold water

experimental_design_for_optimization.design_type: Comparative formulations with varying guar gum levels (no formal DOE or optimization model reported)
formulations_compared: F-1: 40% guar gum matrix tablets with 10% curcumin
F-2: 50% guar gum matrix tablets with 10% curcumin
F-3: Control without guar gum (85% lactose) with 10% curcumin
experimental_design_for_optimization.primary_endpoint: Cumulative curcumin release over time across GI-mimicking media with and without rat caecal contents

how_prepared: Curcumin, guar gum (<250 µm), and lactose (<250 µm) were sieved; drug was sieved <150 µm; powders blended and wet granulated with 8% starch paste; wet mass passed through sieve #18, dried at 50°C for 2 h, sized through #22, lubricated with talc:Mg stearate (2:1), and compressed on eight-station tabletting machine

analytical_details.absorbance_notes: Quantified by UV method at 421 nm using standard curves in each dissolution medium; pre-filtering not applied prior to sampling

results_summary: F-1 and F-2 minimized release in upper GI media and showed enhanced release with rat caecal contents, indicating enzymatic degradation of guar gum in colonic conditions

composition_percent_w_w.Starch paste (8%): q.s. for granulation (binder solution concentration; not part of % w/w totals)

F-1.notes: Percentages sum to 100% excluding binder solution; per abstract each tablet contains ~50 mg curcumin

composition_percent_w_w.Starch paste (8%): q.s. for granulation (binder solution concentration; not part of % w/w totals)

F-2.notes: Higher guar gum content reduces release in upper GI and modestly reduces total 24 h release compared to F-1

composition_percent_w_w.Starch paste (8%): q.s. for granulation (binder solution concentration; not part of % w/w totals)

F-3.notes: Control tablets without guar gum show rapid release in upper GI media (63.2% by 5 h)

F-1.hardness: Not applicable pre-compression; see tablet properties
F-1.friability: Not applicable pre-compression; see tablet properties

controlled_factors: Curcumin fixed at 10% w/w per tablet (~50 mg)
SLS fixed at 2% w/w as solubilizer
Magnesium stearate 1% w/w and talc 2% w/w as lubricant/glidant
Lactose q.s. to balance formulation (35–85% depending on batch)
dissolution_media_sequence: 0.1N HCl (900 mL) for 2 h at 37°C, 100 rpm (USP Apparatus 1)
pH 7.4 phosphate buffer (900 mL) for 3 h at 37°C, 100 rpm
pH 6.8 phosphate buffer (900 mL) up to 24 h at 37°C, 100 rpm

data.ftir_measurements: Not reported; no FTIR analysis described in this study

measurement_methods: Evaluation per standard tablet tests: weight variation, hardness, friability, and content uniformity

granulation: Wet granulation with 8% w/w starch paste as binder; powders blended then granulated
sieving_and_sizing: Curcumin <150 µm; guar gum and lactose <250 µm before blending; wet mass passed through sieve #18; dried granules re-sized through sieve #22
lubrication: Talc–magnesium stearate mixture (2:1) used as lubricant prior to compression
compression: Eight-station tabletting machine; compression pressure not reported; no coating applied
dissolution_testing: USP Apparatus 1 (basket), 100 rpm, 37°C; media sequence: 0.1N HCl 2 h, pH 7.4 buffer 3 h, pH 6.8 buffer to 24 h; 900 mL each stage
analysis: UV spectrophotometry at 421 nm; samples 5 mL withdrawn at intervals, replaced with fresh buffer; analyzed using standard curves per medium
rat_caecal_preparation: Male albino rats pre-dosed with 1% guar gum dispersion (4 mL/day) for 7 days; caecal contents harvested, 4% w/v in pH 6.8 buffer, CO2 bubbled; used to simulate colonic enzymatic environment

influencing_factors: Minimal release in acidic (pH ~1.2) and upper GI buffer stages; matrix integrity attributed to guar gum gel formation
Higher guar gum content further retards release in upper GI media
Absence of guar gum leads to rapid release in upper GI conditions
Enhanced release due to enzymatic degradation of guar gum by caecal microflora
Enzymatic susceptibility of guar gum increases release in simulated colonic environment

release_kinetics.mechanism_interpretation: Qualitative: diffusion/erosion via guar gum degradation in colonic conditions; no model fitting reported
Qualitative: higher polymer fraction increases matrix integrity and slows release; no model fitting reported
Rapid dissolution due to absence of matrix-forming polymer; no model fitting reported

in_vitro: Dissolution profiles in staged GI media (0.1N HCl → pH 7.4 → pH 6.8) without rat caecal contents for F-1/F-2/F-3 (Table 4)
Dissolution profiles in pH 6.8 buffer with 4% w/v rat caecal contents for F-1/F-2 (Table 5)
experimental_data.statistics: Not reported (no SD/SE or model fits shown for dissolution kinetics)
performance_metrics: 24 h cumulative release without caecal contents: F-1 68.7%, F-2 55.7%
24 h cumulative release with caecal contents: F-1 91.1%, F-2 82.1%

notes: No FTIR characterization, kinetic modeling, disintegration time, stability studies, thickness/diameter, formulation density, SD for tests, or compression force were reported in this article

article_title: Combining Liquisolid and Co‑grinding Techniques to Enhance the Dissolution Rate of Celecoxib

paper_metadata.title: Combining Liquisolid and Co‑grinding Techniques to Enhance the Dissolution Rate of Celecoxib

sections_likely_locations.abstract: First page under title and author list
sections_likely_locations.introduction: Starts after abstract; context on dissolution enhancement and celecoxib BCS II characteristics
sections_likely_locations.materials: Section titled Materials listing API and excipient sources and grades
sections_likely_locations.methods_preparation: Preparation of celecoxib formulations: combination (liquisolid + ball mill), conventional liquisolid, direct compression
sections_likely_locations.methods_tests: Dissolution testing (USP II, media, sampling, UV quantification), friability, PXRD, DSC, aging study
sections_likely_locations.results_discussion: Dissolution comparisons in SGF vs SIF and across LSB/LS/DC, PXRD, DSC, aging effects with figures and discussion
sections_likely_locations.conclusion: Final section summarizing enhanced dissolution and stability outcomes

carriers_excipients: Microcrystalline cellulose Avicel PH101 (Mingtai Chemical, Taiwan)
media_reagents: Sodium chloride, KH2PO4, NaOH, HCl 37% (Merck, Germany) for SGF and SIF preparation

formulation_families: LSB (Liquisolid + Ball milling; co‑ground) with API in liquid medication at 30%, 40%, 50%: 012_LSB1, 012_LSB2, 012_LSB3
DC (Direct compression/physical mixture) at nominal 30%, 40%, 50% API load series: 012_DC1, 012_DC2, 012_DC3

compression_force: 150 kg/cm^2 (LSB and LS used to reach 5–7 kg/cm^2 hardness)
aging_condition: 6 months at 25 °C, protected from moisture and light (tested on 40% LSB)

responses: Cumulative % drug release vs time (up to 120 min)
Solid‑state state (PXRD peaks and intensities; DSC melting endotherm)

preparation_summary.LSB: Celecoxib dispersed in PEG 200; milled in planetary ball mill (PM 100 Retsch, 50‑mL zirconia chamber, stainless steel balls 5–10 cm) for 3 h; PVP added and mixed 5 min; Avicel added per load factor at R=20 (carrier:coating); silica (1:20 to Avicel) sieved 60‑mesh added; sodium starch glycolate 3.5% w/w added and mixed 15 min; lubricated with magnesium stearate; tablets with 100 mg celecoxib compressed to 5–7 kg/cm^2 using 50/100/150 kg/cm^2 on Riken P‑16B
preparation_summary.LS: Same as LSB but without ball milling; celecoxib mixed with PEG 200 by mortar/pestle to uniform dispersion; same excipient ratios and compression to hardness 5–7 kg/cm^2
preparation_summary.DC: Same as liquisolid excipient base but without non‑volatile co‑solvent; compressed with 12 mm flat‑faced punch at 50 kg/cm^2

quantification.dissolution_sampling: USP II, 900 mL media, 50 rpm, 37 °C; sampling at 5, 10, 15, 30, 45, 60, 90, 120 min; replace 5 mL; 0.45 µm filter; UV–vis at 249 nm; calibration based on standard curve
quantification.correction_equation: Sampling correction: C_tn = C_on + (v/V) Σ_{i=1}^{n−1} C_oi where v=sample volume, V=total medium volume
quantification.profile_similarity: Similarity factor f2 = 50 log{[1 + (1/n) Σ_t (R_t − T_t)^2]^{−0.5} × 100}; f2 < 50 indicates significant difference
quantification.absorbance_wavelength: UV–vis quantification at 249 nm for celecoxib

pre_compression_parameters_per_batch.notes: Mass distribution, hardness SD, friability SD, and size SD per batch were not fully tabulated; friability raw W1/W2 listed in Table 4; hardness target 5–7 kg/cm^2 for LSB/LS; no batch‑wise SDs reported for hardness or size; DC compressed at fixed 50 kg/cm^2 with 12 mm punch

fourier_transform_infrared_spectral_meas.note: FTIR not performed/reported; solid‑state analysis used PXRD and DSC; no FTIR section or spectra mentioned

process.compression_device: Manual tableting machine Riken P‑16B (Tokyo, Japan) for LS/LSB; 12 mm flat‑faced punch for DC
process.compression_force: LS/LSB: 50, 100, 150 kg/cm^2 to achieve hardness 5–7 kg/cm^2; DC: 50 kg/cm^2
process.target_hardness: 5–7 kg/cm^2; aging showed 6–7 kg/cm^2 after 6 months for 40% LSB

per_batch_QC.hardness: Reported as target range; specific per‑batch mean±SD not tabulated; aged 40% LSB unchanged at 6–7 kg/cm^2
per_batch_QC.weight_variation: Not explicitly tabulated per batch; tablet weights per formulation provided in Tables 1–3; no SDs reported
per_batch_QC.thickness_diameter: Diameter 12 mm for DC; thickness not reported; LS/LSB punch dimensions not specified beyond compression device; no SDs reported
per_batch_QC.drug_content: Not reported; quantification focused on dissolution UV assay

liquisolid_preparation.R_ratio: Carrier to coating R=20 (Avicel:silica 20:1 by weight)
liquisolid_preparation.mixing_times: PVP to liquid medication 5 min; final blend with SSG 15 min

co_grinding.equipment: Planetary ball mill PM 100 Retsch with 50‑mL zirconia chamber; stainless steel balls 5–10 cm

compression.device: Riken P‑16B manual press; DC also used 12 mm flat‑faced punch
compression.pressure: 50/100/150 kg/cm^2 for LS/LSB; 50 kg/cm^2 for DC

process_parameters.coating: No tablet film coating applied; ‘coating’ refers to silica powder coating in liquisolid powder

release_properties_per_batch.test_conditions: USP II, 900 mL SGF pH 1 and SIF pH 7.2, 50 rpm, 37 °C; UV at 249 nm; non‑sink conditions acknowledged

mechanism_models.reported_models: Profiles compared with f2; narrative references Noyes–Whitney and diffusion boundary layer theory; no formal zero/first order, Higuchi, Hixson‑Crowell, or Korsmeyer–Peppas model fitting parameters reported per batch

profiles_and_findings.012_LSB1: In SIF and SGF, cumulative release after 120 min approximately 28.53% (SIF) and 18.1% (SGF); no significant difference between SIF and SGF (pH effect minimal due to celecoxib pKa 11.1)
profiles_and_findings.LSB_series_vs_LS_vs_DC: For 30%, 40%, 50% series in SIF, LSB showed fastest release > LS > DC; at 60 min LSB approx 7.5× DC; increased API% in liquid medication reduced release rate (30% > 40% > 50%)
profiles_and_findings.aging_effect: 40% LSB aged 6 months showed f2 > 50 vs fresh in both SIF and SGF indicating similar profiles; no leaks or cracks observed

factors_influencing_release: Hydrophilic liquid vehicle improves wettability and specific surface area, enhancing dissolution
Co‑grinding reduces particle size and increases specific surface area; potential local co‑solvency increases Cs in boundary layer per Noyes–Whitney
Higher API fraction in liquid medication reduces degree of molecular dispersion, lowering release rate
Bulk pH between 1 and 7.2 shows limited effect due to celecoxib pKa 11.1 under non‑sink conditions

hardness.DC: Not specified beyond compression at 50 kg/cm^2; hardness values not tabulated

friability_percent.per_batch: See Table 4 values; all below 1% acceptance

dissolution_profile.summary: Figures 2–4 show LSB > LS > DC across 30/40/50% in SIF; 012_LSB1 detailed in Fig. 1 for SGF vs SIF

stability.aging: No visual leakage or structural change; hardness and dissolution unchanged (f2 > 50) over 6 months at 25 °C for 40% LSB

tablet_properties_per_batch.weight_variation_percent: Not reported; only batch total tablet weight given per formulation

in_vitro.dissolution: Time points 5–120 min; UV at 249 nm; correction applied; LSB showed significantly faster dissolution vs DC and LS; non‑sink acknowledged
in_vitro.friability: Per‑batch W1, W2, friability% in Table 4; all within <1%
in_vitro.PXRD: Celecoxib sharp peaks at 2θ ≈ 5°, 10.5°, 16°, 21.5°; LS and DC similar patterns with reduced peak intensity suggesting lower crystallinity; no new peaks indicating no interaction with excipients
in_vitro.DSC: Pure celecoxib melting endotherm at 161.26 °C; small peak in physical mixture from low drug fraction; no melting peak in co‑ground and classic liquisolid indicating reduced crystallinity/amorphousness
in_vitro.aging: Hardness stable (6–7 kg/cm^2) and dissolution f2 > 50 after 6 months for 40% LSB

experimental_data.in_vivo: No in vivo studies reported in this article
experimental_data.release_kinetics_fitting: No batch‑wise zero/first order, Higuchi, Hixson‑Crowell, or Korsmeyer–Peppas parameters reported; only mechanistic discussion and f2 comparison provided

equations.mechanistic_reference: Noyes–Whitney dissolution context used qualitatively; no numerical fitting presented

unreported_items: Pre‑compression flow properties, mass distribution SD, hardness SD per batch, thickness, diameter (for LSB/LS), density, assay/drug content, consolidation index, disintegration time, tablet swelling metrics, and formal kinetic model fits are not reported

experimental_design_for_optimization.aim: Prepare extended-release matrix pellets by extrusion-spheronization using different polymers, compress into tablets, optimize for release and uniformity
methods: Dry blend mixed in Rapid mixer granulator Allen Bradley 5L, USA
Extrusion (Multi gran MG 55, Fugi Paudal Co Ltd Japan) with 0.8 mm screen
Spheronization (QJ 230T-1, Fugi Paudal Co Ltd Japan, crosshatch plate 1mm, 850 rpm, 4-15 min)
Fluid bed drying (Retsch, Germany, inlet air 50°C, 20-30 min)
Pellet fraction: ASTM 20 mesh (841 µm), fines below 40 mesh (420 µm) discarded
Calcium ion complexation for alginate batches: Wet pellets poured into CaCl₂ solutions (10%, 20%, saturated), stirred 10 min, filtered

ingredients_identification.polymers: Aquacoat ECD, Eudragit L 30D 55, Eudragit NM 30D, Sodium Alginate; different batches use different types and concentrations
ingredients_identification.preparation_summary: Tablet batches 013_TAB1-013_TAB5 use calcium chloride-treated pellets, microcrystalline cellulose, polyethylene glycol, crospovidone, magnesium stearate

data.Fourier_Transform_Infrared_Measurements_: Not explicitly reported; SEM used for surface characterization instead.

tablet_fabrications.device: 12-station automated single rotary compression machine (Smart Press, SRC 10i, Pacific Industries, India), 19.5 x 9.5 mm capsule shaped biconvex tooling, speed 10 rpm

process_parameters.drying: Fluid bed dryer inlet air 50°C, 20-30 min

pellets.release_kinetics: Not specified as zero/first order for all, but extended release evident. Saturated CaCl₂ gives slowest release.

tablets.compression force: No significant effect on release profile, tablets rapidly disintegrate into pellets

tablet_properties.dissolution_profile: Similar for both pellets and tablets (see figures)
tablet_properties.stability: No significant change after 3 month (99.2% drug retained)
tablet_properties.weight_variation_percent: Not explicitly stated, but Acceptance Value (AV) used for content uniformity (013_TAB1: 18.7, 013_TAB2: 12.2, 013_TAB3: 14)

experimental_data.release_profiles: See figures: Zero/first order release not separately detailed

experimental_data.in_vitro_release_profiles: Provided for all batches, see Figures 1A, 1B, 1C, 1D, 2, 4, 7, 8, 9, 10
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: SD used for AV calculation, values for each batch not detailed

article_sections.abstract: Summary of aims, design, buoyancy, dissolution, and in vivo findings.
article_sections.introduction: Rationale for MT-1207 GRDDS and polymer choices.
materials_and_methods: Blend flowability (bulk/tapped density, Carr index, Hausner ratio)
In vivo Beagle PK and bioanalytical method
article_sections.results_and_discussion: Flowability, factorial outcomes, optimization, mechanical properties, buoyancy vs pH, in vitro release, kinetics, PK.
article_sections.conclusion: Viable once-daily gastroretentive bilayer tablets with immediate and prolonged buoyancy.

experimental_design_for_optimization.design: Face-centered central composite design for gastroretentive layer (27 runs).

modeling.FLT_model_equation: Provided as Eq. 7 with quadratic terms; R^2 = 0.829; adjusted R^2 = 0.738
modeling.TFT_model_equation: Provided as Eq. 8 (and reduced Eq. 9 without non-significant linear X3); R^2 ≈ 0.923; adjusted R^2 ≈ 0.883–0.887

key_effects_summary: FLT increased with compression force; interaction PEO×force negative at 7.5 kN; choose ~7–7.5 kN to balance FLT, hardness, wet strength.
TFT increased with PEO (linear and quadratic), non-linear effect of compression force; interaction PEO×force negative at higher PEO.
Kollidon SR level (fixed at ~34% w/w in gastro layer) boosted wet strength more than buoyancy.

analytical_method_HPLC_UV.mobile_phase: 0.1 M sodium acetate buffer pH 4.0 : methanol = 40:60 v/v
analytical_method_HPLC_UV.validation: ICH/FDA for linearity, LOD/LOQ; specificity, accuracy, precision aligned with dissolution validation

equations_used: General polynomial (Eq. 1) linking Y to X1, X2, X3 and interactions/quadratics for factorial design.
First-order release: Log(Q1) = Log(Q0) − k1 t / 2.303 (Eq. 2).

pre_compression_parameters.blend_flowability: Carr index and Hausner ratio indicated passable to good flowability for both gastro and drug-layer blends (see Supplementary Tables S1–S2).
pre_compression_parameters.per_batch_values: Not numerically reported in main text; refer to Supplementary data.
pre_compression_parameters.mass_distribution_weight_variation: All bilayer batches had acceptable mean weight and USP <905> acceptance value < 15 (see Supplementary Table S5).
pre_compression_parameters.hardness_friability: Friability < 1.0% for all bilayer tablets; hardness/tensile strength sufficient for mechanical integrity (see Supplementary Table S4).
pre_compression_parameters.dimensions: Optimised and A1 swollen dimensions after buoyancy: long axis ≈ 25.8–26.1 mm, short axis ≈ 13.5–13.7 mm, thickness ≈ 12.9–13.2 mm (n=3).

fabrication.gastro_layer: Direct blending: PEO, Kollidon SR, SDL, HPMC K100M, colloidal silica mixed 15 min; add Mg stearate and mix 5 min.
fabrication.drug_layer: Wet granulation per prior A1 procedure; composition varies by batch.
fabrication.compression: Piccola rotary press (Riva), two-step: pre-compress gastro layer, add drug layer and compress; forces: 5, 7, 7.5, or 10 kN; tooling: 13 mm flat round and 17×9 mm concave oblong, depending on tablet weight.

quality_tests.hardness: USP <1217> on C50 tester; tensile strength calculated.
quality_tests.density_porosity: Helium pycnometry and mercury intrusion porosimetry used on optimized tablets.

tablet_fabrication_and_testing.drug_content_assay: Quantified via validated HPLC-UV method in dissolution samples; tablet drug content not separately reported.
tablet_fabrication_and_testing.consolidation_index: Not reported; compressibility summarized by Carr index/Hausner ratio in Supplementary.

compression_process.pre_compression: Applied to gastro layer before adding drug layer
compression_process.notes: Lower force favored FLT but higher force improved hardness and wet strength; ~7–7.5 kN selected.

testing_equipment_conditions.apparatus: USP II, 75 rpm (also 200 rpm), 500 mL 0.1 M acetate pH 4.0, 37±0.5 °C

testing_equipment_conditions.media: FaSSGF pH 1.6 for screening; HCl pH 1.0, pH 3.0, pH 6.0 for pH effect, 75 rpm, 37±0.5 °C

testing_equipment_conditions.instrument: Philips XL30; 10 kV; samples dried at 40 °C, gold-sputtered

dissolution_method.apparatus: USP II, 75 rpm; 500 mL 0.1 M acetate buffer pH 4.0; 37±0.5 °C; sampling at 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 16, 24 h; centrifuge then HPLC-UV
dissolution_method.sinkers: Stainless-steel sinkers used to negate buoyancy effect
dissolution_method.high_shear_assessment: 200 rpm study for FHE6005 and A1; profiles similar to 75 rpm (f2 > 50)

per_batch_summary_drug_layer.note: In vitro release similar to bilayer A1 (f1=13, f2=59)
No distinct numerical profile reported in main text
No distinct numerical profile reported in main text
No distinct numerical profile reported in main text
No distinct numerical profile reported in main text
Only one of two batches (with FHE6005) achieving ≥80% mean release by 24 h
No distinct numerical profile reported in main text
All three tablets ≥80% released by 24 h; selected as optimized ethylcellulose-containing drug layer
No distinct numerical profile reported in main text
No distinct numerical profile reported in main text

profile_comparisons: Single-layer A1 vs bilayer A1: f1=40, f2=36; bilayer slows release vs single-layer.
Bilayer A1 vs bilayer FHE7035: f1=13, f2=59; similar profiles.
FHE6005 A1 at 200 rpm vs 75 rpm: f2 = 89 and 76 respectively (similar).
factors_influencing_release: Ethylcellulose level had minor effect in vitro; gastro layer presence dominated by reducing exposed surface area of drug layer.
At pH 1.0, 3.0, 6.0 buoyancy duration varied, potentially via acid-induced PEO degradation affecting floatation and tablet integrity; release testing itself used pH 4.0 acetate.

mechanical_properties_summary: Hardness/tensile strength sufficient for integrity across batches; no clear trend with ethylcellulose level (values in Supplementary).
Weight variation within USP limits; acceptance value < 15 for all.

density_porosity_optimized.interpretation: Bulk density < 1.004 g/cm3 consistent with near-immediate floatation.

experimental_data_additional.SEM_summary: Dry gastro layer: porous surface with irregular PEO and spherical Kollidon SR particles; at 12 h fibers indicative of swollen/dried PEO; fibers decrease at 22 h in pH 1.0; persistent to 31–43.5 h in pH 6.0.
experimental_data_additional.viscosity_summary: No inter-media differences up to 12 h; significant divergence by 22 h consistent with PEO degradation at low pH; stable between 31 and 43.5 h at pH 6.0.

in_vivo_PKuctions.design: 4 male Beagle dogs (10 ± 2 kg), fed state; dosing: 30 mg bilayer FHE6005 o.d., 30 mg bilayer A1 o.d., and three 10 mg IR tablets; serial sampling up to 32 h; 1-week washout; UPLC-MS/MS quantitation.
in_vivo_PKuctions.outcome_summary: 24 h sustained plasma levels above ~1 ng/mL threshold; both bilayer formulations demonstrated 24 h release in vivo; Beagle half-life shorter than human, supporting feasibility for once-daily in humans.

notes_missing_or_not_applicable: Disintegration time and swelling index (%): not reported.
Per-batch hardness, friability, and weight variation values: referenced to Supplementary Tables S4–S5, not numerically listed in main text.
Pre-compression Carr index/Hausner ratio per blend: referenced to Supplementary Tables S1–S2, not numerically listed in main text.
Percent of pellets: not applicable to this tablet system.

data.material: MT-1207 HCl reference standard and 5:1 micronized MT-1207:colloidal silicon dioxide

Polyethylene oxide 8,000,000 (PEO 8M), Kollidon SR, spray-dried lactose, PVP K30, HPMC K15M, HPMC K100M, colloidal silicon dioxide, magnesium stearate, sodium phosphate monobasic dihydrate, sodium acetate anhydrous
Alpha-D-lactose monohydrate, ethylcellulose 10 cps, iron(III) oxide, HCl 37%, acetic acid, NaOH pellets, orthophosphoric acid, methanol (HPLC), propan-2-ol (HPLC)

article_title: Core in Cup Ethylmorphine Hydrochloride Tablet for Dual Fast and Sustained Pain Relief: Formulation, Characterization, and Pharmacokinetic Study

source.article_title: Core in Cup Ethylmorphine Hydrochloride Tablet for Dual Fast and Sustained Pain Relief: Formulation, Characterization, and Pharmacokinetic Study

materials_origin.Ethylmorphine_hydrochloride: gift sample from Macfarlan Smith Ltd., Edinburgh (UK).
materials_origin.Methyl_cellulose: Dow Chemical Company, Midland, MI, USA.

experimental_design_for_optimization.selection_criteria: From ODT factorial results, two formulas (015_F9 & 015_F10) selected as cup (fast IR). From SR factorial 015_S6 selected as sustained core based on in vitro release profile. These choices are stated in the article.

ODT_lyophilization.binder_hydration: Gelatin or methyl cellulose dispersed in 0.2 mL distilled water for 30 min then 2 mL added; heated to 40°C until clear.
ODT_lyophilization.add_excipients: Mannitol or maltodextrin and glycine (20 mg) added and dissolved; EtM added; aliquots of 2 mL placed into blister pockets (2 cm diameter), frozen at -20°C for 24 h.
ODT_lyophilization.lyophilizer: Christ Alpha 1-2LD plus (Munich, Germany). Condenser temp -55°C, vacuum 7.6 Pa. Primary drying 12 h at -30°C and 0.37 mbar; secondary drying 12 h at 20°C and 0.01 mbar.

SR_direct_compression.equipment: Single punch tablet machine (Shakti Pharmatech, Model SSTP-12, India) with flat-faced 8-mm punch/die.
SR_direct_compression.procedure: Powder components fed manually into die and compressed with constant compression force; each tablet final weight 150 mg containing 40 mg EtM (for SR formulas as composed).

ingredients_identification_notes.glycine: collapse protectant (20 mg in ODT formulas)

UV_spectrophotometry.use_case: Drug content assay for tablets (dissolved in phosphate buffer pH 6.8).

HPLC_for_in_vivo_samples.mobile_phase: water (pH 5.4 with orthophosphoric acid) : acetonitrile 40:60 v/v
HPLC_for_in_vivo_samples.instrument: Shimadzu LC-10ADvp with SPD-20A UV/VIS detector

formulation_details_per_batch.notes: All batch composition values below are taken directly from Table II of the article. Where table text was structured in two parts (Cup and Core) the aggregated composition of final core-in-cup (015_B1, 015_B2) is provided in the 015_B1/015_B2 entries. For 015_S1–015_S9 the SR tablet ingredient masses are shown as given.

015_S2.note_on_table_parsing: Table text for 015_S2 in the PDF is compacted; the article table shows EtM=45 mg and HPMC=52 mg—other columns are blank in the printed table.

015_S3.note_on_table_parsing: 015_S3 row in the article table: EtM=60 mg and HPMC=37 mg.

015_S4.note_on_table_parsing: 015_S4 row layout indicates MC=30, Mg stearate=3, KCl=10, lactose=67; EtM value not explicitly printed in that row in the compact PDF excerpt—interpretation: some rows were split in two-line format in the table; see Article text for explicit dosing logic (total core dose usually 40 mg EtM).

015_S5.note_on_table_parsing: As above, table formatting in PDF compresses entries; taken as presented in Table II.

015_S6.note_on_selection: 015_S6 is explicitly stated in the article as the selected SR formula (best sustained release — showed 70.48 ± 0.08% release after 24 h).

015_S7.note_on_table_parsing: 015_S7 row indicates EC=30, other excipients as indicated.

pre_compression_parameters_for_SR_batche.source_table_note: Table III in article. Data are mean ± SD, n = 3.

fourier_transform_infrared_spectral_meas.method: KBr pellet method; 3 mg sample + 300 mg KBr compressed at 10 kg/cm to form pellet; scanned 4000–500 cm^-1 using FT-IR Affinity-1 (Shimadzu).

findings.compatibility_conclusion: All peaks of the optimum formulae (015_F9, 015_F10 and 015_S6) exist in their original position without shifting — indicating no incompatibility between EtM and excipients in the tested formulas.
findings.note: FT-IR spectra for 015_S6 (SR) and 015_F9/015_F10 (ODTs) are included in the article's figures; the article reports absence of drug–excipient interactions based on the preserved characteristic peaks.

SR_tablet_core_compression.device: Single punch tablet machine (Shakti Pharmatech, Model SSTP-12, India)
SR_tablet_core_compression.compression_force: constant compression force for all formulae (numerical pressure not provided)
SR_tablet_core_compression.EtM_content_in_SR_tablets_mg: 40 mg (where specified in the SR table rows)

process_conditions_temperature_pressure.temperature_control: room temperature for compression (not numerically specified)

coating.coating_for_core_in_cup: The core is compressed and the cup is formed by lyophilized ODT surrounding the core—no additional coating process described.

tablet_properties_per_batch_S1_to_S9.source_table_note: Table IV in article. Means ± SD are provided there (n not explicitly repeated for each test).

SR_tablet_testing_conditions.two_stage_dissolution: 0–2 h in 900 mL 0.1 N HCl (pH 1.2), then transfer to phosphate buffer pH 6.8 (900 mL) for the remainder (up to 24 h).
SR_tablet_testing_conditions.apparatus: USP dissolution apparatus (not further numerically specified in Methods beyond apparatus type); paddle
SR_tablet_testing_conditions.sampling: 5 mL aliquots withdrawn at predetermined intervals and replaced with fresh medium; samples filtered (0.22 µm) and assayed spectrophotometrically at λmax=284 nm.

S1_to_S9_release_summary.general_findings: Formulations compressed with either HPMC or MC or EC showed extended release for up to 24 h; type and concentration of matrix-forming polymer affected release (higher polymer concentration decreased release).

S6_specific.article_statement: 015_S6 shows more hindering of the drug release (70.48 ± 0.08%) after 24 h and therefore was selected as the sustained core formula.

full_timepoint_release_profiles.explanation: The article provides release curves (figures) but does not tabulate the full numeric time-by-time dissolution data in the text or tables; only the summary 24-h value for 015_S6 is numerically given explicitly.

release_mechanism_discussion.authors_conclusions: Reduction of EtM release by ethyl cellulose (hydrophobic polymer) is consistent with decreased solvent penetration and diffusion-limited release; authors reference the 'penetration theory' and attribute SR behavior mainly to matrix viscosity and hydrophobicity — i.e., diffusion-controlled matrix release.

tablet_properties_for_B1_and_B2.manufacture_notes: Core tablet prepared by direct compression (SR formula 015_S6 for core). The compressed core tablet was placed in lyophilized ODT pocket and additional solution added to sandwich the core followed by freezing and lyophilization — producing core-in-cup tablets 015_B1 and B2.

weight_thickness_hardness_friability_for.explanation: Article gives characterization for ODTs (F series) and SR (S series) separately in tables, but does not tabulate final 015_B1/015_B2 hardness/thickness/friability explicitly. Pharmacokinetic experiments used tablets sized/adjusted for rats (30 mg tablets administered to rats).

group_treatments.Group_III: MST® marketed product (morphine sulfate) as comparator

in_vivo_study_design_summary.bioanalysis: Plasma EtM measured by HPLC (method described in article). Non-compartmental analysis using Kinetica software.

statistical_analysis_for_in_vivo.method: One-way ANOVA followed by post-hoc multiple comparisons using LSD; significance at p < 0.05.
statistical_analysis_for_in_vivo.reported_findings: Cmax and AUC for 015_B1 and 015_B2 were significantly higher than MST® (p < 0.05). No significant difference between 015_B1 and 015_B2 (p > 0.1) for Cmax.

stability_data.explanation: Article concludes formulation was successful and suggests further human in vivo bioavailability studies; no accelerated or long-term stability numeric data presented.

tablet_performance_and_stability.dissolution_profile_stability_notes: Article presents dissolution curves in figures for ODT and SR formulas; numeric time series data for each time point not tabulated.

experimental_data_in_vitro_and_statistic.micromeritic_statistics: Micromeritic values reported as mean ± SD (n=3).
experimental_data_in_vitro_and_statistic.tablet_characterization_statistics: Weight, thickness, breaking force, tensile strength, friability, drug content reported as mean ± SD in Table IV. Disintegration time reported for ODTs (not applicable to SR).

performance_metrics_summary.S6_as_selected_SR: 015_S6 showed most sustained release (70.48 ± 0.08% at 24 h).
performance_metrics_summary.ODT_selection_for_cup: 015_F9 and 015_F10 selected as cup ODTs based on fastest disintegration time and highest immediate release (note: F-series not requested for extraction per your choice A).

release_kinetics_for_all_formulations.authors_interpretation: Release shows polymer-dependent sustained-release behavior consistent with matrix diffusion/penetration theory; EC produced more sustained profiles than MC/HPMC.

explicit_missing_numeric_data: Complete time-by-time dissolution numeric tables for 015_S1–015_S9 (except 015_S6 24-h point) are not provided in text/tables.
Kinetic model constants (zero/first order rate constants, Korsmeyer-Peppas n and k) are not tabulated in the article.
Compression pressure in numeric units (kN or MPa) for direct compression is not provided.
Final 015_B1/015_B2 physical attributes (hardness, friability, thickness, weight variation) are not tabulated explicitly in the article.
additional_notes_and_missing_items.where_figures_show_data: Several findings (e.g., full dissolution curves, FTIR spectra, SEM images) are presented as figures in the article but the raw numeric files or tables are not present in the text; the JSON indicates where numeric values are missing.

references_and_citation.primary_article: This JSON was generated from the uploaded article. See file citation above: :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}
references_and_citation.note: All numeric values and text summaries were transcribed from the article's tables and text (Tables II–V and Figs described).

Materials_origin.silymarin: Prepared from Silybum marianum seeds per Kahol et al.14; powdering, defatting, extraction, vacuum concentration, purification
Materials_origin.matrix_polymers: Carbopol 71G, Noveon AA-1 Polycarbophil USP, Carbopol 974 P NF, Carbopol 971P from Lubrizol Corporation
Materials_origin.complexing_agents: 2-Hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin, Heptakis(2,6-di-O-methyl)-β-cyclodextrin, methylated β-cyclodextrin, random methyl-β-cyclodextrin from CycloLab RD Ltd.
Materials_origin.excipients: Mg-stearate, talc, dibasic Ca-phosphate, KH2PO4, NaCl, HCl, NaOH from Hungaropharma; Ludipress from BASF

Experimental_Design_for_Optimization.each_product: 70 mg silymarin per tablet, 150 mg carbopol (type varying)

Process_and_Equipment.compression_process: Manual bench-top tablet press (Debrecen, Hungary), compressing force 50 N

Methodology.FTIR_information: Physical mixture samples and tablets analyzed to confirm complexation, carbopol matrix integrity
Methodology.physical_mixture_method: Silymarin and β-cyclodextrin derivatives weighed in molar ratio and mixed in mortar
Methodology.absorbance_spectrophotometry: Dissolution samples measured at 287 nm; results expressed as percentage to reference

Experimental_Results_Summary.key_results: Noveon and Carbopol 974PNF with methylated cyclodextrin gave best sustained release and cytocompatibility; more than 85% silymarin released
Experimental_Results_Summary.performance_metrics: All physical properties within European Pharmacopeia standards, SD reported for weight, friability, dissolution, cytotoxicity (MTT assay shows >70% cell viability)
Experimental_Results_Summary.release_kinetics_observation: Sustained release demonstrated, kinetic profiles mapped for zero/first order, rate depends on pH and cyclodextrin type

Dissolution_Studies.curve: Methylated β-CD DS-12 in altered medium (gastric→intestinal)
Dissolution_Studies.medium: First hour gastric, then intestinal for 6 hours
