experimental_design_for_optimization.design_type: 3^2 full factorial design applied separately to floating layer (FL) and matrix layer (ML). [file:1]

X1_FL.name: Polyox 303WSR
X1_FL.effect_summary: Positive effect on floating lag time, total floating time, and adhesion force. [file:1]

X2_FL.name: Alginate beads
X2_FL.effect_summary: Decreases floating lag time, increases total floating time and adhesion force. [file:1]

X1_ML.name: HPMC K100M
X1_ML.effect_summary: Negative effect on Q2h, Q8h, Q18h (slows drug release). [file:1]

X2_ML.name: Fumaric acid
X2_ML.effect_summary: Positive effect on Q2h, Q8h, Q18h (facilitates drug release via acidic microenvironment). [file:1]

checkpoint_validation.max_relative_error_percent: Below 5% for all responses at 5% significance level. [file:1]

modeling.optimization_method: Desirability function approach using Design-Expert software (Version 7). [file:1]

FL_layer_fixed_mg_per_tablet.MCC: q.s. to 350 mg FL layer total [file:1]

ML_layer_fixed_mg_per_tablet.MCC: q.s. to 700 mg ML layer total [file:1]

common_components.bead_curing_solution: 2% w/v CaCl2 and 10% v/v glacial acetic acid in 200 ml ionizing solution. [file:1]

preparation_notes.FL_blend: Matrix granules (fixed + variable Polyox + lubricants) blended with alginate beads for 10 min [file:1]
preparation_notes.ML_blend: Matrix granules (QF + HPMC + fumaric acid + fixed excipients) [file:1]
preparation_notes.compression_sequence: ML blend compressed first, then FL blend on top using 17×9 mm oval D tooling [file:1]

drug_content_assay_tablets.sample_preparation: Twenty tablets powdered; accurate amount transferred to 200 ml volumetric flask, diluted with methanol, sonicated 15 min, filtered (0.45 µm), appropriately diluted. [file:1]
drug_content_assay_tablets.analysis_method: HPLC method as per Reddy et al. (2011). [file:1]
drug_content_assay_tablets.absorbance_or_peak: HPLC UV detection at 254 nm; chromatographic conditions detailed separately (plasma method). Specific tablet solution absorbance values not reported. [file:1]

plasma_HPLC_method_for_QF.purpose: Quantification of QF in rabbit plasma for pharmacokinetic study. [file:1]

compression.equipment: 10 station rotary tablet punching machine (Riddhi Pharma Machinery Ltd., India). [file:1]
compression.sequence: ML blend compressed first, then FL blend compressed on top to obtain bilayer tablets. [file:1]

granulation_process.FL_granulation: Wet granulation of powders (excluding Plasdone S630, Mg stearate, beads) using Plasdone S630 in isopropyl alcohol; sieving through #16, drying in FBD at 60 °C for 15 min, sizing through #30, lubrication with Mg stearate 10 min. [file:1]
granulation_process.ML_granulation: Similar wet granulation using Plasdone S630 in isopropyl alcohol. [file:1]
granulation_process.bead_preparation: Ionic gelation by dropping casting mixture via 20G needle into CaCl2/acetic acid solution with stirring; curing 15 min, washing, drying 48 h at room temperature, sieving #20 to remove oversized beads. [file:1]

drying_process.FBD_equipment: Fluid bed drier (Cronimach Machinery, Ahmedabad, India). [file:1]

beads_curing.curing_medium: 2% w/v CaCl2 + 10% v/v glacial acetic acid in 200 ml solution, continuous stirring. [file:1]

dissolution_testing_conditions.apparatus: USP type II (paddle), Veego DA-6D, India. [file:1]
dissolution_testing_conditions.medium: 900 ml pH 1.2 HCl buffer; for pH effect study also pH 3.0 medium. [file:1]
dissolution_testing_conditions.sampling: 5 ml aliquots at specified times, replacement with fresh medium. [file:1]

mucoadhesion_test.apparatus: Modified analytical two-pan balance with goat gastric mucosa in pH 1.2 buffer at 37 ± 0.5 °C. [file:1]

FF1_to_FF9.qualitative_profile: All batches showed similar release up to ~10 h, with divergence thereafter due to different polymer/acidifier levels. [file:1]

optimized_batch_OP.pH_independence: Dissolution at pH 1.2 and 3.0 showed similarity factor f2 > 70, indicating minimal pH effect. [file:1]

kinetic_models_fitting.best_fit_model: Weibull (highest R2, lowest SSR and F value). [file:1]

stability_of_optimized_batch_OP.conditions: 40 ± 2 °C / 75% RH ± 5% RH for 3 months in amber glass bottles. [file:1]
stability_of_optimized_batch_OP.physical_observations: No notable change in appearance, colour, texture. [file:1]

pharmacokinetic_study_design.species: Healthy male New Zealand rabbits (2–3 kg). [file:1]
pharmacokinetic_study_design.design: Two-period crossover with 6 rabbits, 3 per group, washout 7 days. [file:1]
pharmacokinetic_study_design.fasting: 24 h fast with free access to water before dosing. [file:1]

dosing.OP_group: Single animal-equivalent dose of optimized batch OP. [file:1]
dosing.MP_group: Two animal-equivalent doses of immediate-release market product 150 mg given 12 h apart. [file:1]

pharmacokinetic_study_design.sampling: Blood from marginal ear vein at specified times; plasma stored at −70 °C. [file:1]

pharmacokinetic_parameters.relative_bioavailability: OP shows ~12% higher AUC0–∞ compared with MP. [file:1]

in_vivo_experimental_data.plasma_profiles: OP shows sustained, flatter concentration–time curve with prolonged levels vs. rapid peak and decline for MP. [file:1]

process_steps: Step 1: PEG-8000 was melted in a stainless steel vessel. DS was gradually added with continuous stirring.
Step 2: At 97-98°C, DS dissolved completely. Intragranular HPMC K100M was gradually added with continuous stirring to obtain a translucent hot homogeneous mixture.
Step 3: The hot homogeneous phase was poured into stainless steel trays and allowed to cool gradually at room temperature (25°C) to form a solid dispersion block.

results.thermal_stability_of_DS: Assay of 99.579% after heating at 110°C for 30 minutes; chromatogram showed no secondary peak, indicating excellent thermal stability.
results.phase_behavior: PEG-8000 and DS alone showed phase separation upon cooling. With all three components (PEG-8000, HPMC K100M, DS), a monophasic homogeneous mixture without phase separation was obtained.

ftir_absorbance_data.remarks: C=O stretch of valproic acid. No significant shift, indicating absence of carbonyl interaction.
C=O stretch of valproate ion. No significant shift, indicating absence of carbonyl interaction.
C-O stretching vibrations in C-O-C bond of PEG. Higher absorption in solid dispersion suggests involvement in hydrogen bonding, possibly with water.

optimized_formulation.hygroscopicity: 1.71% weight gain at 52% RH after 48h (28.15% less hygroscopic than DS, which gained 2.38%)
optimized_formulation.physical_observation: Solid dispersion remained free-flowing granules; DS clumped into a single mass.

korsmeyer_peppas.mechanism: Non-Fickian (anomalous transport), approaching zero-order (n close to 1)

ftir_spectral_measurements.key_findings: No significant shift in carbonyl peaks (1689 cm⁻¹, 1555 cm⁻¹) of DS in the solid dispersion, indicating absence of chemical interaction between drug and polymers. Increased absorption at 1107 cm⁻¹ suggests hydrogen bonding of PEG's C-O bond, possibly with water.

key_findings.DS: Endothermic peak at 100.25°C (evaporation of valproic acid).
key_findings.DS_solid_dispersion: Endothermic peak at 99.95°C, indicating no significant molecular interaction with valproic acid.
key_findings.PEG_in_solid_dispersion: Tm decreased to 57.41°C, suggesting interaction with water molecules.

process_parameters.granulation: Multi Mill fitted with a 1 mm screen.

experimental_design_for_optimization.pellets_preparation: Drug loading was done in a conventional coating pan. Sugar pellets (50-60#) were coated by alternating cycles of spraying binder solution (Sucrose and HPMC 032_E5 in purified water) and drug powder (Metoprolol succinate, Aerosil, Sucrose) to achieve desired loading. Drug-loaded pellets were dried and sifted. They were then coated with polymer solutions (Surelease, Ethyl cellulose, Kollicoat SR 30D) using a Fluidised Bed Coater (Wurster bottom spray) to achieve different coating levels (7.5%, 10%, 12.5%, 15%). Coated pellets were sifted.
experimental_design_for_optimization.tablets_preparation: MUPS tablets were prepared by direct compression. Coated pellets were mixed with excipients (MCC pH102, Aerosil, Magnesium stearate, Lubritab) in a Double cone blender and compressed using an 8-station tablet compression machine (Karnavati Mini Press II).
experimental_design_for_optimization.results: Formulation 032_F10 (pellets with 7.5% Kollicoat SR 30D coating) was found to be the best pellet formulation. Formulation 032_T4 (tablets with 15% Lubritab) was found to be the most promising MUPS formulation with acceptable physicochemical properties and dissolution profile similar to the innovator product.
experimental_design_for_optimization.equation_absorbance: Drug release was analyzed by UV Spectrophotometric method at a wavelength of 223 nm.\nWater content by K.F. titration: % Water Content = (V × F × 100) / W, where V = Volume of K.F. reagent, F = Factor for K.F. reagent, W = Weight of sample in grams.\nSimilarity factor (f2) and dissimilarity factor (f1) were calculated to compare dissolution profiles.

data.composition_of_independent_variables: The independent variables were the type of polymer coating (Surelease, Ethyl cellulose N50, Kollicoat SR 30D) and the percentage of coating (7.5%, 10%, 12.5%, 15%) for pellets. For tablets, the independent variable was the concentration of Lubritab (0, 10, 15, 20 mg/tablet).

ftir_spectral_measurements.method: About 2 mg of sample was mixed with dried KBr, pressed into a pellet, and scanned from 4000 to 400 cm-1 in a spectrophotometer.
ftir_spectral_measurements.findings: FT-IR spectra showed typical peaks for Metoprolol succinate, confirming its purity. No significant interactions or instability were observed between the drug and excipients (Ethyl cellulose N50, Kollicoat SR30D). Major peaks for functional groups (C-N, C-H Alkane, N-H Bending, OCH3-stretching, C=C) showed no significant change in physical mixtures.

coating.equipment: Fluidised Bed Coater (FBC) with bottom spray Wurster

compression.equipment: 8 station tablet compression machine (Karnavati Mini Press II)
compression.conditions: Direct compression method. Blend mixed in Double cone blender for 2-4 minutes.

in_vitro_dissolution.method: USP type II (Paddle) apparatus, 500 mL pH 6.8 phosphate buffer, 50 rpm, 37±0.5°C, samples withdrawn at 1,4,8,12,20 hours, analyzed at 223 nm.

experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Similarity factor (f2) and dissimilarity factor (f1) were used to compare dissolution profiles of test formulations with the marketed product (innovator).

performance_metrics.best_pellet_formulation: 032_F10 (7.5% Kollicoat SR 30D coating) with good flow, compressibility, and dissolution profile similar to innovator.
performance_metrics.best_tablet_formulation: 032_T4 (20 mg Lubritab) with hardness 5.0 Kg/cm2, friability 0.12%, disintegration time 10.20 min, drug content 99.5%, and dissolution profile most similar to innovator (f2=87.4).

032_T4.mechanism: Super case-II transport (anomalous diffusion) by erosion and diffusion

experimental_data.stability_summary: 032_T4 formulation was stable for 3 months at 25°C/60% RH and 40°C/75% RH. No significant changes in description, weight, hardness, thickness, friability, or dissolution profile.

data.release_profile: Dissolution profile not super-imposable with marketed preparation. Showed faster release initially? (from Fig 7)
Dissolution profile not super-imposable with marketed preparation. Showed faster release initially? (from Fig 7)
Similar drug release pattern to marketed preparation, but 032_T4 was more similar.

data.release_mechanism: Super case-II transport (anomalous diffusion) by erosion and diffusion mechanism.
data.release_profile: Most similar to innovator (TOPROL-XL). Extended release over 20 hours.

data.dissolution_profile: At 20 hrs: not specified, but f2 with innovator is 59.3.
At 20 hrs: not specified, but f2 with innovator is 58.9.
At 20 hrs: not specified, but f2 with innovator is 79.2.
At 20 hrs: ~91.5% (from Fig 12), f2=87.4, f1=2.8. Most similar to innovator.
data.stability: Stable at 25°C/60% RH and 40°C/75% RH for 3 months. No significant change in dissolution profile or physicochemical parameters.

materials_origin.api: IMM-033_H014 was produced in the author's laboratory (Institute of Materia Medica, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences & Peking Union Medical College).

excipients.hydroxypropyl_methylcellulose: Purchased from Shin–Etsu Chemical Co., Ltd. (Dalian, China)
excipients.lactose: Purchased from DFE pharma GmbH & Co. KG. (Shanghai, China)
excipients.silicon_dioxide: Purchased from Anhui Sunhere Pharmaceutical Excipients Co., Ltd. (Huainan, China)
excipients.magnesium_stearate: Purchased from Anhui Sunhere Pharmaceutical Excipients Co., Ltd. (Huainan, China)

materials_origin.animals: Beagles (female or male), purchased from Beijing Marshall Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China)

ingredients_identification.preparation: The appropriate amount of the IMM-033_H014 active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) was loaded into the gelatin capsule shell as the IMM-033_H014 immediate-release preparation. For ER tablets, required quantities of drug and ingredients were passed through an 80-mesh screen. IMM-033_H014, HPMC, and lactose were mixed completely using the equivalent addition method. Then, after adding silicon dioxide and magnesium stearate, the sample was mixed for a further 10 min. Tablets were compressed using a flat-faced 8mm punch.
ingredients_identification.results: The optimal formulation (033_F9) was determined. HPMC (90SH-4000SR) was selected as the best matrix material. The formulation with 15% HPMC (033_F9) showed an AUC equivalent to IR, significantly lengthened MRT, and significantly lowered Cmax.
ingredients_identification.equation: Not specified in the provided text.
ingredients_identification.absorbance: Drug release was quantified by ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometry at a wavelength of 230 nm. For content determination, HPLC with a UV detector wavelength of 230 nm was used.

data.composition_of_independent_variables_and: Not explicitly detailed in the provided text.
data.fourier_transform_infrared_spectral_meas: Not specified in the provided text.

compression_process.pressure: Not specified, but target tablet hardness was 100-130 N.

process_parameters.equipment_used: Flat-faced 8mm punch for compression; HPLC (Shimazu LC-20AT, Kyoto, Japan); Dissolution apparatus (USP type II); Friability tester (Xixin Instrument, Tianjin, China); Hardness tester (Tianda Tianfa Instrument, Tianjin, China).

process_conditions.temperature: In vitro release studies were maintained at 37 ± 0.5°C. HPLC column temperature was 35°C. Stability studies were at 40°C.

optimal_formulation_f9.dissolution_profiles: Figure 4 shows dissolution in pH 2.0 HCl, pH 4.5 ABEP, pH 6.8 PBS, water, and pH 2.0 → pH 7.5 PBS. Total amount released within 24h reached more than 90% in all five media. Drug release amount within the initial stage (1h) at pH 2.0 was higher.
optimal_formulation_f9.reproducibility: Three batches of 20,000 tablets showed f2 values >50 compared to batch 1, indicating similar release profiles.
optimal_formulation_f9.stability: After 6 months at 40°C/75% RH, f2 values in five media were >50 compared to 0-month formulation, content >99%.

in_vivo_studies.study_design: Two-treatment, two-period crossover design in 10 healthy beagles. Single dose equivalent to 75mg IMM-033_H014 (3 tablets). Fasted conditions.
in_vivo_studies.plasma_concentration_profiles: Figure 5 shows plasma concentration vs. time for ER and IR.

pharmacokinetic_parameters.statistical_significance: Tmax, Cmax, and MRT showed significant difference (p < 0.05) between IR and ER. AUC showed no significant difference (p=0.87, 0.95).

ivivc.method: Loo-Riegelman method for absorption fraction. Linear regression of in vitro release vs. in vivo absorption fraction.
ivivc.conclusion: Good association between drug release and fraction absorbed, indicating in vitro release can predict in vivo absorption.

experimental_data.zero_and_first_order_of_release_of_drug_: Only provided for the optimized formulation (033_F9) in Table 3. See 'release_kinetics_optimized_formulation'.

experimental_data.performance_metrics: 034_F8 showed >24h floating time in vitro, 44.41% release at 4h, 99.81% at 24h. Radiolabeling efficiency >93%.

data.experimental_design_for_optimization: The study evaluates the effect of different ratios of polymers (HPMC K100 and Ethylcellulose) at 1:1, 1:2, and 1:3 drug-to-polymer ratios, and different ratios of drug-to-β-cyclodextrin complex (1:1, 1:0.75, 1:0.5) on the release profile of Candesartan Cilexetil from matrix tablets.

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Kneading method for inclusion complex; Dry granulation by slugging for tablets.

ingredients_identification.equation_for_drug_content: Content determined spectrophotometrically at λmax 255 nm using UV-visible spectrophotometer.
ingredients_identification.absorbance: Samples analyzed at 255 nm for CC.

data.ftir_spectral_measurements: FTIR spectra were performed for CC pure powder, β-cyclodextrin pure powder, CC/HPMC K100 (1:1) physical mixture, and CC/β-cyclodextrin complex (1:1) physical mixture. Results showed characteristic peaks of CC with no shifts or new bands, indicating good compatibility and no interaction. The inclusion complex showed peaks at corresponding wavenumbers of both pure CC and β-cyclodextrin.

HPMC K100 showed higher swelling and more sustained release compared to ethylcellulose. F15z (HPMC K100 1:3, CD 1:0.5) showed most sustained release.

article_metadata.authors: Naser Tavakoli, Jaleh Varshosaz, Farid Dorkoosh, Sedigheh Motaghi, Lana Tamaddon

experimental_design_for_optimization.preparation_method: Direct compression method. All components were powdered, mixed for 20 minutes, and lubricated with 1% magnesium stearate.
experimental_design_for_optimization.key_results: Tablets with 15% effervescent base, 20-30% HPMC, 30% Na CMC (or 20% PVP or 20% sodium alginate) showed good floating and drug release properties.
equations_used: Korsmeyer-Peppas equation (Mt/M∞ = kt^n) for release mechanism analysis
T50% (time to release 50% of drug content)

absorbance_wavelengths.assay_and_content_uniformity: 259 nm (and 295 nm for assay in 0.1N NaOH)

independent_variables_levels.effervescent_base_total_percent: 9%, 12%, 15% (1:1 ratio of sodium bicarbonate to citric acid)

data.fourier_transform_infrared_spectral_meas: Not mentioned in the provided text.

tablet_fabrication_process.equipment: Rotary tablet press (Killian and Co., Germany)
evaluation_parameters: Drug content (UV spectrophotometry at 259 nm in 0.1N HCl, and at 295 nm in 0.1N NaOH for assay)

process_parameters.compression_device: Rotary tablet press (Killian and Co., Germany)

in_vitro_studies: Drug release in 0.1N HCl (pH 1.2) at 37±0.5°C using USP apparatus II (paddle, 50 rpm)
Floating lag time and duration determined visually
Mucoadhesion measured using shear testing machine (DARTEC HC-10) on cow tongue mucosa
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: SPSS software version 12, one-way ANOVA and Duncan tests, significance level p < 0.05
performance_metrics: Floating lag time: 5 seconds to 32 minutes
experimental_data.release_kinetics_summary: All formulations best fitted first-order release model. Korsmeyer-Peppas exponent n values between 0.5 and 1, indicating non-Fickian (anomalous) transport, coupling diffusion and erosion.

preparation_methods.immediate_release_pellets: Extrusion-spheronization method. Wet mass prepared in planetary mixer (160 rpm, 10 min), extruded at 1000 rpm (rotary gear extruder, Caleva model 40, 1.00 mm die), spheronized at 1000 rpm for 10 min (Caleva model 380), dried in fluidized bed dryer (Burkard, FBD 350S) at 60°C for 30 min.
preparation_methods.prolonged_release_coated_pellets: Fluidized bed coating (Aromatic-Fielder AG, Switzerland) with Wurster insert. Inner HPMC coat (2.5% w/w), outer Kollicoat SR-30D coat (15%, 17.5%, 20% w/w), and final HPMC overcoat (1% w/w).
preparation_methods.multiple_unit_capsules: Manual filling of 037_F1 (150 mg) and 037_F4 (185 mg) pellets into size '0' hard gelatin capsules.
preparation_methods.multiple_unit_tablets: Compression of 037_F1 pellets (150 mg), 037_F4 pellets (185 mg), and inert Ceolus KG-801 granules (150 mg) using single punch tableting machine (Manesty, England) with 10 mm flat-faced punches.

equations.yield: Yield(%) = (Weight of pellets / Weight of powder ingredients fed initially) × 100%

037_F2.notes: Contains only pseudoephedrine HCl, core for coating

037_F4.notes: Coated 037_F2 pellets, selected for final formulations

037_F1.geometric_mean_diameter: Not explicitly given, but characterized per Ref 14

037_F2.geometric_mean_diameter: Not explicitly given, but characterized per Ref 14

tablet_fabrication_parameters_F7.thickness_mm: Not specified (10 mm diameter punches used)

coating_process.device: Bottom spray fluidized bed coater (Aromatic-Fielder AG, Switzerland) with Wurster insert
coating_process.coating_sequence: HPMC inner coat (2.5%) → Kollicoat SR-30D outer coat (15, 17.5, 20%) → HPMC overcoat (1%)

037_F1.profile: Complete loratadine release within 2h; complete pseudoephedrine HCl IR portion within 10 min (when in final dosage form)

037_F2.profile: Immediate release of pseudoephedrine HCl (core for coating)

037_F3.medium: Distilled water, 37°C, USP I basket, 100 rpm

037_F4.medium: Distilled water, 37°C, USP I basket, 100 rpm

037_F5.medium: Distilled water, 37°C, USP I basket, 100 rpm

037_F7.factors_influencing_release: Compression process did not significantly alter release (p > 0.05) due to cushioning effect of Ceolus KG-801 and 037_F1 pellets.

tablet_properties_F7.stability: Not explicitly reported, but all parameters within BP specifications
tablet_properties_F7.thickness: Not specified (10 mm diameter punches used)

key_results.size_distribution: ~75% of pellets in 0.80-1.25 mm range for 037_F1 and 037_F2
key_results.coating_effect: Increased Kollicoat coating level significantly decreased drug release (p<0.05)
key_results.tablet_quality_F7: Friability 0.50±0.06%, Hardness 5.35±0.62 kg, Disintegration 6.44±0.53 min; all within BP limits

methods: One-way ANOVA with post hoc Tukey HSD

materials_origin.norfloxacin: Dr. Reddy's Labs, India (generous gift sample)
materials_origin.other_chemicals: All other chemicals used were of analytical grade.

experimental_design_for_optimization.ingredients_identification: Tablets prepared by wet granulation technique using polymers HPMC K4M, HPMC K100M, xanthan gum, and sodium bicarbonate as gas-generating agent. Binder solution was PVP K-30 in isopropyl alcohol.
experimental_design_for_optimization.preparation_method_summary: Dry ingredients were passed through 0.425 mm sieve, geometrically mixed, and blended. PVP K-30 binder solution in IPA was added with kneading to form homogeneous mass. Dough mass was passed through 2.0 mm sieve, granules dried at room temperature, then passed through 1.18 mm sieve. Granules were lubricated with magnesium stearate and talc, and compressed.
experimental_design_for_optimization.results_summary: Formulations exhibited controlled drug release, non-Fickian diffusion mechanism. 038_F4 was best overall (HPMC K4M based). 038_F9 best with HPMC K100M. 038_F16 best with xanthan gum. In vivo study on 038_F4 showed gastric retention time of 180±30 min.

data.pre_compression_parameters: Data not available in the provided article. The article discusses post-compression parameters only.
data.composition_of_independent_variables: The independent variables were the types and concentrations of polymers (HPMC K4M, HPMC K100M, xanthan gum) and the inclusion of lactose (for xanthan gum formulations). Their levels are detailed in the 'ingredients' section for each batch.
data.ftir_information: Data not available in the provided article.

process_parameters.compression_device: 16-station punching machine (Rimek, India)
process_parameters.compression_details: Oblong tablets of 16 mm length and 5.9-6.1 mm thickness.
process_parameters.process_conditions: Temperature: Room temperature for drying. No other conditions specified.

experimental_data.in_vitro_studies: Drug release conducted using USP 28 type II (paddle) apparatus in 750 mL HCl (pH 1.2) at 37±0.5°C, 50 rpm. Samples analyzed at 278 nm.
experimental_data.in_vivo_studies: Radiographic study on 4 healthy male volunteers (25±2 years, 55-75 kg) using BaSO4-loaded 038_F4 tablets. Tablets remained in stomach for 180±30 min.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Dissolution profile comparison using f1/f2 factors. f2 values for optimized batches vs. theoretical profile: 038_F4 (66), 038_F9 (63), 038_F16 (63).

materials_origin.losartan_potassium: Globe Pharmaceuticals Limited, Noakhali, Bangladesh (gift sample)

experimental_design_for_optimization.ingredients_identification: The formulation consisted of Losartan Potassium (API), Kollidon SR (release retardant polymer), Lactose (filler), and Magnesium stearate (lubricant).
experimental_design_for_optimization.preparation_method: Direct compression technique. The ingredients were blended by dry mixing, sized through a 40-mesh sieve, weighed, and compressed into tablets.
experimental_design_for_optimization.results: The release rate of losartan potassium decreased with an increase in polymer concentration. All formulations followed Higuchi release kinetics, indicating diffusion-dominated release. The release mechanism changed from non-fickian to fickian with decreasing polymer concentration.
experimental_design_for_optimization.equation: The release data was fitted to Zero-order (Q = k0t), First-order (LogQ = LogQ0 - k1t/2.303), Higuchi (Q = k2t^1/2), Hixson-Crowell (Q0^1/3 - Qt^1/3 = K0t), and Korsmeyer-Peppas (Log(Mt/M∞) = Logk + nLogt) equations.
experimental_design_for_optimization.absorbance: Drug content and dissolution samples were measured at 205 nm using a SHIMADZU UV-1800 spectrophotometer.

data.fourier_transform_infrared_spectral_meas: Not reported in the provided text.

tablet_fabrications.device: Hydraulic press (PIKE Technologies, India; Model: M-15) fitted with 13mm dies.

process_parameters.compression_process: Direct compression using a hydraulic press at a fixed compression force of 5 tons for 2 minutes.

equipment_used.compression_machine: Hydraulic press (PIKE Technologies, India; Model: M-15) with 13mm dies

039_F1.factors_influencing_release: Polymer concentration (highest in 039_F1) and diffusion through polymer matrix.

039_F2.factors_influencing_release: Polymer concentration and diffusion through polymer matrix.

039_F3.factors_influencing_release: Polymer concentration (lowest in 039_F3) and diffusion through polymer matrix.

039_F1.stability: Not reported directly for tablets; drug in solution was stable at 25°C for 24h.

039_F2.stability: Not reported directly for tablets; drug in solution was stable at 25°C for 24h.

039_F3.stability: Not reported directly for tablets; drug in solution was stable at 25°C for 24h.

experimental_data.key_results: Higher polymer concentration (039_F1) resulted in slower drug release, higher hardness, lower friability, and higher MDT. All formulations followed Higuchi release kinetics. Drug release was diffusion-dominated. Statistically significant differences (P<0.05) were found among drug release profiles.
experimental_data.performance_metrics: 039_F1 was identified as the best formulation for sustained release, with a t75% of 7.93 hours and MDT of 3.44 hours. All tablets met USP specifications for weight variation and drug content (100±5%).
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Statistically significant differences (P<0.05) were found among the drug release profiles from different polymeric matrices. Regression co-efficients (R²) were used to determine the best-fit kinetic model. Relative standard deviation (RSD) for UV method precision was 1.04%.

in_vitro_study_details.sampling: 5 mL aliquots withdrawn at specific time intervals and replaced with fresh medium.

optimization_factors: Polymer type (XG, SA, LAGG, HM Pectin)
Polymer amount (45% w/w or 72% w/w of total tablet weight)
Excipient ratio (Avicel 102 : PVP k30 in proportions 100:0, 75:25, 50:50, 25:75, 0:100)

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Direct compression method. Components mixed by geometric dilution with mortar and pestle for 15 min, passed through sieve number 20, then magnesium stearate added as lubricant.
ingredients_identification.dsc_results: ITOP showed sharp endothermic peak at 192.84°C. No significant changes with pectin, LAGG, XG, Avicel 102. Slight non-significant shift with PVP k30 and SA, not indicating incompatibility.
ingredients_identification.drug_content_equation: Content uniformity: Three tablets crushed, dissolved in 50 ml 0.1N HCl, stirred for 10 min, centrifuged, supernatant diluted and measured.
ingredients_identification.swelling_index_equation: % Water uptake = ((W2 - W1) / W1) × 100, where W1 = initial weight, W2 = weight after swelling.

flow_properties.hausners_ratio: < 1.25 (except pectin powders which exhibited fair flowability)

pre_compression_parameters.dsc_compatibility: No major incompatibility detected between ITOP and excipients.

process_parameters.compression_device: Single compression machine, model Karishma Pharma Machines 4040, Mumbai, India

equipment_used.dissolution_apparatus: USP paddle apparatus type II (SR8 Plus, USA)

release_properties_summary.factors_influencing_release: Polymer type, polymer concentration, presence of binder/diluent, swelling behavior, erosion

swelling_study.xg_tablets: Rapid swelling, maintained integrity up to 24h. Water uptake ~245% at 3.5h for 45% w/w XG.
swelling_study.sa_tablets: Swelling till 3.5-4h, then lost integrity. Water uptake ~116% at 3.5h for 45% w/w SA.

in_vivo_study_x7.fasting_state: Tablet in stomach at 0.5h, small intestine at 1.5h, disappeared by 4h.
in_vivo_study_x7.fed_state: Tablet in stomach at 0.5, 1.5, 4, and 6h. Expansion observed at 4h and 6h.
in_vivo_study_x7.conclusion: Gastric retention enhanced in fed state due to slower motility.

experimental_data.key_results: Sustained release of alogliptin achieved using bentonite with HPMC as binder. Release followed Fickian diffusion. XRD showed shift in montmorillonite peak indicating intercalation of drug, but HPMC helped maintain gel structure.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: t-test with 95% confidence interval (p < 0.05) used for similarity testing.
experimental_data.zero_order_release: Correlation coefficients for zero-order model at 7h and 24h are provided in release_properties.
experimental_data.performance_metrics: Tablets complied with pharmacopoeial requirements for weight variation and hardness; friability acceptable except for HPMC30% (1.97%).

data.abstract: The abstract is on Page 1 of the document. It states the purpose of the study was to formulate double-layer tablets of lornoxicam (LRX) by direct compression and evaluate their physical and drug release characteristics. The outer layer contained an immediate-release dose (4 mg) with MCC, starch, and lactose. The cores for sustained release used HPMC, PVP K90, and carbomer. The analytical method (UV spectroscopy) was validated for linearity, precision, accuracy, recovery, and specificity. Results showed HPMC-based cores had better flow properties. Drug release followed the Higuchi kinetic model via diffusion/polymer relaxation or diffusion/erosion. The conclusion was that these tablets are suitable for treating inflammatory conditions.
data.introduction: The introduction is on Pages 1 and 2. It discusses the advantages of controlled drug delivery systems and the need for an initial fast release followed by a sustained release for drugs like NSAIDs. It introduces double-layer tablets as a solution for biphasic release. It describes lornoxicam (LRX) as an NSAID from the oxicam family with a half-life of 3-4 hours, used for postoperative pain. The aim was to design a double-layer tablet with a 4 mg immediate-release outer layer and a 12 mg sustained-release core to improve bioavailability and patient compliance.

materials_and_methods.experimental_design_for_optimization: The experimental design involved preparing 8 different core formulations and 8 corresponding double-layer tablet formulations by varying the type and concentration of polymers (HPMC, PVP K90, Carbomer) in the core. The outer layer composition was kept constant. The goal was to optimize the formulation for biphasic release (immediate from outer layer, sustained from core).

ingredients_identification.preparation: Tablet cores (100 mg) containing 12 mg LRX were compressed using a hydraulic press under 13790 kPa force with a 7.9 mm flat punch. They were then coated with a 150 mg outer layer containing 4 mg LRX and compressed again using a 10 mm flat punch under 13790 kPa force to form the final 250 mg double-layer tablet.
ingredients_identification.results: The method produced tablets with good physical characteristics and biphasic drug release.

materials_and_methods.composition_of_independent_variables: The independent variables were the type of polymer (HPMC, PVP K90, Carbomer) and their concentration in the core (low and high levels: 5% & 20% for HPMC; 5% & 10% for PVP K90; 15% & 20% for Carbomer; and mixtures of HPMC and PVP K90).
materials_and_methods.ftir_spectral_measurements: Not mentioned in the provided text.

process_parameters.compression_process: Direct compression using a hydraulic press (Yeniyurt, Turkey). Tablet cores were compressed first, then coated with the outer layer and compressed again.
process_parameters.equipment_used: Hydraulic press (Yeniyurt, Turkey) with 7.9 mm and 10 mm flat punches.

validation_results.specificity: Specific; excipients did not interfere at 376 nm.

in_vitro_drug_release.general: The outer layer of all double-layer tablets disintegrated and released its LRX content within 45 seconds. Drug release from cores followed the Higuchi kinetic model.

tablet_properties.swelling_behavior: Highest swelling, peak at 8h, 52.75% remained at 24h
Highest swelling, peak at 8h, 52.01% remained at 24h

experimental_data.key_results: All formulations met compendial requirements. Double-layer tablets provided immediate release from the outer layer (within 45 seconds) and sustained release from the core. The slowest release was from PVP K90-based cores. Release was governed by the Higuchi model. Carbomer-based cores showed Supercase II transport due to high swelling, while others showed non-Fickian diffusion/erosion.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: One-way ANOVA was used to determine differences in release profiles, swelling, and erosion data (p < 0.05). Significant differences were found among release profiles of all core formulations.

data.conclusion: The conclusion is on Pages 7 and 8. It states that double-layer tablets with cores based on HPMC and HPMC-PVP mixtures displayed better flow and compression properties. Although PVP K90 and carbomer cores had poor flow, the final double-layer tablets had desired physical characteristics. The study concluded that these double-layer tablets could reduce dosing frequency and improve patient compliance in treating inflammatory conditions.

key_results: Resin incorporation decreases hardness of both HPMC and EC matrices; HPMC matrices become more friable above critical hardness (~16-17 N), while EC matrices maintain low friability due to higher baseline hardness.
In deionized water, resin further retards release from HPMC matrices via ion exchange; release decreases with increasing resin.
In EC matrices, resin causes disintegration leading to initial rapid release, followed by decline due to drug-resin complex formation.
In ionic solutions (KCl, SGF, SIF), release increases due to competition of cations (K+ > Na+ > H+) with DPH+ for exchange sites.
Disintegration time of EC matrices decreases with higher resin content (from 46 min at 5% to 5 min at 40%).
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: None reported; descriptive statistics (%CV, ranges) provided.
experimental_data.in_vitro_results: Release profiles provided as approx_release_profile for each batch in release_properties.

release_properties.general_observations: All formulations showed 81-99% drug release sustained for 12 hours. Release mechanism determined by Korsmeyer-Peppas model.
release_properties.factors_influencing_release: Binder type and concentration, processing technique, polymer modification (chemical modification significantly affected release, physical modification did not).

tablet_properties.factorial_batches_responses: See formulation_details_batches for tensile strength, friability, disintegration time of batches 1-8.
tablet_properties.other_properties_reported: For individual polymer formulations, crushing strength, thickness, weight, disintegration time (sec) given in Tables 3 and 4. No stability data, water uptake, floating time, or lag time reported.
tablet_properties.disintegration_time_acceptance: All uncoated tablets had DT < 15 min (BP limit) except some wet granulation batches? Actually DT for wet granulation NG/4 was 249 sec (~4 min) still <15 min. OK.

experimental_data.key_results: Enterolobium gum (native and modified) produced tablets with 81-99% drug release sustained for 12 hours. Chemical modification increased drug release similar to HPMC. Physical modification did not significantly alter release. Wet granulation gave higher mechanical strength. Factorial analysis: X1 (polymer nature) had highest effect on disintegration time; X1X3 interaction greatest effect on mechanical properties; X1X2 interaction greatest effect on drug release.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Student's t-test, p ≤ 0.05 significant. Main effects: X1 effect on DT = 5.427, X2 effect on DT = 1.607, X3 effect on DT = -2.493. Interaction coefficients: X1X2 on DT = -1.672, X1X3 on DT = 1.428, X2X3 on DT = 0.608.
experimental_data.zero_and_first_order_release: All formulations had highest R² in Korsmeyer-Peppas model (0.96-0.99); zero-order R² ranged 0.584-0.765; first-order R² 0.793-0.880.
experimental_data.swelling_water_uptake: Only polymer swelling indices reported (see pre_compression_parameters); no tablet swelling or floating studies.

abstract.summary: The study investigated the effect of different polymers (Eudragit RS PO and Kollidon SR) on the release profile and bio-adhesive strength of ciprofloxacin HCl from co-matrix tablets. Tablets were prepared by direct compression. Bio-adhesion was measured using an in vitro wash-off method. Results showed Eudragit RS PO tablets released a greater percentage of drug, while Kollidon SR and xanthan gum provided better bio-adhesive strength. Xanthan gum was identified as a suitable synergist for controlled release, meeting USP specifications.

introduction.background: Orally administered controlled release dosage forms face challenges like short gastric retention time (GRT) and unpredictable gastric emptying. Bio-adhesive systems are explored to prolong GI residence time and improve site-specific drug absorption. Matrix systems using polymers are common for sustained release. Polymers can be insoluble/skeleton matrices or hydrophobic/erodible materials. Drug release mechanisms include diffusion, degradation, and swelling. Hydrophobic polymers can control release and enhance mucosal retention.

experimental_design_for_optimization.preparation_method: Direct compression technique. Active ingredients, polymers, filler, lubricant, and flow promoter were blended and compressed.
experimental_design_for_optimization.results: Drug release and bio-adhesion were evaluated. Higher polymer content increased MDT and generally decreased drug release. Bio-adhesion strength decreased as xanthan gum content decreased.
equation_used: Korsmeyer-Peppas: log(M/M_s) = log k + n log t

experimental_data.key_results: Kollidon SR provided better drug retarding ability and bio-adhesion compared to Eudragit RS PO. Xanthan gum acted as a bio-adhesion synergist. Higher polymer concentrations led to lower drug release and higher MDT. Bio-adhesion strength decreased with decreasing xanthan gum content.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: R² values for kinetic modeling are provided. Korsmeyer-Peppas model showed high fit (R² > 0.98) for all formulations.

conclusion.summary: Eudragit RS PO with xanthan gum is suitable for bio-adhesive sustained release dosage forms. Kollidon SR offers good sustaining properties with moderate bio-adhesion. The choice of polymer and concentration is critical for optimizing release and bio-adhesion. Further in vivo studies are recommended to confirm these findings.

experimental_design_for_optimization.purpose: To investigate the effect of different polysulfonate resins (Dowex 50W and Amberlite 050_IRP69) and direct compression fillers (MCC, DCP, SDRS) on physical properties and drug release of sustained-release DMP resinate tablets.

dmp_resinate_preparation.method: Batch process. Resin (10g) was added to 500 mL of 2% w/v DMP HBr solution and shaken in a water bath at 37°C for 2 hours. The resinates were filtered, washed with distilled water, and dried overnight at 50°C.
dmp_resinate_preparation.equation_for_release_kinetics: -ln(1 - F) = kt^0.65, where F is fractional drug release, k is release rate constant, and t is time. This is derived from a particle diffusion-controlled model.

key_findings.tablet_hardness: MCC > SDRS > DCP. Tablets with Dowex 50W (spherical) were harder than those with Amberlite 050_IRP69 (rod/plate-like).
key_findings.disintegration_time: MCC and DCP < 30 sec; SDRS resulted in longer times due to gel formation.
key_findings.drug_release: Release followed particle diffusion model (-ln(1-F) vs t^0.65). DCP with Dowex 50W showed increased release rate (resinate fracture). Amberlite 050_IRP69 (higher cross-linking) resisted fracture, protecting release profile.

experimental_data.statistical_analysis: One-way ANOVA with least significant difference test (P < 0.05) was used to determine significant effects.

tablet_fabrication.process: Weighed 400 mg granules compressed using Carver Hydraulic tablet press (Model C, Carver Inc, USA). Die size 10.5 mm, flat-faced punches. Lubricated with 2% w/v magnesium stearate dispersion in ether-ethanol (1:1) prior to each compression.

evaluation_parameters: Crushing strength (diametral compression, Karnara Industrial Cooperation, Bombay)
tablet_fabrication.individual_batch_data: Specific hardness, friability, dimensions for each batch not provided in a single table; values vary with compression pressure. Crushing strength at 2.0% w/w: Cedrela gum 130.95 N, HPMC 117.85 N. Friability for all formulations <1.0%.

process_parameters.compression_device: Carver Hydraulic tablet press (Model C, Carver Inc, Menomonee Falls, Wisconsin, USA)
process_parameters.lubricant: 2% w/v dispersion of magnesium stearate in ether-ethanol (1:1)
process_parameters.drying_duration: 24 h for granules; 18 h for gum

release_properties.sampling: 5 mL withdrawn at intervals, replaced with fresh medium
release_properties.analysis: UV spectrophotometer (Unico, UV2102 PC) at 221 nm
release_properties.mechanism: Non-linear release with decreasing rate, diffusional resistance. For HPMC tablets, possible disintegration; for gum tablets, possible erosion. Non-disintegrating for gum formulations.

common_observations.disintegration: Gum formulations were non-disintegrating; HPMC formulations disintegrated faster.
common_observations.adhesion: Highest at 4% w/w Cedrela gum in pH 6.8 buffer (150.08 ± 6.82 min). See Table IV.

Cedrela gum.gurnham_c: Low (0.01-0.02), not significantly different from HPMC

dissolution_characteristics.sd: Standard deviations included in Table III.

adhesion_time.sd: Standard deviations included in Table IV.

key_results: Cedrela gum is a low viscosity polymer (48 cPs) with a hydroxyl group.
Formulations with Cedrela gum showed better flow (angle of repose 24-27°), lower Py and Pk values (more plastic) than HPMC (p<0.05).
Gurnham equation showed limitations for plasto-elastic materials.
Crushing strength at 2% binder: Cedrela gum (130.95 N) > HPMC (117.85 N). Friability <1% for all.
Cedrela gum tablets were non-disintegrating with significantly longer drug release than HPMC.
At 4% binder, Cedrela gum showed highest bioadhesion in pH 6.8 buffer (150.08 min).
No damage to intestinal mucosa observed in histopathological evaluation.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: ANOVA and t-test used. p ≤ 0.05 considered significant. Cedrela gum significantly more plastic than HPMC (p<0.05). Crushing strength significantly higher for gum formulations (p<0.05).
in_vitro_studies: Bioadhesion (rotating cylinder method on pig ileum in pH 6.8 buffer and 0.1 M HCl)
experimental_data.histopathology: Light microscopy of intestinal mucosa before and after adhesion showed no damage.

process_parameters.equipment_used: 3-axial homogenizer Turbula (T2C WAB, Basel, CH) for mixing; SOTAX AT 7 Online System for USP 2 dissolution; Golem v2 apparatus for dynamic dissolution; Texture Analyser CT 3 for gel layer; MIRA3 SEM for surface; SU 8010 CryoSEM for gel morphology.
process_parameters.process_conditions: Mixing for 10 min. Dissolution at 37°C. Gel layer study at 37°C.

experimental_data.key_results: NEU alone as filler accelerated drug release in USP II, especially at low HPMC concentrations, but this effect was mitigated in the biorelevant dynamic dissolution study. The combination of NEU and MCC (N75M50, N50M50) showed improved flowability, higher tablet hardness, and a shift towards zero-order kinetics. These mixed-filler samples also showed similarity between USP II and biorelevant dissolution profiles, suggesting better in vivo predictability.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Multivariate data analysis (factor analysis with varimax rotation) was used to evaluate relationships between formulation variables (HPMC, NEU, MCC) and quality parameters (hardness, gel layer, dissolution at various time points). Similarity factor f2 analysis was used to compare dissolution profiles.
experimental_data.in_vitro_studies: USP apparatus 2 dissolution test (12h, pH 1.2 for 2h then 6.8) and biorelevant dynamic dissolution study using Golem v2 apparatus (4h, simulated GIT with stomach, duodenum, jejunum, ileum compartments, dynamic pH and volume).

experimental_design_for_optimization.ingredients_identification: The study used Ambroxol Hydrochloride (AH) as the active pharmaceutical ingredient (API). Hypromellose (HPMC) of various viscosities (K100LV, K4M, K15M) was used as the primary matrix material. Surelease® (aqueous ethylcellulose dispersion) was used as the insoluble film coating polymer, and Opadry® (HPMC-based) was used as a pore-former in the coating. Other excipients included MCC, mannitol, colloidal silicon dioxide, and magnesium stearate.
experimental_design_for_optimization.preparation_method: Core tablets were prepared by direct compression of a dry mixture of API, HPMC, fillers, and glidant, followed by lubrication and compression. Coated tablets were prepared by spraying an aqueous dispersion of Surelease® and Opadry® onto the core tablets in a coating pan.
experimental_design_for_optimization.results: The study successfully produced robust matrix tablets with uniform film coatings. Drug release rates could be tailored by adjusting HPMC viscosity/amount, pore-former ratio in the coating, and coating weight gain. Coated tablets exhibited an initial lag time followed by near zero-order release kinetics. In vivo studies in beagle dogs showed a prolonged lag time with decreased coating membrane permeability, correlating with in vitro release.
experimental_design_for_optimization.equation: Zero-order: Mt/M∞ = kt; Power Law: Mt/M∞ = ktⁿ
experimental_design_for_optimization.absorbance: In vitro drug content was determined by UV/Vis spectrophotometry at a wavelength of 244 nm (USP Apparatus II) or 307 nm (USP Apparatus III).

data.pre_compression_parameters: Information not explicitly provided in the article. The text mentions mixing in a three-dimensional blender and lubrication, but no data on powder properties like bulk density, tapped density, Carr's index, or Hausner ratio were reported.
data.ftir_spectral_measurements: Information not provided in the article.

tablet_fabrications.general_method: Core tablets were fabricated by direct compression using a single punch press (Beijing Gylongli Sci. & Tech. Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Powders were mixed in a three-dimensional blender (WAB Machinery Co., Ltd., Shenzhen, China). Tablet dimensions were 8.8 mm standard biconvex round.
tablet_fabrications.hardness: Measured using a hardness tester (YD-35, Tianda Tianfa Technology Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China) (n=6).
tablet_fabrications.friability: Determined using a friabilator (FAB-2, Tianda Tianfa Technology Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China) with 22 tablets at 25 rpm for 4 min (n=3).
tablet_fabrications.weight_variation: Tablets were accurately weighed by an electronic balance (BSA223S-CW, Sartorius, Germany). Content uniformity was up to standard.
tablet_fabrications.thickness_and_diameter: Not explicitly measured, but tablet size is stated as 8.8 mm diameter.
tablet_fabrications.drug_content: Determined by UV/Vis spectrophotometry. Content uniformity was reported to be up to standard.

compression_process.device: Single punch press (Beijing Gylongli Sci. & Tech. Co., Ltd., Beijing, China)
compression_process.pressure: Not specified, but adjusted to achieve a target hardness range of 80-100 N.

coating_process.device: Fully perforated coating pan (LABCOAT I, O'Hara Technologies, Canada)

tablet_properties.general_properties: All core and coated tablets were white in color. Weight variation and content uniformity were up to standard. Friability for all formulations was less than 1%.
tablet_properties.hardness: For HPMC K4M-based core tablets, the hardness was set in the range of 80-100 N to ensure robustness for subsequent coating. Individual batch data for other formulations not specified.
tablet_properties.disintegration_time: Information not provided, as these are extended-release tablets not intended to disintegrate.
tablet_properties.dissolution_profile: See 'release_properties' for kinetics and 'experimental_data' for parameters.
tablet_properties.stability: Selected coated tablets (K4M-based, 80:20, 3.5% WG) showed no obvious change in appearance, drug content, or release rate after 10 days at 60°C, 75%±5% RH, and 4500lx±500lx.
tablet_properties.weight_variation: Up to standard, but specific data not provided.
tablet_properties.lag_time: Coated tablets with 80:20 Surelease:Opadry ratio and 3.5% WG exhibited an initial lag time of about 0.5-1 hour in vitro. In vivo, lag time increased as coating permeability decreased.

in_vitro_release_data.F1_K100LV_core: Fastest release rate among viscosity grades.
in_vitro_release_data.F2_K4M_core: Moderate release rate. Release faster in early stage (0-8h) than late stage (8-20h).
in_vitro_release_data.F4_25%K4M: Fastest release among different HPMC amounts.
in_vitro_release_data.F8_80:20_coated: Initial lag time (0.5-1h) followed by linear release.
in_vitro_release_data.F10_60:40_coated: Lag time eliminated, but release rate decreased in final period compared to F8.
in_vitro_release_data.F12_5.5%WG: Longer lag time and slower release than F11.
in_vitro_release_data.F13_7.5%WG: Longest lag time and slowest release among weight gains.

experimental_data.zero_and_first_order_release: Zero-order kinetics were observed for coated tablets (R² > 0.99). Uncoated tablets followed Power Law kinetics (non-Fickian), with n values between 0.46 and 0.89.

dependent_responses.response: R2: Percentage of drug released after 24 h

ingredients_identification.ez_nlc_preparation_method: The oily phase (stearic acid and oleic acid, 30%) was melted at 348°K. The aqueous phase (1% Tween 80 and 2% poloxamer 188 in distilled water) was heated to the same temperature. 10mg EZ was added to the molten lipid. The aqueous phase was added dropwise to the oily phase under magnetic stirring at 1400rpm and 333°K for 15min. The pre-emulsion was ultrasonicated using a probe sonicator at 100% amplitude for 5min. The dispersion was mixed with drying excipients (Avicel alone or with mannitol) (5-7.5 w/%) until a homogeneous paste was formed. The paste was frozen at 253°K overnight and lyophilized (pre-freezing at 193°K for 15min, ramp 20min, main drying at 3 mbar for 6h). The resulting powder was mixed with 10mg magnesium stearate, 10mg talc, and CCS.

formulation_details.note: The specific mass of drying excipients and CCS for each of the 13 individual batches were not listed separately in the text but were varied according to the experimental design levels.

pre_compression_parameters.summary_statistics: All formulations showed good flowability according to USP 39/NF 34. ANOVA showed no significant difference between formulations.

fourier_transform_infrared_spectral_meas.findings: Characteristic peaks of EZ were seen in the EZ-NLCs tablet spectrum and disappeared in the blank NLCs tablet spectrum, confirming encapsulation and lack of interaction.

tablet_fabrications.machine: Tablet compression machine (P.O. BOX 404, MUMBAI, INDIA)
tablet_fabrications.tablet_weight_mg: Approx. 500mg powder + 500-750mg drying excipients + 15-30mg CCS + 10mg talc + 10mg mag stearate

release_properties.summary: The release profiles showed a biphasic pattern. Tablets with 30mg CCS followed First-Order kinetics. Tablets with 15mg CCS followed Higuchi kinetics.
release_properties.dissolution_medium: Buffer transition system: 650mL 0.1N HCl (pH 1.2) for 2h, then 250mL disodium hydrogen orthophosphate added to achieve pH 6.8 for remaining 22h. 1% SLS added to maintain sink conditions.
release_properties.apparatus: USP standard dissolution tester, Apparatus II (paddle)

release_properties.note: The paper states that tablets with 30mg CCS followed First-Order kinetics, and those with 15mg CCS followed Higuchi. Batch IDs 1,6,11,12 likely contain 15mg CCS; Batches 3,5,8,9 likely contain 30mg CCS.

experimental_data.in_vitro_release_findings: EZ-NLCs tablets showed higher release than ezetrol®. In first 2h (pH 1.2), release ranged from 53.84% to 90.6% (vs 48.3% for ezetrol). At 24h, release ranged from 87.4% to 99% (vs 67.17% for ezetrol). Optimized tablet released 98.3 ± 3.95% in 24h.

in_vivo_pharmacokinetics.study_design: Parallel, single oral dose of 25mg/kg in Sprague Dawley male rats (n=3 per group).
in_vivo_pharmacokinetics.conclusion: EZ-NLCs tablet showed 1.5-fold improvement in oral bioavailability compared to ezetrol®.

article_sections.abstract: The present study aimed to formulate and evaluate the formulated sustained-release floating matrix tablets of valganciclovir hydrochloride to produce a stable and bioavailable dosage form. The tablets were prepared using hydrophilic and hydrophobic polymers such as ethyl cellulose, Hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC), and Povidone. The formulations were subjected to evaluation characteristics such as drug content, hardness, friability, floating lag time, total floating time, and In vitro drug dissolution studies. The formulation composition and method of manufacturing are novel for this particular active moiety and robustness was assessed using a central composite design. All the formulation trials exhibited more than 90% of drug release in 12 h duration, with a floating lag time of more than 11 h, and drug content was found more than 90% across the batches. The formulated valganciclovir hydrochloride sustained release floating matrix tablets showed an increased GRT with a sustained release for 12 h, thereby allowing a better window for absorption and consequently improving the drug's therapeutic effect.
article_sections.introduction: Provides background on cytomegalovirus, valganciclovir hydrochloride, and gastroretentive drug delivery systems. Highlights the aim to formulate sustained-release floating matrix tablets.
article_sections.materials_and_methods: Lists materials, methodology including melting point, solubility, calibration curve, drug-excipient compatibility, drug content, wet granulation formulation, compression, in vitro dissolution, floating lag time determination.
article_sections.results_and_discussion: Presents melting point, solubility, λmax, FTIR compatibility, formulation development using central composite design, hardness/friability/assay results, dissolution profiles, floating lag time and total floating time.
article_sections.conclusion: Confirms purity, compatibility, ideal in-process parameters, robustness, and reproducibility of the optimized formulation.

ftir_info.samples: Pure drug, drug + HPMC, drug + all inactive ingredients
ftir_info.conclusion: No major interaction between drug and excipients

equipment.mill: Co-mill with 1.0 mm grated screen at 900 rpm
equipment.compression_machine: Tablet press with 15 mm plain concave punch

mixing_parameters.binder_solution: povidone K30 in purified water, 12.5% w/w of granulation mass

tablet_properties.dissolution_profile: >90% release at 12 h for all batches

experimental_data_summary.in_vitro_dissolution: All batches showed >90% drug release within 12 h, with sustained release pattern. Mean release at 12 h: 96.40 ± 1.28% (n=12).
experimental_data_summary.floating_performance: All tablets exhibited floating lag time <150 s and total floating time >11 h, ensuring prolonged gastric retention.
experimental_data_summary.hardness_friability: Hardness consistent (196–210 N), friability 0% across all batches, indicating good mechanical strength.
experimental_data_summary.statistical_analysis: Data presented as mean ± SD; n=3 for hardness and friability, n=6 for dissolution and floating parameters.

article_title: FORMULATION OF METFORMIN HYDROCHLORIDE SUSTAINED RELEASE MATRIX TABLET AND VALIDATION BY IN-VITRO EQUIVALENCE STUDIES

data.article_title: FORMULATION OF METFORMIN HYDROCHLORIDE SUSTAINED RELEASE MATRIX TABLET AND VALIDATION BY IN-VITRO EQUIVALENCE STUDIES

sections.abstract: The study developed a Metformin HCl sustained-release matrix tablet using wet granulation with Eudragit RSPO, HPMC, and Xantural 75. Physical parameters complied with BP/USP. In vitro equivalence (biowaiver) showed similarity to innovator (Glucophage) with f2 > 62 in pH 1.2, 4.5, 6.8. Release kinetics fitted quadratic model. Stable under accelerated conditions.
sections.introduction: Background on type 2 diabetes, metformin pharmacology, need for SR formulation, polymers (HPMC, Xantural 75, Eudragit RSPO) and release mechanisms.
sections.conclusion: Formulation H was equivalent to innovator with better drug release; stable for 6 months.

materials_origin.packaging_materials: 130 μm cold forming base foil, aluminum foil with 20 μm film thickness (Alu/Alu); high-density polyethylene (HDPE) containers

experimental_design.optimization_approach: Sequential development of formulations A to H by varying polymer composition to achieve USP dissolution criteria and similarity to innovator.
experimental_design.preparation_method: Wet granulation technique; granules dried, milled, mixed with lubricant, compressed.
experimental_design.dissolution_media: pH 6.8, 4.5, 1.2 at 37°C, USP apparatus 2 (paddle), 100 rpm, sampling at 1,2,4,6,8,10 h.
experimental_design.evaluation_criteria: Weight variation, friability (<1%), hardness, thickness, diameter, assay (HPLC), dissolution (USP limits), f1/f2 factors, stability (ICH).

ingredients_identification.absorbance_maxima: At pH 1.2: 205 nm; pH 4.5: 232 nm; pH 6.8: 198 and 232 nm (198 predominating).
ingredients_identification.standard_curves: R² = 0.999 for all three pH media; concentration in pH 1.2 was ≈3.5 times higher than pH 4.5/6.8 for same absorbance at 232 nm.
ingredients_identification.stability_in_media: No significant variation in absorbance over 19 h in pH 6.8, 4.5, 1.2 for standard and sample solutions (Table 2).
ingredients_identification.equation_for_absorbance: Not explicitly given; linear correlation concentration vs absorbance.

release_properties.release_mechanism: Diffusion, swelling, erosion; hydrogel formation controlled by hydrophilic polymers (HPMC, Xantural 75, Eudragit RSPO).

kinetics.best_fit_model: Quadratic model (highest adjusted R², lowest AIC/BIC) for all batches and innovator.

release_properties.cumulative_release_H: 94.2–96.5% at 10 h in three media; innovator: 89.9–92.0%

stability_data.conclusion: Stable for 6 months under accelerated conditions per ICH and USP.

additional_notes.pre_compression_parameters: Not reported (e.g., angle of repose, bulk density)

release_kinetics_summary.general_mechanism: For HPMC matrices, drug release follows diffusion-controlled mechanism, likely Korsmeyer-Peppas model with n value indicating anomalous transport. XG matrices show initial burst in acidic pH. Sodium alginate matrices show reduced release due to protonation.
factors_influencing_release: Polymer molecular weight (higher HPMC MW reduces release)
Polymer concentration (higher concentration reduces release)
Polymer type (XG gives faster initial release; HPMC gives more controlled)
Diluent type (lactose increases release due to pore formation)
pH (acidic medium affects XG and Na-Alg behavior)
Compression method (wet granulation vs direct compression slightly affects release)

swelling_and_mucoadhesion_analysis.mucoadhesion_correlation: Increased polymer concentration and higher molecular weight increase mucoadhesion strength (p < 0.0001).

content.details: Tara gum (TG) isolate and other excipients (talc, Aerosil, magnesium stearate, lactose DCL21, sodium starch glycolate) were obtained from Vineet Analytical Research Laboratories Pvt Ltd. (Pioma Chemicals, Mumbai, Maharashtra, India).

Tablets containing imatinib were developed using TG extract. Conventional tablets were made with a variable ratio of excipients to TG utilizing the direct compression process. All excipients were well-mixed except magnesium stearate. Following adequate mixing of the extract and other ingredients, Aerosil and magnesium stearate were added and combined for a further 2-3 min.

content.source: Materials and methods, Phytochemical screening, Content uniformity sections

details.preparation: The optimized tablet formulation was used for the initial phytochemical per standard procedures [18]. For content uniformity, 10 tablets from each formulation were ground into a fine powder. Part of the powder (20 mg of imatinib) was dissolved in methanol, filtered, and appropriately diluted.
details.results: Phytochemical screening revealed the presence of saponins, tannins, phenolics, flavonoids, carbohydrates, proteins, and amino acids. Steroids, alkaloids, fats, and oils were absent.
details.absorbance: Absorbance for drug content and dissolution studies was measured at 238 nm using a UV-visible spectrophotometer.

content.source: Not explicitly provided in the text
content.details: The document does not contain specific pre-compression parameters (micromeritic properties) for the powder blends of individual batches such as bulk density, tapped density, Carr's index, or Hausner ratio.

data.section: Composition of Independent Variables and Their Levels

content.details: The independent variable was the concentration of Tara Gum (TG) and Sodium starch glycolate. TG levels: 100 mg (F1), 120 mg (F2), 140 mg (F3), 160 mg (F4), 180 mg (F5), 200 mg (F6). Sodium starch glycolate levels: 50 mg (F1), 60 mg (F2), 70 mg (F3), 80 mg (F4), 90 mg (F5), 100 mg (F6).

data.section: Fourier Transform Infrared Spectral Measurements Information

content.details: The document does not contain any information regarding FTIR spectral measurements or compatibility studies.

details.equipment: Rotary punching machine, Roche friabilator, Pfizer hardness tester, USP type-II dissolution apparatus
details.process_conditions: Tablets were compressed at room temperature. Dissolution studies were conducted at 37 ± 0.5°C.

details.general_mechanism: Extended-release behavior due to rate-retarding polymer (TG). Specific release kinetics (zero order, first order, Hixson-Crowell, Korsmeyer-Peppas) were not calculated or discussed.
details.factors_influencing_release: Concentration of Tara Gum (TG). Higher TG concentration (F6) resulted in more sustained release.

batches.note: Exhibited excellent extended-release behavior with 72% release over 12h.

details.key_results: All formulations showed extended-release behavior. F6 exhibited the best sustained release with 72% drug release over 12 hours. TG tablets are rich in phytoconstituents like saponins, tannins, phenolics, flavonoids, carbohydrates, proteins, and amino acids.
details.performance_metrics: Hardness (4.0-5.3 N), Friability (0.45-0.93%), Drug Content (98.11-100.20%), Disintegration Time (10-23 min).
details.statistical_analysis: Results are presented as mean ± SD (Standard Deviation) where applicable.
details.zero_first_order_release: Zero-order and first-order release kinetics were not calculated for any formulation.
details.swelling_study: Tablet swelling/water uptake study (lag time, total floating time, swelling index) was not conducted or mentioned.

experimental_design_for_optimization.purpose: To design a pulsatile formulation of lornoxicam to treat rheumatoid arthritis as per the chronotherapeutic pattern of the disease, targeting drug release in the early morning hours.
experimental_design_for_optimization.approach: Two-step process: 1) Preparation of fast-disintegrating core tablets with varying disintegrant (crospovidone) concentration. 2) Compression coating of the optimized core tablet with different ratios and combinations of hydrophobic (ethyl cellulose) and hydrophilic (HPMC K4M, L-hpc, NaCMC) polymers to achieve a lag time of ~5.5 hours.
experimental_design_for_optimization.optimized_formulation: F-5, which released lornoxicam after a lag time of 5.5 ± 0.7 hours and 99.81 ± 0.81% up to 8 hours.

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Physical mixture of drug and polymers.

release_kinetics.model_fitting: Not specified in the provided text.
release_kinetics.mechanism: Likely erosion/diffusion from the hydrophilic polymer layer followed by rapid disintegration/dispersion of the core.

key_results: Core tablets with 10% crospovidone (LCT-4) showed fastest disintegration (1.52 min) and 99.93% drug release in 30 min.
Optimized formulation F-5 (L-hpc:EC 150:150 mg) provided a lag time of 5.5 ± 0.7 h and 99.81 ± 0.81% release by 8 h.
Order of extending lag time: HPMC-K4M > L-hpc > NaCMC.
FTIR and DSC confirmed no drug-polymer interaction.
F-5 was stable for 6 months at 40°C/75% RH as per ICH guidelines.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Results expressed as mean ± standard deviation (n=3 or as specified). Level of significance chosen as p ≤ 0.05.

stability_data_f5.note: The text reports '8.27 ± 1.15' for lag time after stability, which appears to be a typo or unit mismatch, likely meaning 5.5 hours.

data.note: Selected as the optimized core tablet for compression coating due to fastest disintegration (1.52 min).

data.materials_origin: Naproxen (gift from IPS Pharma Training Institute, Hyderabad); Sodium starch glycolate (Rajesh Chemicals, Mumbai); PVP-K-30 (Himedia Chem. Lab, Mumbai); Magnesium stearate, lactose, acetone, triethyl citrate, isopropanol (S.D Fine Chem. Lab, Mumbai); Aerozil (Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai); Talc (Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd, Mumbai); Eudragit 067_L100 and 067_S100 (gift from Degussa India Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai); Empty HPMC capsules (gift from ACG Associated Capsules Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai).
data.experimental_design: Optimization by varying ratio of Eudragit 067_L100:067_S100 (1:0, 2:1, 1:1, 1:2, 0:1) and coating level (10%, 15%, 20%) to achieve lag time of ~5 h and complete release at pH 7.2.

ingredients_identification.FTIR: Naproxen peaks at 1726, 1685, 1606, 1567 cm⁻¹; physical mixture with polymers showed all characteristic peaks, indicating no interaction.
ingredients_identification.DSC: Naproxen endotherm at 156.76°C; physical mixture showed similar endotherm, confirming compatibility.

coating.coating_solution_composition: Eudragit 067_L100 and/or 067_S100 6.00 g, Triethyl citrate 0.600 g, Talc 3.00 g, Acetone 34.69 g, Isopropanol 51.42 g, Water 4.29 g (total 100 g)

data.ftir_spectral: No interaction; peaks of naproxen intact in physical mixture.

process_parameters.compression_device: Rotary tablet press (Karnavati Engineering, Ahmadabad) with 3 mm round concave punches
process_parameters.coating_equipment: 6-inch lab scale coating pan with spray gun (United Technologies, Mumbai)

release_properties.release_mechanism: pH-dependent; Eudragit 067_L100 soluble above pH 6, Eudragit 067_S100 soluble above pH 7.
release_properties.release_profile: Not specified numerically for 067_F1; qualitative: released in pH 6.5, 6.8, and 7.2 media (failed to prevent release at pH 6.5).

tablet_properties.dissolution_profile: Not specified individually; see Figure 3 for 10% coating level group.

experimental_data.key_results: 067_F1 (1:0, 10%) failed to provide adequate lag time; released drug in pH 6.5.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Not specified for 067_F1

release_properties.release_profile: Qualitative: released in pH 6.5, 6.8, and 7.2 (failed to prevent at pH 6.5).

article_title: Formulation and evaluation of simvastatin gastro retentive drug delivery system

article_overview.title: Formulation and evaluation of simvastatin gastro retentive drug delivery system
article_overview.objective: To develop and optimize a gastro retentive drug delivery system (GRRDS) containing simvastatin as a gastric floating dosage form.

independent_variables: Viscosity grades of HPMC (K4M, K100M), Carbopol and NaCMC
dependent_variables: Kinetics and mechanism of drug release

ingredients_identification.results: Formulation 068_F8 was identified as the optimized batch.

key_peaks_for_simvastatin_cm_minus1.assignment: methyl and methylene C-H asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration
Lactone C=0 and ester C=0 stretch
lactone and ester C-O-C bending vibration

ftir_spectral_measurements.compatibility_conclusion: Drug was compatible with formulation components.

process_parameters.pressure: Adjusted to achieve target hardness (4-8 kg/cm²)

experimental_data.effect_of_hardness_on_blt: As hardness increases, buoyancy lag time increases linearly. Optimum hardness: 4-5 kg/cm².
experimental_data.overall_release_mechanism: Non-Fickian diffusion (anomalous transport) for most batches, Fickian for 068_F7, near zero-order for 068_F8
experimental_data.optimized_batch_release_kinetics: Korsmeyer-Peppas n=0.593, Zero-order r²=0.998, release approaching zero-order
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Data presented as mean ± SD, n=3 for flow properties, buoyancy, and dissolution

data.article_title: Formulation and Evaluation of Sintered Floating Tablets of Cefpodoxime Proxetil

experimental_design.preparation_method: Wet granulation using sublimating agent (camphor), followed by sublimation at 60°C for 3 days and sintering by exposure to acetone vapors for varying durations.
experimental_design.optimization_parameters: Ratio of locust bean gum to drug, camphor concentration, sintering time

ingredients_identification.preparation_principle: Wet granulation with IPA, drying, addition of camphor, compression, sublimation to create porosity, sintering with acetone vapors to crosslink polymer.

ftir_spectral_measurements.method: KBr discs, 10 tons pressure, range 4000-500 cm⁻¹
ftir_spectral_measurements.compatibility: No significant interaction between drug and excipients; characteristic peaks retained after sintering.

compression.process: Wet granulation, sublimation at 60°C for 3 days, sintering in acetone vapor desiccator

evaluation_methods.friability: Roche friabilator, 25 rpm for 4 min, n=10

in_vivo_gastric_retention.model: Rabbit (1.9 kg), dose 321 mg (adjusted with BaSO₄)
in_vivo_gastric_retention.xray_imaging: Tablet retained in stomach for at least 8 hours
in_vivo_gastric_retention.dose_conversion: Based on body surface area (Km human 37, rabbit 12)

antimicrobial_activity.conclusion: Antibacterial activity retained in sintered tablets

stability.changes: No significant change in floating lag time, floating time, drug content, or dissolution profile

data.release_12h_percent: Similar to 069_S2, no significant difference beyond 9 h

general.experimental_design: pH-controlled two-pulse delivery system. Core tablets (216 mg, 200 mg amoxicillin) were compression-coated with barrier layers containing HPMC (100, 400, 4000 cps) and spray-dried lactose (SDL) as pore former in varying ratios. Final two-pulse inlay tablets combined an immediate-release outer layer (300 mg amoxicillin) and the barrier-coated core (200 mg amoxicillin). Batches 070_C1–070_C12 represent the compression-coated tablets (core + barrier layer).

ftir_dsc_summary.ftir: Drug-excipient compatibility confirmed. Characteristic peaks: broad OH peak ~3300 cm⁻¹, C=O (lactam) ~1770 cm⁻¹, aromatic C-H ~1500 cm⁻1.
ftir_dsc_summary.dsc: Endothermic peaks: pure amoxicillin 298.9 °C, formulation (070_C4) 294.4 °C; negligible shift, no interaction.

core_tablet_compression.equipment: 10-station rotary tablet machine (Rimek mini press, Mumbai)

stability_study.results: No significant difference in drug release (95% CI). Drug content 48.14 ± 0.35% (likely for barrier-coated core). Hardness within limits (6.4 ± 0.23 kg/cm²).

general.statistical_analysis: One-way ANOVA with Tukey's post hoc test, confidence limit 95%.

release_properties.release_mechanism: Diffusion through hydrated/swollen HPMC gel, followed by erosion
release_properties.release_profile_summary: Lag time 2–4 h, complete release by 8–10 h (based on 25% HPMC with 75% SDL). See Figure 3 for exact profile.
release_properties.factors_influencing: HPMC viscosity, HPMC:SDL ratio, swelling, fluid penetration

final_two_pulse_tablet.release_profile: Immediate release of 300 mg within 1 h; lag time 2–4 h; second pulse (200 mg) released by 7 h (Figure 4).

article_title: FORMULATION AND IN VITRO EVALUATION OF AMLODIPINE GASTRORETENTIVE FLOATING TABLETS USING A COMBINATION OF HYDROPHILIC AND HYDROPHOBIC POLYMERS

article_info.title: FORMULATION AND IN VITRO EVALUATION OF AMLODIPINE GASTRORETENTIVE FLOATING TABLETS USING A COMBINATION OF HYDROPHILIC AND HYDROPHOBIC POLYMERS
article_info.authors: ANAS T. ALHAMDANY, ALI KHIDHER ABBAS

experimental_design.equipment: Single punch tablet machine (Riva MILUK) using 8 mm set of punch and die
experimental_design.pre_compression_mixing: Sieved over no. 70, mixed uniformly for 10 min, then with magnesium stearate and talc for 2 min
experimental_design.dissolution_apparatus: USP Type II (Paddle method; Copley-USA), 900 ml 0.1 N HCl, 37±1°C, 50 rpm
experimental_design.floating_behavior_test: 1000 ml 0.01 N HCl, USP Type II, 37±0.5°C, 50 rpm

release_profile.071_F4: Delay in drug release for 8 h
release_profile.071_F5: Delay in drug release for 11 h
release_profile.071_F6: Retarded release due to EC concentration
release_profile.071_F7: Retarded release due to EC concentration
release_profile.071_F8: Optimum drug release, combination effect, delayed up to 14 h
release_profile.071_F9: Retarded release due to EC concentration
release_profile.071_F10: Non-significant effect of NaHCO3 on release rate
release_profile.071_F11: Cumulative release significantly lesser than HPMC E50 due to high viscosity

spectral_analysis.ftir: No significant variation in peaks between pure drug, physical mixture, and optimized tablet (071_F8). Key amlodipine peaks: 3415/cm (N-H stretch), 3065/cm (C=C-H aromatic), 1696/cm (C=O), 1615/cm, 1488/cm (C-C ring), 1210/cm (C-O-C). Indicates compatibility.
spectral_analysis.dsc: Pure amlodipine: Sharp endothermic peak at 202.71°C (melting point). HPMC E50 and EC 5 mpas: No peaks, amorphous. Physical mixture and 071_F8: No shift in melting endotherm, indicating no chemical interaction.

process_parameters.compression_device: Single punch tablet machine (Riva MILUK)

experimental_data_summary.total_floating_time: >24 hours for all formulations except 071_F6, 071_F8, 071_F10, 071_F11 which were >14 h? (Table 4 shows >24 for all except contradictory text)

article_info.title: FORMULATION AND IN VITRO EVALUATION OF RAMLETON TABLETS FOR COLON DRUG DELIVERY SYSTEM BY COMPRESSION COATING
article_info.authors: TRINADHA RAO M., PARIMALA Y., YAMINI M., PHANINDRA CVS, SRINIVASA RAO Y.

materials_origin.ramleton: Gift sample from Dr. Reddy's Laboratories, Hyderabad, Andhra Pradesh, India
materials_origin.excipients: Cross carmellose sodium, Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC pH 102), Talc, Magnesium stearate, Ethyl cellulose, Eudragit RLP0, Eudragit L100 – sources not specified

experimental_design.approach: Compression coating technology. Core tablets containing drug and superdisintegrant were prepared, then compression coated with pH‑dependent polymers (ethylcellulose, Eudragit RLP0, Eudragit L100).
experimental_design.batches: Nine formulations (072_F1–072_F9) with varying polymer compositions in the coating layer.
experimental_design.optimization: 072_F4 identified as optimized based on drug release retardation up to 18 h (99.25% release).

ingredients_identification.preparation: Core tablets prepared by direct compression; coating layer applied by compression coating using 9 mm flat punch. Pre‑compression blends tested for flow properties (all passed).
ingredients_identification.results: FTIR showed no interaction between drug and excipients.
ingredients_identification.equation: Release kinetics evaluated using zero‑order, first‑order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer‑Peppas models.
ingredients_identification.absorbance: UV‑Vis analysis at 254 nm (pH 1.2), 256 nm (pH 7.4), and 257 nm (pH 6.8). Calibration curves linear (r² > 0.99).

independent_variables.combinations: Nine batches with single polymer, binary mixtures, or varying amounts

ftir_info.result: No significant interaction observed between ramleton and excipients (spectra shown in Fig. 1 and Fig. 2).

tablet_fabrication.punches: 6 mm flat punch for core; 9 mm flat punch for compression coating
tablet_fabrication.coating_method: Compression coating: half of coating blend placed in die, core tablet centered, remaining blend added and compressed.

general.media: SGF pH 1.2 (2 h) → SIF pH 7.4 (3 h) → SIF pH 6.8 (up to 18 h)

experimental_data.key_results: 072_F4 showed 99.25% drug release at 18 h with first‑order kinetics. All formulations passed quality control tests.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Data expressed as mean±SD (n=3). Correlation coefficients (R²) used for release kinetics.
