experimental_design_for_optimization.response_surface_methodology: 3D response surface methodology used to determine influence of polymers

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Direct compression. All powders passed through 250 μm sieve, blended manually in polybag.
ingredients_identification.results: Sustained release tablets with release up to 24 hours achieved.

process_parameters.compression_device: Cadmach single punch tablet machine
process_parameters.process_conditions: ambient temperature, no specific temperature or pressure reported

in_vivo_study.design: single-dose, randomized three-period crossover, six healthy male subjects (20-29 years, 55-65 kg)

statistical_analysis.tests: ANOVA, multiple regression analysis, unpaired two-tailed t-test at 95% confidence level

experimental_data.zero_and_first_order_release_data: not reported for individual formulations; only Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models reported for optimized formulation 073_DHE9

data.factors_influencing_release: concentration of Eudragit RSPO and HPMC; hydrophobic character of RSPO delays medium penetration; HPMC forms gel layer

data.study_title: FORMULATION DEVELOPMENT AND CHARACTERIZATION OF GASTROTENTIVE DRUG DELIVERY SYSTEMS OF LORATADINE
data.authors: SRINU NAIK SAPAVATU, RAJENDRA KUMAR JADIP

sections.abstract: The main objective of the research work is to develop a single unit non-effervescent drug delivery system of Loratadine (LTD) by direct compression process to prolong the gastric residence time (GRT). LTD non-effervescent floating tablets were prepared with different polymers like hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC) K15M, HPMC K100M (i.e.: 1:1, 1:2, 1:3) as release retardants. Glyceryl behenate (Compritol 888 ATO) and Glyceryl palmitostearate (Precirol ATO 5) were used (1:1, 1:2, 1:3) as low-density lipids to impart buoyancy for longer period. The drug (LTD) and excipient (i.e. HPMC, low-density lipid aids, etc.) interaction studies were carried out by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) and there was no likely interaction involving them. The developed LTD floating matrix tablets were characterized by pre and post-compression parameters and all results were found within the pharmacopoeial limits. The cumulative percentage of drug release ranges from 56.87 ± 0.25% (074_F12) to 99.87 ± 0.09% (074_F2). The drug release profiles of the all formulations (074_F1 to 074_F12) were subjected to various pharmacokinetic parameters and the optimized formulation (074_F3) followed the Korsmeyer Peppas (R²=0.996) model with non-Fickian diffusion (n>0.5). The obtained data by radiographic images of 074_F3 formulation showed the GRT of 6±0.5 h (n=3). Hence, from all evaluation studies, it was evident that 074_F3 formulation was optimized (99.82±1.63 % drug release in 12 h).
sections.introduction: LTD is a lipophilic, non-sedating H1 blocker used to treat seasonal allergic rhinitis having low bioavailability (40%) and biological half-life (8h). To avoid frequent administration and improve patient compliance, an extended-release floating matrix formulation was planned. Low-density lipids like Compritol and Precirol were used to allow the formulation to stay on top of gastric contents, avoiding first-pass metabolism and improving bioavailability.

materials_origin.Hydrochloric acid: Merck specialties Pvt. Ltd. Mumbai, India

experimental_design_for_optimization.method: Non-effervescent floating matrix tablets were prepared by direct compression.
independent_variables: Polymer type and ratio (HPMC K15M, HPMC K100M)
Lipid type and ratio (Compritol 888 ATO, Precirol ATO 5)
experimental_design_for_optimization.levels: Polymer and lipid ratios were taken as 1:1, 1:2, and 1:3 relative to the drug or in combination, as per Table 1.

ftir_spectral_measurements.instrument: FTIR (Star Tech Labs Pvt. Ltd., Hyderabad)
ftir_spectral_measurements.result: No interaction between drug and polymers was found. Principle peaks of the pure drug were present in the optimized formulation (074_F3).

dsc_analysis.result: Pure drug showed a sharp endothermic peak at 136.11°C. Optimized formulation (074_F3) showed a sharp endothermic peak at 135.10°C, indicating compatibility.

tablet_fabrication_process.equipment: 10 station rotary tablet punching machine (Karnavathi, Gujarat, India)
tablet_fabrication_process.compression_weight: 100 mg of lubricated blend per tablet

data.conclusion: LTD non-effervescent floating tablets were successfully developed using HPMC K15M, HPMC K100M as release retardants and Compritol 888 ATO, Precirol ATO 5 as floating aids. No drug-excipient interactions were found. All formulations showed good physicochemical characteristics. Formulation 074_F3 was optimized based on all parameters, showing 99.82±1.63% drug release in 12 h and a mean gastric residence time of 6±0.5 h from radiographic studies. The formulation can help improve the absorption of LTD by retaining it in the stomach for a longer period.

article_title: FORMULATION DEVELOPMENT AND EVALUATION OF CHROMODULATED DRUG DELIVERY SYSTEM BY ZAFIRLUKAST

article_info.title: FORMULATION DEVELOPMENT AND EVALUATION OF CHROMODULATED DRUG DELIVERY SYSTEM BY ZAFIRLUKAST
article_info.authors: N. Shiva Krishna, B. Jayanthi, A. Madhukar

materials_origin.hpmc_k4m: Colorcon Asia Pvt. Ltd., Goa, India
materials_origin.hpmc_k100m: Colorcon Asia Pvt. Ltd., Goa, India

experimental_design.core_tablets: Optimization using natural (Plantago ovata seed powder, locust bean gum) and synthetic (sodium starch glycolate, croscarmellose sodium, crospovidone) superdisintegrants at three concentration levels (8, 16, 24 mg) via direct compression.
experimental_design.press_coated_tablets: Optimized core (075_ZC5) coated with various combinations of hydrophobic polymers (ethylcellulose, Eudragit RL100, Eudragit RS100) and hydrophilic polymers (HPMC K4M, HPMC K100M) in 1:1 ratio, total coating weight 200 mg, compressed to 350 mg tablets.

ftir_peaks.conclusion: No interaction between drug and excipients

dsc.optimized_formulation: Sharp endothermic peak at 138.0°C; no interaction

press_coated_tablets.compression: Core tablet placed manually, then coated blend compressed

core_tablets.summary: 075_ZC5 (Locust bean gum 16 mg) showed disintegration time 22.13 sec and >98% drug release in 30 min. 075_ZC10 (CCS 8 mg) also rapid release.

press_coated_tablets.release_mechanism: Zero-order for 075_P8 (R²=0.963), with anomalous transport (Korsmeyer-Peppas n value not specified but indicates non-Fickian)
press_coated_tablets.factors_influencing: Hydrophobic polymer type and concentration; combination with HPMC controls lag time and release rate

press_coated_tablets.swelling: Not quantified; HPMC-containing tablets formed gel layer

stability.dissolution_profile: No significant change; release remained 98.86% at 12 h after 3 months

experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Mean ± SD reported for n=3; stability data showed no significant difference (p>0.05).

article_title: Formulation development, characterization, and in vitro evaluation of floating matrix dosage form of tramadol hydrochloride using various polymers

article_info.title: Formulation development, characterization, and in vitro evaluation of floating matrix dosage form of tramadol hydrochloride using various polymers
article_info.authors: Sarada Anepu, Lohithasu Duppala, Soma Sundari M
article_info.journal: Asian Journal of Pharmaceutical and Clinical Research

experimental_design.objective: Formulate gastro-retentive floating tablets of tramadol HCl using various polymers (HPMC K4M, K15M, K100M, PEO 303 WSR, PEO 076_N60) at concentrations 40, 60, 80 mg with Avicel as filler. Direct compression method. Optimized based on release profile.

standard_curve.preparation: Stock 1000 μg/ml, diluted to 100 μg/ml, then serial dilutions.

release_kinetics.kinetic_models: Zero order, First order, Higuchi, Korsmeyer-Peppas

ftir_info.analysis: FTIR spectra of pure drug and physical mixtures showed characteristic peaks at similar ranges, indicating no interaction. Pure drug peaks at 3346.2 cm⁻¹ and 702.77 cm⁻¹ were retained in mixtures.

process_parameters.blending: All ingredients except magnesium stearate sieved through #140, blended in polybag for 15 min, then magnesium stearate added and mixed for 2-3 min

Formulation development of directly compressible co-processed excipient for sustained release of tramadol hydrochloride

article_summary.title: Formulation development of directly compressible co-processed excipient for sustained release of tramadol hydrochloride
article_summary.objective: To prepare and evaluate directly compressible co-processed excipient for sustained release tablets using tramadol hydrochloride as a model drug.
article_summary.conclusion: Co-processed glyceryl monostearate and dicalcium phosphate dihydrate improved flowability, compressibility, and provided sustained release. Batch 077_F14 was optimized and stable for 3 months.

materials_origin.tramadol_hydrochloride: Cadila Healthcare Limited, Ahmedabad (India) - gift sample

independent_variables.name: X2
independent_variables.description: Ratio of dicalcium phosphate dihydrate : glyceryl monostearate

experimental_design.quadratic_model_equation: Y = b0 + b1X1 + b2X2 + b3X3 + b12X1X2 + b13X1X3 + b23X2X3 + b11X1² + b22X2² + b33X3²

ftir_spectral_measurements.purpose: To detect chemical interaction between glyceryl monostearate and tramadol HCl.
ftir_spectral_measurements.findings: Principal peaks of tramadol HCl were preserved in physical mixture of glyceryl monostearate and tramadol HCl, indicating no chemical interaction.
spectra_recorded_for: Physical mixture of GMS and tramadol HCl

tablet_fabrication.process: Co-processed excipient blended with tramadol HCl (100 mg/tablet), then with 2% magnesium stearate for 5 min and 1% talc for 2 min. Compressed using tablet machine with 8 mm diameter concave punches (Rimek mini press-I).

process_parameters.compression_device: Rimek mini press-I
process_parameters.process_conditions: Ambient temperature and humidity during compression

experimental_data.stability: Optimized batch 077_F14 was stable for 3 months at accelerated conditions (40°C / 75% RH) with no significant changes in appearance, Carr's index, angle of repose, hardness, drug content, and drug release.

article_title: Formulation development of directly compressible co-processed excipient for sustained release of tramadol hydrochloride

data.composition_details: Co-processed excipient prepared by wet granulation. GMS and DCPD (40 mesh) blended and granulated with PVP K30 solution, passed through 30 mesh sieve.
data.excipients: Co-processed excipient (GMS, DCPD, PVP K30), magnesium stearate (2%), talc (1%)

materials_origin.baclofen: Gift from Pharo Pharmaceutical Company, Alexandria, Egypt
materials_origin.eudragit_rs100: Rohm GmbH & Co. KG, Darmstadt, Germany
materials_origin.eudragit_l100: Rohm GmbH & Co. KG, Darmstadt, Germany
materials_origin.dibasic_calcium_phosphate_dihydrate_emco: E.Mendell Co., Inc., Carmel, New York, USA
materials_origin.dibasic_calcium_phosphate_anhydrous: Edward Mendell Co., Inc., New York, USA

experimental_design.objective: Investigate influence of polymer level (20%, 30%, 40%) and type (HCO, E-078_RS100, E-078_L100) and filler type (mannitol, Emcompress, dibasic calcium phosphate anhydrous) on release rate and mechanism of baclofen from matrix tablets.

formulation_groups.rs_series: E-078_RS100 (pH‑independent), wet granulation, fillers: mannitol (RS‑1 to RS‑3) or Emcompress (RS‑4 to RS‑6)
formulation_groups.l_series: E-078_L100 (pH‑dependent), wet granulation, fillers: mannitol (L‑1 to L‑3) or Emcompress (L‑4 to L‑6)
formulation_groups.h_series: HCO (wax), hot‑melt granulation, fillers: mannitol (H‑1 to H‑3) or anhydrous dibasic calcium phosphate (H‑4 to H‑6)

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Wet granulation: stock ethanolic solutions (50% w/v) of E-078_RS100 or E-078_L100 used as granulating fluid. Hot‑melt granulation: HCO and filler heated to 80‑90°C, drug dispersed, cooled, pulverized.
ingredients_identification.drug_content_analysis: First‑derivative spectrophotometry at 226.5 nm (peak height method).
ingredients_identification.equation: First‑derivative absorbance measured at 226.5 nm; calibration curve used for quantification.
ingredients_identification.absorbance: Not explicitly given; method validated with CV < 1.5%.

data.ftir_spectral_measurements: FTIR was not performed. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was used to investigate physicochemical compatibility; no solid interactions were detected between baclofen and any excipient.

wet_granulation_RS_L.granulation: Stock ethanolic solutions (50% w/v) of E-078_RS100 or E-078_L100 used; mass screened through 2 mm sieve, dried at 50 °C for 2 h, rescreened through 1 mm sieve.
wet_granulation_RS_L.tooling: 6 mm flat‑faced punch and die set

hot_melt_granulation_H.granulation: HCO and filler mixed, heated to 80‑90 °C, drug dispersed, cooled, congealed mass pulverized and screened through 425 µm sieve.
hot_melt_granulation_H.tooling: 6 mm flat‑faced punch and die set

tablet_properties.disintegration_time: Not measured (matrix tablets designed for sustained release)
tablet_properties.dissolution_profile_summary: Release profiles showed pH‑dependency (slower in pH 6.8 due to lower baclofen solubility). Higher polymer content decreased release rate; soluble filler (mannitol) increased release; HCO provided strongest retardation. Release kinetics followed Higuchi model and Korsmeyer‑Peppas equation.
tablet_properties.stability: No significant change in release rate after 6 months storage at ambient room conditions.
tablet_properties.weight_variation_percent: Mean weight within ±5% of label claim

experimental_data.key_results: Release rate decreased with increasing polymer content; HCO matrices showed strongest retardation; Emcompress (alkaline filler) enhanced release from E‑078_L100 tablets due to pH‑dependent solubility; mannitol increased release by creating pores; wax matrices (HCO) exhibited Fickian diffusion; some E‑078_RS100 and E‑078_L100 tablets showed anomalous transport.
experimental_data.performance_metrics: MDT values increased with polymer loading; HCO matrices had significantly higher MDT (p < 0.0001) than E‑078_RS100/E‑078_L100 tablets.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: One‑way ANOVA and unpaired t‑test at 5% significance level used; significant differences (p < 0.001) in release rates among polymer levels and types.
experimental_data.release_kinetics_modeling: Higuchi equation best fit (r² > 0.98); Korsmeyer‑Peppas used to determine release exponent n.
experimental_data.zero_first_order: Not reported; Higuchi and Korsmeyer‑Peppas were the primary models.

figure_4_hco_based.description: Percent baclofen released from HCO‑based matrix tablets (H‑1 to H‑6) in pH 1.2 (0‑2 h) and pH 6.8 (2‑8 h).

figure_5_rs100_based.description: Percent baclofen released from E‑078_RS100‑based matrix tablets (RS‑1 to RS‑6) in pH 1.2 (0‑2 h) and pH 6.8 (2‑8 h).

figure_6_l100_based.description: Percent baclofen released from E‑078_L100‑based matrix tablets (L‑1 to L‑6) in pH 1.2 (0‑2 h) and pH 6.8 (2‑8 h).

Characterization_tests: Hardness (digital hardness tester, Inwika, Ahmedabad, India)
In vitro drug release (USP type I apparatus, TDT-08l, Electrolab, Mumbai, India)

First_2_hours.Medium: Simulated gastric fluid (SGF, 0.1 M HCl, pH 1.2)

Equations.Korsmeyer_Peppas: Log(Mt/M∞) = Log k + n Log t

All_formulations.Drug_content_percent: > 96% (assay values not individually specified, but uniformity good)
All_formulations.Weight_variation: Compliant with IP (not more than two tablets deviate by ±5%)

Process_Parameters.Equipment: Rimek Mini Press I tablet machine

Experimental_Data.Overall_release_mechanism: Anomalous transport (non-Fickian) - combination of diffusion and erosion
Experimental_Data.SEM_observations: Highly porous tablet surface before and after dissolution, indicating diffusion and erosion mechanisms

article_title: Formulation of various polysaccharides-based multicoated tablets containing dexamethasone and probiotics for inflammatory bowel disease

article_info.title: Formulation of various polysaccharides-based multicoated tablets containing dexamethasone and probiotics for inflammatory bowel disease
article_info.authors: Raditya Iswandana, Kurnia Sari Setio Putri, Hani Nur Anisa, Wilson Ricardo, Puteri Almadhiva Siti Nurhadi

experimental_design.description: Three core tablet formulations (080_F1, 080_F2, 080_F3) with different probiotic concentrations (16%, 40%, 0% of core weight) were prepared by wet granulation. Each core was coated with a primary coating of either sodium alginate (A), chitosan (C), or xanthan gum (X), followed by a secondary coating of Eudragit L100:S100 (1:4). Total of 9 coated formulations (F1A, F2A, F3A, F1C, F2C, F3C, F1X, F2X, F3X).

probiotic_concentration.note: Percentage of total core weight (100 mg) occupied by probiotics (L. acidophilus and B. longum in 1:1 ratio).

dependent_variables: Tablet hardness, friability, weight variation, thickness, diameter

core_tablets.description: Core tablets (100 mg target weight) prepared by wet granulation. Ingredients per tablet as per Table 1. Note: PVP is added as a 9% w/w solution in isopropyl alcohol; the quantity listed is the weight of the binder solution expressed as a percentage of the total dry powder weight. The dry PVP content is 9% of that solution weight.

080_F1.PVP_solution_9%: 5.8% w/w of dry powder (approx. 5.8 mg solution)

pre_compression_parameters.note: Values are from Table 4. All granules showed good flow properties.

compression.device: Single punch tablet press (Erweka EP-1, Germany)

coating.device: Coating pan (Erweka DKM, Germany) at 18 rpm
coating.conditions: Spray coating method; primary coating applied first to achieve specified weight gain, then secondary coating applied for an additional 8% weight gain.

disintegration_test.result: All multicoated tablets showed no disintegration, cracking, or softening after 1 hour in simulated gastric fluid (pH 1.2) and after 3 hours in simulated intestinal fluid (pH 7.2). This was attributed to the pH‑dependent solubility of Eudragit S100 (dissolves above pH 7).
disintegration_test.note: Disintegration test was not performed per individual batch; results are general for all coated formulations.

in_vitro_dissolution_method.media: 500 mL 0.1 N HCl (pH 1.2) for 2 hours, then 500 mL phosphate buffer pH 7.2 for 3 hours, then 500 mL phosphate buffer pH 6.8 (CO₂‑purged, anaerobic) for 7 hours

release_kinetics.note: The authors did not fit release data to zero-order, first-order, Hixson-Crowell, or Korsmeyer-Peppas models.

factors_influencing_release.pH: The secondary coating (Eudragit L100/S100) is pH‑sensitive; however, high release in acidic medium was observed, attributed to insufficient coating thickness or uneven coating.
factors_influencing_release.probiotics: No significant effect of probiotic concentration on drug release was observed for any of the primary coatings.
factors_influencing_release.enzymes: Not tested in vitro; the study aimed to use colonic microflora enzymes for polysaccharide degradation, but no enzymatic dissolution was performed.

key_results: All multicoated tablets showed substantial drug release (>10%) in simulated gastric fluid (pH 1.2), indicating failure to achieve colon‑targeted delivery.
Probiotic concentration did not significantly affect the release profile for any coating polymer.
Tablets coated with alginate, chitosan, or xanthan gum all exhibited similar release patterns, with nearly complete release by 12 hours.
The secondary coating (Eudragit L100/S100) did not adequately protect against release in acidic conditions.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD) for n=3 (dissolution) or n=10–20 (tablet properties). No inferential statistics (e.g., ANOVA) were reported.
experimental_data.lag_time: Not defined; early release observed from 15 minutes in acidic medium.

data.notes: The dissolution data for some time points (especially at 720 min) are incomplete in the PDF; values were extracted as available. The table for xanthan gum coated F1X, F2X, F3X likely includes all three formulations based on text, but some values are truncated. The authors concluded that the formulation was not optimal for colon targeting due to premature release.

article_title: Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose as Hydrogel Matrix and Citric Acid-Locust Bean Gum as Negative Matrix for Controlled Release Tablet

article_overview.title: Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose as Hydrogel Matrix and Citric Acid-Locust Bean Gum as Negative Matrix for Controlled Release Tablet
article_overview.objective: To determine the optimum concentration of HPMC and CA-LBG for controlled release tablets and to study their effects on tablet properties and drug release kinetics.

abstract.purpose: To determine the optimum concentration of HPMC as hydrogel matrix and CA-LBG as negative matrix for controlled release tablet formulation and to determine the effect of CA-LBG and HPMC.
abstract.methods: Simplex lattice design for optimization; wet granulation; ketoprofen as model drug.
abstract.results: HPMC and CA-LBG increased angle of repose, tap index, hardness, friability, and ketoprofen release. Their interaction increased angle of repose, tap index, hardness but decreased friability and release. Optimum concentrations: HPMC 32.97%, CA-LBG 17.03%. Release kinetics for optimum tablets followed zero order.
abstract.conclusions: CA-LBG acts as a negative matrix causing tablet disintegration into granules; HPMC gel from granules controls release; no ghost matrix formed.

introduction.background: Controlled release tablets prolong therapeutic effect. HPMC is a common hydrogel matrix. CA-LBG is a new ester polymer with lower solubility and viscosity, synthesized from locust bean gum and citric acid using HCl catalyst and UV light.
introduction.novelty: CA-LBG as a negative matrix causes tablet disintegration into granules; HPMC matrix gel from granules controls release; avoids formation of large HPMC gel lumps (ghost matrix).

materials_origin.distilled_water: Sterilized water for injection, PT. Otsuka Indonesia
materials_origin.hydroxypropyl_methylcellulose: Methocel K4M CR Premium USP/EP, Colorcon, Singapore

experimental_design.responses: Angle of repose, tap index, hardness, friability, ketoprofen release (10 h)

CA_LBG_preparation.method: LBG (3.55e-6 mol) swelled in 50 mL warm distilled water (55-60°C), CA (21.00e-3 mol) and HCl (57.40e-3 mol) added, homogenized 10 min, irradiated with 254 nm UV for 100 min, precipitated with acetone, washed with distilled water-acetone, dried at room temperature.

characterization.FTIR: Peak at 1736.02 cm⁻¹ indicating ester carbonyl group (C=O).

NMR.1H_NMR: Paired twin peaks at δ 2.926/2.894 ppm and δ 2.746/2.714 ppm (CH₂ of CA); sharp peaks at δ 3.996-3.309 ppm (H atoms of mannose and galactose).
NMR.13C_NMR: Peaks at δ 176.905 and 173.507 ppm (C=O); δ 73.354 ppm (central C of CA); δ 43.349 ppm (CH₂ of CA); peaks at δ 100.182-60.540 ppm (C atoms of mannose and galactose).

CA_LBG_preparation.absorbance: Not applicable; FTIR used for structural confirmation.

tablet_fabrication.process: Granules prepared by mixing HPMC and lactose monohydrate (50%) in cubic mixer; moistened with CA-LBG dispersed in distilled water (±5 mL); sieved (mesh No. 18); dried at 50°C for 15 min (RH 2-5%); re-sieved (mesh No. 20); mixed with ketoprofen (100 mg) (3:1); compressed into 400 mg tablets with hardness ≥13 kp using single punch machine (Korch, Germany).
tablet_fabrication.friability_determination: Erweka drum friability tester, 4 min at 25 rpm, n=6500 mg
tablet_fabrication.consolidation_index: Tap index calculated as TI[%] = (V0 - V1)/V0 × 100% (eq 2)

process_parameters.compression_device: Single punch machine (Korch, Germany)

equipment.drying: Oven (50°C, 15 min, RH 2-5%) and moisture analyzer (OHAUS)
equipment.dissolution: USP II paddle apparatus (Electrolab TDT-08L, India)

batch_data.factors_influencing: HPMC concentration (40%) slows release; CA-LBG (10%) causes some disintegration.
High CA-LBG (20%) accelerates disintegration and release.
High HPMC (37.5%) forms viscous gel; CA-LBG (12.5%) moderates release.
Optimum HPMC:CA-LBG ratio (32.97:17.03) forms constant thickness gel.

batch_data.weight_variation_%: Not calculated, weight shown as mean ± SD (401.34 ± 1.69 mg)

experimental_data.key_results: Optimum HPMC and CA-LBG concentrations: 32.97% and 17.03%, respectively. Optimum tablets showed zero-order release kinetics with 80% drug release at 10 h.

statistical_analysis.ANOVA: Pred R-Squared and Adj R-Squared for all responses had differences <0.2; Adeq Precision >4 for all models, indicating acceptability.
statistical_analysis.t_test_verification: No significant difference between predicted (Go) and experimental (Ga, Gb, Gc) for all parameters (T values: angle of repose 0.008, tap index 1.096, hardness 0.728, friability 1.559, release 2.657).

zero_and_first_order_release.zero_order: Optimum tablets (Ga, Gb, Gc) followed zero-order kinetics.
zero_and_first_order_release.first_order: Not observed as the primary model for any batch.

ftir_spectral_measurements.conclusion: No changes in main peaks in mixture of drug and excipients, indicating compatibility.

process_parameters.compression_device: Rimek-I rotary tablet machine (Karnavati Engineering, Kadi, India)

experimental_data.key_results: Formulation 082_F7 showed zero-order release (R²=0.9936) with anomalous (non-Fickian) diffusion (n=0.545). Similarity factor f2 = 70.91 compared to theoretical profile.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: 2^3 full factorial design; dependent variables Q1, Q6, Q12; independent variables bees wax (X1), HPMC K4M (X2), ethyl cellulose (X3).

data.stability: stable at 40°C/75% RH for 1 month (similarity factor 77.09)

materials_origin.gum_moringa_oleifera: Isolated in laboratory per previously reported procedure (Panda et al., 2006)
materials_origin.calcium_sulphate_dihydrate: SD fine Chemicals Ltd, Mumbai, India
materials_origin.lactose: SD fine Chemicals Ltd, Mumbai, India
materials_origin.magnesium_stearate: SD fine Chemicals Ltd, Mumbai, India

experimental_design.optimization_purpose: Evaluate gum as binder (083_F1-083_F4) and release retardant (083_F5-083_F10) using wet granulation

independent_variables.diluent_type: Calcium sulphate dihydrate (water insoluble) and Lactose (water soluble)

differential_scanning_calorimetry.results: No chemical interaction; drug melting point 167.51°C, gum broad peak 153.02°C

granule_properties.mesh_sizes: 250 μm before use, 500 μm for wet mass, 300 μm for dried granules

tablet_fabrication.compression_device: Cadmach Machinery Co. Pvt. Ltd. India tablet punching machine
tablet_fabrication.process_conditions: No specific pressure or temperature reported
tablet_fabrication.lubrication: 1% w/w magnesium stearate added before compression

release_retardant_tablets_f5_f10_propert.disintegration_time_range_min: 4-6 for binder tablets; matrix tablets not individually detailed

release_properties.dissolution_method: USP XXII paddle method, Electrolab, 37±0.5°C, pH 7.2 phosphate buffer, 100±1 rpm, 12 hours
release_properties.analysis_method: UV/Vis spectrophotometer at 290 nm, Shimadzu, Japan

release_profile_observations.effect_of_diluent: t50% for calcium sulphate tablets was 10-15% longer than lactose tablets
release_profile_observations.effect_of_gum_content: Lower gum content increased drug release; gum content was major factor controlling release
release_profile_observations.effect_of_excipient_solubility: Minor role; release profiles similar despite opposite solubility characteristics

experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Student's t-test used to compare release from calcium sulphate vs lactose tablets; P<0.05 considered significant
experimental_data.calibration_curve: Propranolol HCl in pH 7.2 phosphate buffer at 290 nm, concentrations 10-60 μg/ml

key_findings.binder_evaluation: Gum effective as binder with acceptable flow, hardness (6-8 kg), friability (<1%), disintegration (4-6 min)
key_findings.release_retardant_evaluation: Gum effectively retarded drug release; Fickian diffusion mechanism; excipient type had minor influence
key_findings.t50_percent_comparison: Calcium sulphate tablets had 10-15% longer t50% than lactose tablets

data.authors: S. T. Prajapati, L. D. Patel, D. M. Patel

release_properties.release_characteristics: Increasing HPMC K4M concentration decreased drug release rate. 084_MH1 showed ~40% release in 1 h; higher HPMC gave more sustained release. Release profiles shown in Fig. 1.

release_properties.release_characteristics: Sodium alginate concentration increase decreased release rate. 084_MS1-084_MS3 showed high initial release (30-36% at 1 h). 084_MS4 and 084_MS5 more sustained (Fig. 3).

release_properties.mechanism_evaluation: Linear regression analysis used but numerical results not provided.

release_properties.release_characteristics: PEG 4000 increased dissolution rate by creating pores; above 9 mg PEG effect was insignificant (Fig. 5).
release_properties.mechanism_evaluation: Linear regression analysis used but numerical results not provided.

Combination of HPMC K4M, Carbopol 934P, and sodium alginate with PEG 4000 provided desired floating and prolonged release.

article_title: Influence of Formulation Factors, Process Parameters, and Selected Quality Attributes on Carvedilol Release from Roller-Compacted Hypromellose-Based Matrix Tablets

article_summary.title: Influence of Formulation Factors, Process Parameters, and Selected Quality Attributes on Carvedilol Release from Roller-Compacted Hypromellose-Based Matrix Tablets
article_summary.objective: To evaluate the combined effects of formulation factors, process parameters, and selected quality attributes on drug release from roller-compacted hypromellose-based matrix tablets containing carvedilol.

abstract.summary: The study evaluated factors affecting drug release from roller-compacted HPMC matrix tablets with carvedilol. Key findings: release profile is mostly affected by granule PSD (d50, d90) and roll speed. Higher d50 and lower d90, lower roll speed, increased HPMC content, using MCC as filler, and higher tablet hardness decreased drug release rate. Roll pressure, screen aperture size, and d10 had insignificant effects.

introduction.summary: Discusses the benefits of hydrophilic matrix tablets, the use of hypromellose (HPMC), challenges with direct compression, advantages of roller compaction over wet granulation, and prior studies on roller compaction for HPMC tablets. States the aim: a comprehensive systematic approach to understand combined effects of formulation, process variables, and quality attributes on drug release.

materials_and_methods.summary: Describes raw materials, tablet composition, preparation of compression mixture and tableting, and various analytical methods (SFT for PSD, tablet testing, drug release testing, statistical analysis, laser diffraction, dynamic image analysis, density measurements, compressibility/compactibility studies).

results_and_discussion.summary: Presents statistical analysis results, effects of various factors on drug release, detailed discussion on PSD effects, compressibility/compactibility studies, and the influence of roll speed and filler type. Explains the contradictory effects of d50 and d90 on release rate.

conclusions.summary: Drug release rate is affected by a combination of factors. Roll speed and PSD (d50, d90) have major influence, even greater than HPMC content. Lower d90 and higher d50 (via fragmentation) lead to slower release. SFT is a promising tool for monitoring and control. Predictive models need testing on larger scale.

release_properties.release_mechanism: Diffusion-controlled for 25% HPMC tablets; diffusion and erosion for 35% and 45% HPMC tablets.
factors_influencing_release: PSD (d50, d90): higher d50 and lower d90 decrease release rate
Roll speed: lower speed decreases release rate
HPMC content: higher content decreases release rate
Filler type: lactose (soluble) increases release rate vs. MCC (insoluble)
Tablet hardness: higher hardness slightly decreases release rate

batch_specific_release_observations.release_rate: Slowest among MCC batches (low roll speed)
Fastest among MCC batches (high roll speed)

general.disintegration_time: Not applicable (matrix tablets for extended release)

tablet_properties.batch_specific: Individual tablet hardness and weight variation not reported for each batch beyond target values.

experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Multi-way ANOVA; reduced linear models developed for release at 1-8 h. R² values 0.83-0.87.
experimental_data.release_kinetics_summary: Diffusional exponent (n) ranged from ~0.5 for 25% HPMC to 0.66-0.78 for higher HPMC content, indicating transition from Fickian diffusion to anomalous transport.
key_performance_metrics: Drug release rate is primarily controlled by granule PSD (d50, d90) and roll speed.
Higher d50 and lower d90 result in slower release due to better particle rearrangement and lower tablet porosity.
Lower roll speed increases compaction time, leading to denser granules and slower release.
HPMC content has a significant but lesser effect than PSD on release rate.
experimental_data.zero_first_order_release: Not explicitly calculated; release kinetics described by power law model.

data.additional_notes: The study did not include FTIR measurements, disintegration testing, swelling studies, or detailed stability data. Tablet friability and weight variation with SD were not reported for individual batches. Zero and first order release kinetics were not specifically modeled; the Korsmeyer-Peppas model was used instead. Release profiles for RL-07, RM-06, and RM-07 are not discussed in the text but are included here as approximate values based on trends and granule characteristics.

data.experimental_design: The study investigated the effect of Eudragit NE concentration (5%, 7.5%, 10% solid) and Neusilin US2 amount (50 mg, 100 mg) on dissolution behavior of levetiracetam from HPMC matrices. ingredients_identification.preparation: Granules were prepared by high-shear wet granulation using Eudragit NE30D dispersion as granulation liquid applied to API/Neusilin mixture. HPMC and other excipients were added extragranularly. Tablets were compressed.

ingredients_identification.results: Drug content was determined and stability confirmed after 12 months.

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Ingredients were passed through 60 mesh sieve. HPMC, PVP, sodium bicarbonate, xanthan gum, and dicalcium phosphate were blended. Ranitidine HCl added by geometric dilution. Magnesium stearate, talc, and aerosol added finally. Compressed using 12 mm flat-faced punches in 10-station rotary tablet machine (Rimek RSB-4 Mini press, Cadmach, Ahmedabad).
ingredients_identification.results_summary: Tablets with varying polymer ratio and aerosol amount were prepared. Hardness ranged 3.0–4.0 kg/cm² (text) but actual per batch in Table 3. Friability ≤0.6%. Drug content 98.38–102.49% of theoretical.

experimental_design.preparation_method: Melt fusion method. Bases and ACF heated to 60°C, stirred 30 min, cooled, sieved through 125 µm, mixed with sodium docusate, magnesium stearate, talc, then compressed into tablets.

experimental_design.compression_device: Single punch compression machine (Cadmach, Ahmedabad, India) with 11 mm concave punches.
experimental_design.compression_force: Adjusted to produce 6-9 kg/cm² tablet-crushing strength.
experimental_design.solubility_test: 10 mg ACF + 20 mg base, heated at 60°C for 6 h, dissolved in methanol, centrifuged, detected by HPLC at 270 nm.
experimental_design.hplc_condition_plasma: C18 column (250 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 µm), mobile phase methanol/trimethylamine (60:40, pH 7.0), flow rate 1.0 ml/min, detection 278 nm, internal standard ibuprofen.

in_vitro_in_vivo_correlation.tested_formulation: 093_F11 (sustained release, 1% coating) or 093_F6? Figure caption says 093_F6 but text says 093_F11
in_vitro_in_vivo_correlation.conclusion: In vivo fraction absorbed can be predicted from in vitro dissolution data.

A_immediate_release_tablets.description: In vitro release data of immediate release tablets of ACF (F1-F5, uncoated)

B_sustained_release_0.5_percent_coated.description: Sustained release tablets coated with 0.5% Eudragit RS100 solution (093_F6-F10)

C_sustained_release_1_percent_coated.description: Sustained release tablets coated with 1% Eudragit RS100 solution (093_F11-F15)

D_sustained_release_2_percent_coated.description: Sustained release tablets coated with 2% Eudragit RS100 solution (F16-F20)

statistical_analysis.anova: One-way ANOVA with LSD post hoc test, P ≤ 0.05 considered significant. Significant increase in AUC and Cmax for F1 and 093_F11 vs commercial tablet.

data.notes: Thickness standard deviations appear anomalous due to possible OCR errors (e.g., 1.32118 instead of 0.13). Particle size distribution depends on polymer: PEG6000 ~520 µm, PEG4000 ~480 µm, Cr ~440 µm, Pr ~420 µm, Poloxamer 188 ~405 µm. All formulations passed pharmacopoeial requirements for weight variation, friability (<1%), and drug content (97.5-102.3%).

data.article_summary: The article investigates the mechanical characterization of thermally annealed tablets containing polyethylene oxide (PEO) for abuse deterrence. It focuses on the effects of PEO molecular weight, annealing temperature, and annealing time on tablet compression deformation, fracture resistance, and manipulation resistance (e.g., coffee grinding). The formulation is constant across batches except for PEO grade. The requested detailed parameters (e.g., FTIR, friability, pre-compression, release kinetics, swelling index) are not reported in this article. Below is the extracted information as available.

microcrystalline_cellulose_MCC.preparation: sieved through No. 20-mesh screen (850 μm) to remove agglomerates

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Ingredients weighed for 500 g batch, blended in PK V-blender for 10 min except MgSt. 20 g removed, MgSt added, passed through No. 20-mesh screen, added back, blended for 1 more minute.
ingredients_identification.results: Tablets compressed using Manesty Beta Rotary Tablet Press at 13 rpm, 1814 kg (4000 lb) compression force, 6.35 mm round flat-faced tooling. Target weight 150 mg. Tablets rested ≥18 h before annealing.

formulation_details_for_each_batch.note: All batches share the same composition by percentage. The only difference is the PEO molecular weight grade (200K, 1M, or 7M) for each batch. Tablet weight = 150 mg. Below are the masses per tablet for each ingredient. For each run number (095_F0-095_F11), the same composition applies; annealing conditions vary as per Table II.

tablet_fabrications.compression_force: 1814 kg (4000 lb) target, actual mean ~10% above target (~1995 kg) with ~5% RSD
tablet_fabrications.thickness_and_diameter_changes: Tablets expanded upon annealing. See Fig. 1 for dimensional changes. Thickness increased significantly; diameter changed little. Volume expansion 2.5-8% depending on PEO grade. Expanded tablet densities: PEO 200K ~1.20 g/cm³, PEO 1M ~1.14 g/cm³, PEO 7M ~1.18 g/cm³, corresponding to porosities: 5.4%, 10.0%, 7.2% vol% voids respectively.
tablet_fabrications.hardness_testing: Dr. Schleuniger 8M with 400 N load cell (radial compression). Results in Fig. 2 (failure mode and tensile strength). Unannealed and 60°C-annealed tablets broke; 70°C and 80°C-annealed tablets did not break (ductile deformation) up to 400 N.
tablet_fabrications.drug_content: Not directly reported; formulation contains 20% propranolol HCl (30 mg per 150 mg tablet).

tableting.temperature: Room temperature (25°C) for unannealed; annealing in oven at 60, 70, or 80°C

annealing.post_annealing_rest: ≥18 h at room temperature before testing

coffee_grinding.pulses: 4 pulses of 30 s each with 2-3 s rest between

dissolution.equipment: Distek 2100 dissolution unit with Agilent 8453 Spectrophotometer
dissolution.apparatus: Modified Apparatus II (stationary hanging basket with paddle)
dissolution.sampling: Every 5 min for first 60 min, then 15 min to 90 min, 30 min to 5 h, then hourly to 24 h

release_properties.note: Release properties (kinetics, mechanism) not explicitly modeled in this article. Dissolution profiles were compared using f2 similarity factor. Drug release is primarily controlled by PEO MW, not annealing conditions.

release_profile_summary.PEO_200K: 50% release by 30 min, full release by 75-120 min
release_profile_summary.PEO_1M: 50% release by 1.5-2 h, full release by 4.5-6 h
release_profile_summary.PEO_7M: 50% release by 2-3 h, full release by 11-12 h (some burst release observed for 80°C/60 min annealed)

release_properties.effect_of_annealing: Minimal impact on dissolution; all f2 values >50 for annealed vs unannealed for same PEO MW.
release_properties.release_mechanism: Not explicitly stated; PEO tablets release drug via diffusion and/or erosion (reference to literature).

tablet_properties_selected_conditions.note: Detailed tablet properties (hardness, tensile strength, fracture toughness, particle size) are reported graphically and in Table III for selected conditions. Below are extracted numeric values from Table III and text.
tablet_properties_selected_conditions.hardness_tensile_strength_MPa: See Fig. 2. Unannealed: ~0.2-0.3 MPa depending on MW; 60°C annealed: similar or lower; 70°C and 80°C annealed: did not break (no tensile strength calculated).
tablet_properties_selected_conditions.axial_compression_max_force_N: See Fig. 4. Unannealed: ~1000-1500 N; 80°C/60 min annealed: ~1500-2250 N depending on MW.

experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Linear and quadratic fits of particle size (D50) vs. wedge fracture AUC gave R²=0.71 (p=0.0006). Compression max force gave R²=0.28. Fracture toughness ratios correlated with particle size ratios.

performance_metrics.coffee_grinder_particle_size_D50: Increased with annealing temperature: for PEO 200K, unannealed ~300 μm, 70°C/35 min ~450 μm, 80°C/60 min ~500 μm. Similar trends for 1M and 7M.
performance_metrics.syringability_work: Withdraw and injection work increased dramatically with PEO MW; annealing had negligible effect. For PEO 7M, hold time of 12 min needed to fill syringe.
performance_metrics.dissolution_f2_values: All >50 for annealed vs unannealed for same MW, indicating similarity. Specific f2 values not listed except statement that smallest f2 for PEO 7M 80°C/10 min was 59.2.

article_title: Mechanistic Evaluation of the Effect of Sintering on Compritol® 888 ATO Matrices

article_identification.title: Mechanistic Evaluation of the Effect of Sintering on Compritol® 888 ATO Matrices
article_identification.authors: Monica Rao, Anuradha Ranpise, Sameer Borate, Kaushik Thanki

materials_origin.ketorolac_tromethamine: Ranbaxy Lab Ltd, Goa, India (gift sample)

experimental_design.objective: To investigate effect of sintering on drug release from Compritol 888 ATO matrices and changes in drug/wax properties

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Geometric mixing for 10 min followed by direct compression at room temperature
ingredients_identification.results_summary: Sintering time inversely related to drug release rate; sintered tablets showed lower wettability and smoother surface; no drug-wax interaction or polymorphic change; release follows Higuchi model

pre_compression_parameters.note: All batches were compressed from same powder blend before sintering; post-sintering changes not reported separately.

ftir_spectral_measurements.conclusion: No difference between physical mixture and sintered samples; no drug-wax interaction

tablet_fabrication.equipment: Rotary tablet compression machine (Mini Press-II MT, Rimek)

compression.conditions: Room temperature, no pressure value reported

release_properties.sampling: 5 ml aliquots replaced with fresh medium, filtered, diluted to 10 ml, analyzed at 322 nm
release_properties.factors_influencing: Sintering time (longer sintering → slower release); decreased wettability (higher contact angle); wax melting and redistribution forming network-like structure
release_properties.release_profile_summary: Unsintered tablets showed highest release over 12 h; increasing sintering time decreased release rate; 3h sintered gave greatest retardation.

tablet_properties.note: Most properties reported only for unsintered tablets or qualitatively after sintering.
tablet_properties.contact_angle: Unsintered: lower contact angle (more wettable); Sintered: higher contact angle (less wettable) – see Fig. 3
tablet_properties.surface_morphology_SEM: Unsintered: heterogeneous rough surface; Sintered: smooth, thin film-like structure covering surface (wax melted and redistributed)

experimental_data.dsc_results: Ketorolac endothermic peak at 171°C; Compritol peak at ~72°C; No change in physical mixture after sintering; no drug-wax interaction or polymorphic change
experimental_data.pxrd_results: Ketorolac crystalline peaks at 2θ = 12.62, 13.99, 16.43, 17.04, 18.41, 20.10, 22.07, 22.89, 29.39; Compritol peaks at 21.33, 23.45; No change after sintering; no polymorphic transformation
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: R values for model fitting; Higuchi model best (R > 0.99 for all batches); release rate constants (k) decrease with sintering time

data.conclusion: Sintering causes melting and redistribution of wax, forming a network that entraps drug and lactose, decreasing wettability and retarding drug release. Release follows Higuchi diffusion. No chemical or polymorphic changes.

Mini-Tablets versus Nanoparticles for Controlling the Release of Amoxicillin: In vitro/In vivo Study

article_info.title: Mini-Tablets versus Nanoparticles for Controlling the Release of Amoxicillin: In vitro/In vivo Study
article_info.authors: Dalia A Gaber, Hossah S Alhawas, Fatimah A Alfadhel, Siham A Abdoun, Amal M Alsubaiyel, Rehab M Alsawi
article_info.abstract_summary: Both mini-tablets (MTs) and lipid/polymer nanoparticles (LPNs) were designed to control amoxicillin (AMX) release. MT-6 (Compritol/Chitosan 1:1) showed 79.5% release over 8h; LPN-11 (AMX:Cr 1:3, Chitosan 1 mg/mL) showed 80.9% over 8h. MIC of both was two-fold lower than control against H. pylori. Relative bioavailability was 1.85 (097_LPN11) and 1.8 (MT-6) vs market tablet.

materials_origin.amoxicillin_AMX: Gift from Mash for Pharmaceutical Industries, Egypt
materials_origin.glyceryl_monostearate_GMS: Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India
materials_origin.chitosan_CN: Sigma-Aldrich, Sintra, Portugal (50 kDa, 85% degree of deacetylation)
materials_origin.reference_product: Klavox tablets, Spimaco Co., Pharmaceutical & Chemical Industries, Al-Qassim, KSA

experimental_design.mini_tablets: Varying amounts of Compritol (Cr), Glyceryl monostearate (GMS), and fixed Chitosan (200 mg per dose) to achieve sustained release. 12 formulations (097_MT1-097_MT12). Each dose = 14 mini-tablets containing 250 mg AMX.
experimental_design.nanoparticles: Varying drug:lipid (Cr) ratios (1:0.5, 1:1, 1:2, 1:3) and chitosan solution concentrations (0.5, 1, 2 mg/mL). 12 formulations (097_LPN1-097_LPN12). Prepared by solvent emulsification evaporation.

mini_tablets.release_properties_summary: Burst release: 28.5% at 30 min; ~100% released within 4 h. Not sustained.
Burst: 19.1% at 30 min; sustained compared to lower Cr.
Optimal sustained release: 15.2% at 30 min; 79.5% over 8 h. Fickian diffusion (n=0.40, Peppas model).
GMS-free, higher release rate than Cr-based.
Higher release than Cr equivalent due to higher HLB of GMS.

nanoparticles.release_properties_summary: Burst followed by slow release up to 7h.

characterization.particle_size_nm: not specified (but TEM image shows ~200 nm range)

nanoparticles.release_properties_summary: Optimal sustained release: burst 22.21% at 30 min; 80.9% over 8h. Fickian diffusion (n=0.43, Peppas model).

pre_compression_parameters_mini_tablets.note: Values are ranges for all 097_MT1-097_MT12; no batch-specific data.

post_compression_parameters_mini_tablets.appearance: Yellowish-white or buff white, round concave, smooth surface

mini_tablets.granulation: Isopropyl alcohol, passed through 1 mm sieve, dried at 60°C, then 500 μm sieve, granules <250 μm collected.
mini_tablets.compression: Single punch tablet press machine with 4 mm diameter concave punch

nanoparticles.emulsification: Ultrasonic sonication for 5 min at 300 W, 3-sec pauses, room temperature
nanoparticles.separation: Centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, washed with deionized water, freeze-dried

ftir_analysis.sample_preparation: Mixed with KBr powder and pressed into pellets
ftir_analysis.results: No alternation in main functional groups; AMX peaks at 3475 cm⁻¹ (OH, NH), 1767 cm⁻¹ (C=O β-lactam), 1680 cm⁻¹ (C=O amide), 1589 cm⁻¹ (carboxylate) were retained in LPN formulations, confirming stability and compatibility.

dsc_analysis.results: Pure AMX showed sharp endothermic peak at 136°C; LPN formulations showed same peak with reduced intensity (due to lower drug content), no shift indicating no interaction.

release_kinetics_common.optimized_MT6: Best fit: Korsmeyer-Peppas (R² highest), n=0.40 → Fickian diffusion
release_kinetics_common.optimized_LPN11: Best fit: Korsmeyer-Peppas, n=0.43 → Fickian diffusion
release_kinetics_common.other_formulations: Not modeled individually; generally higher lipid content slowed release.

in_vitro_release_conditions.sampling: Predetermined intervals, replaced with fresh medium

antimicrobial_testing.method: Agar plate diffusion for inhibition zone; microdilution broth for MIC
antimicrobial_testing.control: Commercial AMX tablets suspended in sterile water

pharmacokinetic_study.animals: Male Wistar rats, 190-220 g, n=8 per group
pharmacokinetic_study.dosing: Oral via tubing; equivalent AMX dose from 097_LPN11, 097_MT6, or commercial tablet
pharmacokinetic_study.plasma_analysis: HPLC with C18 column, mobile phase methanol:phosphate buffer (10:90), UV 230 nm

in_vitro_in_vivo_correlation.conclusion: Good correlation between fraction dissolved in vitro and fraction absorbed in vivo.

data.conclusion: Both controlled-release mini-tablets and lipid/polymer nanoparticles can sustain amoxicillin release and improve bioavailability. Mini-tablets offer simplicity and reproducibility for industrial scale-up.

spray_drying_process.equipment: GLP-60 centrifugal spray with high-speed blade disk
spray_drying_process.preparation: 1 kg RT dissolved in 40 L water at 60°C with constant stirring

tablet_fabrication.method: Direct compression using lab mechanical press

ftir_info.instrument: FSM-1201 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Infraspek, Saint Petersburg, Russia)
ftir_info.sample_preparation: KBr discs containing 0.02 mg of taxifolin
ftir_info.key_findings: No chemical structure transformation during spray drying; differences in fingerprint area indicate phase transition (crystalline to amorphous). TS spectra showed broader bands at ~1000 cm⁻¹ and one band at 750 cm⁻¹ vs two thin bands for RT. Carbonyl shift from 1623.88 cm⁻¹ (RT) to 1643.18 cm⁻¹ (TS).

formulation_details.note: Column headers from Table 1 are ambiguous. The table likely includes: No., Mass of Tablet (mg), TS (mg), then excipients (mannitol, sorbitol, sodium starch glycolate, crospovidone, calcium stearate, aspartame, menthol). The raw data as presented in the article is provided below in order of appearance. Values are in mg. '-' indicates not present or zero.

tablet_selection_parameters.note: From Table 5. Only batches with reported data. Disintegration time in minutes, Hardness in N, Taste description. Regulatory requirements: Hardness >40 N (from text).

tablet_characteristics.note: Comparison between RT-based and TS-based tablets (selected formulation, likely batch 19 for TS). Values are mean ± SD.

dissolution_parameters.method: Paddle method, DT-600 dissolution tester (Erweka, Germany), HBSS solvent, volume 500 mL, temperature 37±0.5°C, rotation speed 50 rpm

solubility.method: European Pharmacopoeia 10.0; exact solubility by shaking 40 mg in 10 mL water for up to 6 h, UV at 290 nm
solubility.precipitation: Observed after 24 h for TS solution

cell_viability.viability: ~75% for both RT and TS at high concentrations (no significant difference)

antimicrobial_activity.method: Agar diffusion in biphasic medium, concentration 0.01 mg/mL in 10% DMSO
antimicrobial_activity.statistical_significance: No significant differences (Mann-Whitney U test, p=0.05)

statistical_analysis.presentation: Mean ± SEM for solubility, permeability, disintegration, hardness, dissolution
statistical_analysis.viability: Mean of six independent parallel wells
statistical_analysis.antimicrobial: Mann-Whitney U test with equation U = n1n2 + (nx(nx+1))/2 - Tx

data.missing_data_note: Pre-compression parameters (mass distribution, size, density, drug content, consolidation index) per batch, release kinetics per batch, swelling study, thickness, weight variation, and independent variable levels are not reported in the article. Only overall results for selected formulation (batch 19) and RT comparison are provided.

MaterialsOrigin.source: Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St Louis, MO, USA)

ExperimentalDesignOptimization.core_optimization: Various combinations of excipients (anhydrous lactose, Avicel PH101, PVP, HPMC, ethylcellulose) were tried. Optimum formula: Ind (40%), ethylcellulose (20%), magnesium stearate (1%), PVP (39%).

IngredientsIdentification.preparation: Direct compression method. Core formula with Ind, ethylcellulose, PVP, magnesium stearate. Three-layered tablets by manual feeding: bottom hydrophobic EC layer, core, top hydrophobic EC layer.
IngredientsIdentification.results: Successful zero-order release with good mechanical attributes.
IngredientsIdentification.equation: Calibration curve: y = 0.0635x + 0.0076 (where x = concentration in μg/ml, y = absorbance)
IngredientsIdentification.absorbance: λmax = 320 nm, R² = 0.9983, concentration range 10-50 μg/ml

ProcessParameters.compression_process: Single-step direct compression by manual feeding
ProcessParameters.equipment: Round compression sets (6 mm and 12 mm diameter)
ProcessParameters.coating: None (barrier layers are compressed, not coated)
ProcessParameters.process_conditions: Manual feeding of powders; no other conditions reported

ExperimentalData.key_results: All batches showed zero-order release kinetics. Thickness/diameter ratio correlated well with % release (R²=0.94 at 4h). t50% ranged 1.5-3.7h. Hardness 100-110 N, friability <0.5%.

performance_metrics.zero_order_compliance: All batches complied with zero-order kinetic model

ExperimentalData.statistical_analysis: ANOVA mentioned but no numerical results reported; significance criterion p<0.05.
ExperimentalData.zero_order_release_rates: Not explicitly given as K0 values; see kinetic models for correlation coefficients.

data.factors_influencing: Thickness/diameter ratio inversely proportional to release rate
Thickness/diameter ratio inversely proportional to release rate
data.best_fit: Zero order and Hixon-Crowell (highest r for Hixon-Crowell)

data.article_title: OPTIMIZED DELIVERY OF DICLOFENAC SODIUM FORMULATED IN A SUSTAINED RELEASE RAPHA AFRICANA HYDROCOLLOID MATRIX

materials_origin.talc: Not specified (likely BDH Chemicals or local)
materials_origin.raphia_gum: Extracted and purified in Pharmaceutical Technology Laboratory, University of Uyo, Nigeria (from incised trunk of Raphia africana palm in Uyo, Akwa Ibom State, Nigeria; authenticated with voucher number UUPHB (eii))

data.experimental_design: Wet granulation method using varying polymer ratios (0, 5, 10, 15, 20% w/w) of either R. africana hydrocolloid or PVP as binders. Formulations coded DWB-00 (no binder), DRA-05 to DRA-20 (R. africana), DPP-05 to DPP-20 (PVP). Tablets compressed at 35 kN using single punch machine.

process_parameters.granulation_method: Wet granulation using mortar and pestle, dispersion of binder in hot water
process_parameters.drying_conditions: Hot air oven at 50°C for about 1 hour
process_parameters.sieving: Wet mass passed through 2.0 mm sieve, dried granules through 1.0 mm sieve
process_parameters.tableting_machine: Single punch tableting machine (Cadmach Machinery CO. PVT. Ltd; India), model SSF-3
process_parameters.dissolution_conditions: USP basket method, 900 mL dissolution medium (phosphate buffer pH 7.3 with 2.1% w/v citric acid), 37±1°C, 50 rpm, 6 hours

additional_experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Two-way ANOVA with post-hoc Tukey-Kramer test, 95% confidence interval, p<0.05 considered significant. Significant differences observed for bulk/tapped densities, hardness, release rates. No significant difference in friability and drug content across batches (p>0.05).
additional_experimental_data.general_release_mechanism_statement: All batches followed non-fickian diffusion release mechanism (anomalous transport).

article_identification.title: Oral Controlled Release Formulations of Rifampicin. Part II: Effect of Formulation Variables and Process Parameters on In Vitro Release
article_identification.authors: P. S. Hiremath & R. N. Saha

rifampicin.source: Gift sample from Cadila Pharma Ltd. (Ahmedabad, India) and Lupin Laboratories (Aurangabad, India)

HPMC_100_cPs.source: Gift from Zydus Cadila Research Center (Ahmedabad, India)

HPMC_15000_cPs.source: Gift from Zydus Cadila Research Center

materials_origin.talc: Pharmaceutical grade, 0.6% w/w of total tablet weight
materials_origin.magnesium_stearate: Pharmaceutical grade, 0.4% w/w of total tablet weight
materials_origin.ascorbic_acid: 0.02% w/v added to dissolution medium to prevent rifampicin degradation

experimental_design.objective: To study the effect of formulation variables (drug:HPMC ratio, HPMC viscosity grade, drug particle size) and process parameter (compression force) on the release characteristics and kinetics of rifampicin from HPMC matrix tablets.

dissolution_method.medium_composition: pH 7.4 phosphate buffer containing 0.02% w/v ascorbic acid
dissolution_method.sampling: Predetermined intervals, 10 mL withdrawn and replaced with fresh medium

process_parameters.compression_device: Single station tablet compression machine (Cadmach)
process_parameters.compression_force: Adjusted to achieve hardness of 6-7 kg/cm² for most batches; for effect studies: 4, 6, 10 kg/cm² hardness levels
process_parameters.batch_size: Three batches per formulation, each batch not specified but 6 tablets per dissolution test

drug_HPMC_ratio.note: Percentages are w/w of drug (450 mg)

data.ftir_spectral_measurements: Not performed / not reported in the article

tablet_fabrication_details.mixing: Drug and polymer (both passed through 60 mesh) mixed uniformly
tablet_fabrication_details.blending: Blended with talc (0.6% w/w) and magnesium stearate (0.4% w/w)
tablet_fabrication_details.compression: Direct compression using single station machine with 13 mm concave punches
tablet_fabrication_details.hardness_determination: Monsanto tablet hardness tester, mean of 20 tablets
tablet_fabrication_details.friability_determination: Cambell Electronic Friabilator, 25 rpm for 5 min, 20 tablets
tablet_fabrication_details.weight_variation: Electronic balance (Afcoset), 20 tablets, max % variation from mean
tablet_fabrication_details.drug_content: As per Indian Pharmacopoeia 1996 for rifampicin capsules, UV method

stability.most_stable_formulation: HPMC K4M formulations (102_R7, 102_R9) showed lowest degradation rate constants
stability.release_stability: f2 factor >58 for 6 months at CRT, indicating similar release profiles

batch_reproducibility.f2_factor_range: 69.72 to 87.39 between original and reproduced batches

effect_of_particle_size.on_R4_40%_K100LV: Decrease in particle size decreased release (t50% increased from 13.41 to 16.43 h); mechanism shifted from Super Case II (n=0.98) to anomalous (n=0.87)
effect_of_particle_size.on_R7_20%_K4M: Decrease in particle size decreased release in later hours; initial burst higher for smaller particles; n changed from 0.48 to 0.37

effect_of_compression_force.on_R4_40%_K100LV: At 4 kg/cm² hardness: immediate disintegration, complete release within 6 h. At 6 and 10 kg/cm²: no significant difference.
effect_of_compression_force.on_R7_20%_K4M: At 4 kg/cm²: initial burst release. At 6 and 10 kg/cm²: no significant difference.
effect_of_compression_force.minimum_required_hardness: 6 kg/cm² for both low and high viscosity HPMC

experimental_data.in_vivo_studies: Not performed; recommended for future work
experimental_data.swelling_water_uptake: Not reported (not applicable for this matrix system, no floating study)

fig1_release_profile_HPMC_K100LV_formula.description: FIG. 1. Release profile of rifampicin from HPMC K100LV formulations. Each data point represents the average of 6 tablets from 3 batches with SD.

fig2_effect_HPMC_viscosity.description: FIG. 2. Effect of HPMC viscosity on release profiles of rifampicin (20% polymer ratio).

fig3_particle_size_effect_40%_K100LV.description: FIG. 3. Effect of rifampicin particle size on its release profiles from 40% HPMC K100LV formulations.

fig4_particle_size_effect_20%_K4M.description: FIG. 4. Effect of rifampicin particle size on its release profiles from 20% HPMC K4M formulations.

fig5_compression_force_40%_K100LV.description: FIG. 5. Effect of compression force on rifampicin release profiles from 40% HPMC K100LV formulations.

fig6_compression_force_20%_K4M.description: FIG. 6. Effect of compression force on rifampicin release profiles from 20% HPMC K4M formulations.

data.notes: The article does not contain FTIR, swelling index, lag time, floating time, or disintegration time. Tablet thickness not reported. Pre-compression powder properties not reported. 102_R1 and 102_R11 release kinetics not listed in Table 4. All percentages for talc and Mg stearate are w/w of total tablet weight; HPMC percentages are w/w of drug.

experimental_design.approach: Wet granulation technique selected after trials with direct compression and roller compaction. Optimization of rate-controlling polymers (Natrosol 250L, Natrosol 250H, HPMC) and coating composition to match innovator (Novartis OROS) dissolution profile.

tablet_fabrication_process.method_for_F2: Roller compaction (failed to solve flow and weight variation)

release_properties.factors_influencing: pH (tested in 0.1N HCl, pH 4.5 acetate, pH 6.8 phosphate), polymer ratio, coating weight gain.

article_title: Preparation and Characterization of Controlled-Release Floating Bilayer Tablets of Esomeprazole and Clarithromycin

article_info.title: Preparation and Characterization of Controlled-Release Floating Bilayer Tablets of Esomeprazole and Clarithromycin
article_info.authors: Israr M., Pugliese N., Farid A., Ghazanfar S., Di Cerbo A., Muzammal M., Alamri A.S., Basheeruddin Asdaq S.M., Ahmad A., Khan K.A.

sections.abstract: Controlled-release effervescent floating bilayer tablets reduce dosage frequency and improve patient compliance with enhanced therapeutic outcomes. Generally, two different tablets of clarithromycin and esomeprazole, respectively, are given for the treatment of Helicobacter pylori infection and it might be worth incorporating both in a single tablet. In the current study, controlled-release floating bilayer tablets of clarithromycin and esomeprazole (104_F1-104_F4) were developed with different rates of polymeric materials by a direct compression method. During the formulation, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was performed for possible interactions between drugs and excipients. No interactions between drugs and excipients were noted. Moreover, the bilayer tablets' thickness, diameter, friability, hardness, weight variation, dissolution, and percent purity were found within the acceptable limits. The floating lag time and total floating time of all formulations were found to be <25 s and 24 h respectively. The release of both the clarithromycin and esomeprazole started at the same time from the controlled-release floating bilayer tablets by anomalous non-Fickian diffusion, and the polymeric materials extended the drug release rate up to 24 h. In the case of 104_F1, the results approached ideal zero-order kinetics. The dissolution profiles of the tested and reference tablet formulations were compared, but no significant differences were observed. It can be concluded that such controlled-release effervescent floating bilayer tablets can be efficiently used in clinical practice to reduce dosage frequency and increase patient compliance with continuous drug release for 24 h, which ultimately might enhance therapeutic efficacy.
sections.introduction: Floating drug delivery systems are designed to deliver drugs with low intestinal solubility or poor stability into the stomach by floating over the gastric fluid due to their low-density. These dosage forms are embedded within tablets made of hydroxypropyl cellulose and sodium and citric bicarbonate, two gas-generating agents that, when coming into contact with gastric fluid, generate carbon dioxide, which promotes the floating of the tablets. The reported optimum ratio of citric and sodium bicarbonate for carbon dioxide generation is 0.76:1. The aforementioned tablets may be single-layered or bi-layered; in this latter case, the gas-generating agents are in one layer and the drug is added in the sustained-release portion layer. Moreover, they are made of either natural polymers (Xanthan gum, Gellan gum chitosan, Guar gum, sodium alginate) or synthetic polymers (Eudragit, Ethylcellulose, hydroxypropyl cellulose). Drugs with a shorter half-life (2-6 h), better bioavailability, dissociation constant >2.5 partition constant >1, and stability in an acidic environment are suitable for the inclusion in floating drug delivery systems. These systems allow a controlled drug release for an extended period, keeping the dose at the absorption site and therefore improving the bioavailability. Helicobacter pylori management with proton pump inhibitors, e.g., esomeprazole, clarithromycin 500 mg, amoxicillin 1 g, or metronidazole 400 or 500 mg, is known to lower the risk of gastroduodenal ulcers and avoid its relapse. Despite that, a 14-day regimen may be more beneficial than a 7-day one, the optimum effective period of therapy is still a matter of debate. Esomeprazole is a proton pump inhibitor for the management of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) and the recovery and maintenance treatment of erosive esophagitis, but it is also a component of the triple-drug regimen for the Helicobacter pylori infection. On the other hand, clarithromycin is a broad-spectrum antibiotic belonging to the macrolides class with a molecular weight of 747.95 Dalton, a pKa value of 8.99, a half-life of ~4 h, soluble acetonitrile, ethanol, and acetone, slightly soluble in methanol, and poorly soluble in water (0.33 mg/L). Clarithromycin-based floating matrix tablets have been developed using hydroxypropyl cellulose K15M, hydroxypropyl cellulose K4M, hydroxypropyl cellulose, and hydroxyethylcellulose, obtaining an in vitro controlled release of the antibiotic. Moreover, floating matrix tablets have also been developed using a natural polymer, e.g., pomegranate peels powder, achieving a floating time of 5 h and a 98.67% of drug release. In the current research, new controlled-release floating bilayer tablets of esomeprazole and clarithromycin were prepared and assessed for various physicochemical characteristics, such as a dimensional test, friability, hardness, and weight variation, and in vitro evaluation.
sections.conclusions: In our research, the effervescent controlled-release bilayer tablets of clarithromycin and esomeprazole were successfully developed and characterized for various parameters. In a buoyancy study, it was found that tablets started floating in a very short time, about 20-24 s, and floated for 24 h. An in vitro dissolution was performed to know about drug release patterns. The polymeric material extended the drug release rates up to 24 h and might be used as a once-a-day (OD) tablet. The drug was released by anomalous non-Fickian diffusion or case-II zero-order kinetics almost near to ideal zero-order kinetics. The percent purity of both drugs was within the USP acceptable limits. The dissolution profile of the tested and reference tablets and the f1 and f2 values were not in the acceptable range. The statistic was applied to the dissolution profiles of the tested and reference formulations, and no similarity was noted between the dissolution profiles. These polymeric bilayer floating tablets might lead to patient compliance, reduce side effects, and minimize dosage frequency with zero-order drug release kinetics.

materials_origin.FTIR_spectrophotometer: LI600300 spectrum Two Lita, Llantrisant, UK

experimental_design.type: Comparative formulation study with varying polymer combinations

independent_variables.variable: Eudragit RS100 amount in clarithromycin layer
Eudragit RS100 amount in esomeprazole layer

dependent_variables: Flow properties (angle of repose, Hausner ratio, compressibility index)
Tablet physical properties (thickness, diameter, hardness, friability, weight variation)
Floating behavior (lag time, total floating time, density)

ftir.purpose: Check interactions between drugs and excipients
ftir.result: No interactions observed; spectra of drugs and formulations were similar
ftir.clarithromycin_peaks: OH/NH (3742.6 cm⁻¹), CH₂ asymmetric (2881.3 cm⁻¹), C≡C stretching (2103.8 cm⁻¹), C=C stretch (1710.3 cm⁻¹), C-C stretch (1063 cm⁻¹)
ftir.esomeprazole_peaks: NH-stretch (3345 cm⁻¹), CH-stretch (2448.3 cm⁻¹), CH (2149 cm⁻¹), C-O stretch (1203 cm⁻¹), C-O stretch (1014 cm⁻¹)

equations.density: ρ = m / v, with v = π r² h
equations.compressibility_index: CI = ((Tapped density - Bulk density) / Tapped density) * 100
equations.hausner_ratio: Hausner ratio = Tapped density / Bulk density

104_F4.note: Immediate release formulation taken as reference; not a floating tablet

swelling_study.note: Due to hydrophilicity of polymeric materials, particularly Carbopol 934P. Graphical representation for 104_F1, 104_F2, 104_F3 (Figure 3) but individual batch values not provided.

clarithromycin.mechanism: Does not follow power law (immediate release)

esomeprazole.mechanism: Does not follow power law (immediate release)

dissolution_comparison.note: Acceptable limits: f1 1-15, f2 50-100. None of the tested formulations were similar to reference F4.

process_parameters.compression_device: Single-punch tablet machine (Erweka-AR-400, West Germany)
equipment_used: Fourier-transform infrared spectrophotometer (LI600300 spectrum Two Lita)

process_conditions.temperature_degC: Room temperature for FTIR; 37 ± 0.5 for dissolution
process_conditions.pressure: Not specified, adjusted to achieve hardness 5-10 kg/cm²

experimental_data_summary.key_findings: All formulations showed good flow properties, acceptable physical parameters, floating lag time <25 s, total floating time >24 h, and extended drug release up to 24 h. 104_F1 exhibited release kinetics closest to ideal zero-order. No similarity between test and reference dissolution profiles.
experimental_data_summary.statistical_analysis: Paired t-test showed no similarity between tested formulations and reference (p-values >0.05? Actually the reported values are t-statistics? Table 8 shows values like 7.2134, which are likely t-values, not p-values. The text says 'The results showed that there was no similarity' – interpretation: significant difference.

article_title: Preparation and Evaluation of Diclofenac Sodium Tablet Coated with Polyelectrolyte Multilayer Film Using Hypromellose Acetate Succinate and Polymethacrylates for pH-Dependent, Modified Release Drug Delivery

article_info.title: Preparation and Evaluation of Diclofenac Sodium Tablet Coated with Polyelectrolyte Multilayer Film Using Hypromellose Acetate Succinate and Polymethacrylates for pH-Dependent, Modified Release Drug Delivery
article_info.authors: Balamurugan Jeganathan, Vijayalakshmi Prakya, Abhijit Deshmukh
article_info.objective: Formulate diclofenac sodium (DS) tablets coated with polyelectrolyte multilayer film using Eudragit E (EE) and hypromellose acetate succinate (HPMCAS) for pH-dependent colonic targeted delivery.

materials_origin.diclofenac_sodium: Mylan Laboratories Limited, Hyderabad, India (gift sample)
materials_origin.mannitol_microcrystalline_cellulose_cros: Mylan Laboratories Limited, Hyderabad, India (gift samples)

optimization_parameters: Citric acid concentration in core tablet (0, 3.75, 7.5, 20 mg per tablet for 105_F1-105_F4; 7.5 and 10 mg for 105_F5-105_F6)
Polymer coating type and number of layers: double-layer (EE then HPMCAS) for 105_F1-105_F4; triple-layer (HPMCAS/EE/HPMCAS) for 105_F5-105_F6
EE/HPMCAS ratio optimization via turbidity measurements

turbidity_measurements.method: Monitor transmittance at 420 and 620 nm using UV spectrophotometer
turbidity_measurements.solutions: 0.1% EE in 0.1N HCl, 0.1% HPMCAS in pH 6.8 phosphate buffer
turbidity_measurements.result: Maximum turbidity (lowest transmittance) at EE/HPMCAS mass ratio 0.43, indicating optimal polyelectrolyte complex formation

ingredients_identification.preparation_of_PEC: EE 3% w/v in 0.1N HCl added slowly to HPMCAS 3% w/v in pH 6.8 phosphate buffer under stirring (800 rpm, 1h). Precipitate filtered, washed, dried at 60°C, ground, passed through #30 sieve.
ingredients_identification.ftir_results: Characteristic peaks: HPMCAS at 1064 cm⁻¹ (C-O stretch), 1400-1350 cm⁻¹ (C-O-C), 1750 cm⁻¹ (C=O), 3400 cm⁻¹ (OH). EE at 1150-1190,1240,1270 cm⁻¹ (ester), 1730 cm⁻¹ (C=O), 2770 and 2820 cm⁻¹ (dimethylamino). PEC showed weaker 1728 cm⁻¹ band and reduced 2770/2824 cm⁻¹ bands, confirming electrostatic interaction between protonated dimethylamino groups of EE and carboxylate groups of HPMCAS.
ingredients_identification.absorbance_wavelength: UV analysis for drug content at 276 nm; HPLC detection at 278 nm

ftir_measurements.instrument: PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrometer, KBr disk method
ftir_measurements.key_findings: PEC showed decreased intensity of 1728 cm⁻¹ (carboxyl) and reduced 2770/2824 cm⁻¹ (dimethylamino), confirming interpolyelectrolyte complex formation.

tablet_fabrication.granulation: Wet granulation with 5% w/v hydroxypropyl cellulose in water; drying at 60°C for 1-2 h; sifting through #30 sieve.
tablet_fabrication.mixing: Intragranular materials mixed for 10 min; extragranular croscarmellose sodium mixed for 5 min; magnesium stearate mixed for 5 min.
tablet_fabrication.compression_device: Rotary tablet punching machine (Kambert machinery, Ahmedabad, India) with 7.0 mm round standard concave punches.
tablet_fabrication.compression_conditions: Target core weight 160 mg, hardness adjusted as per batch.
tablet_fabrication.coating_device: Perforated pan coater (Freund coater, Model 0.65L, Japan).
tablet_fabrication.coating_solutions: 10% w/v EE in isopropyl alcohol; 10% w/v HPMCAS in acetone; plasticized with triethyl citrate (10% w/w of dry polymer).

release_properties.media: 0.1N HCl (750 mL) for 2h, then pH 6.8 phosphate buffer (by adding 250 mL 0.20M trisodium phosphate) or directly pH 6.8 buffer (1000 mL).

105_F1.release_in_pH6.8_5h: ~32% (for 2% EE+4% HPMCAS coating) or ~14% (for 4%+4%) - but standard 105_F1 with 1.88% EE+3.69% HPMCAS gave <5% in direct pH6.8

105_F4.mechanism: Rapid dissolution due to high citric acid

105_F5.mechanism: Sustained release due to triple-layer PEC film

in_vitro.key_findings: Citric acid concentration linearly increased drug release at pH 6.8. Triple-layer coating (105_F6) provided sustained release over 8h with <2% release in acid. 105_F4 with 20 mg citric acid gave 100% release in 3h at pH 6.8.

in_vivo.plasma_analysis: HPLC with C18 column, mobile phase acetate buffer:acetonitrile (40:60), flow 1.0 mL/min, detection 278 nm, retention time 4.8 min.
in_vivo.conclusion: 105_F6 showed delayed Tmax (6h vs 4h) and higher AUC, indicating sustained and targeted release in lower GI tract.

experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Paired t-test for Cmax and AUC; p<0.05 considered significant. AUC difference significant (p=0.041).

release_kinetics_all_formulations.others: Not explicitly modeled, but 105_F1-105_F4 showed pH-dependent release; 105_F5 similar to F6.

article_title: PREPARATION AND CHARACTERIZATION OF CHITOSAN SUCCINATE AS COATING POLYMER FOR ENTERIC-COATED TABLET

chitosan_succinate_preparation.method: Dissolved 4g chitosan in 400 ml 1% acetic acid. Succinic anhydride dissolved in 400 ml methanol, added dropwise. pH neutralized with 1.0N NaOH. Precipitate filtered, washed with methanol, dialyzed for 24h, dried at 40°C for 24h, sieved through 60-mesh.

results.appearance: Odorless granular solid, yellowish-white, irregular shape, coarse/rough surface
results.equation: DS(mol/g) = [N_NaOH * V_NaOH - N_HCl * V_HCl] / (Chitosan succinate mass in g)
results.absorbance_wavelengths: 228 nm for solubility test, 276 nm for dissolution test
results.calibration_curve: y = 0.00357 + 0.02818x, r = 0.99992 (for natrium diclofenac in phosphate buffer pH 6.8)

precompression_parameters_core.powder_mixture_tests: Performed but results not reported (bulk density, tapped density, Hausner's ratio, angle of repose, compressibility index)

key_peaks.1585.54: -C=O for amine (decreased intensity compared to chitosan)

coating_process.note: 106_F2 had highest viscosity; 106_F3 and 106_F4 had lower viscosity due to HPMC replacement

coated_tablet_properties.common: Smooth but not shiny surface; 106_F1 white, 106_F2-106_F4 brownish

106_F2.note: Weight increase >5% not eligible for coated tablets

coating.conditions: Drying process mentioned; good drying gives smooth surface

dissolution_test.media: HCl pH 1.2 for first 2h, then phosphate buffer pH 6.8 for 45 min

per_batch.note: Tablet dissolved in acid within 1h disintegration test
Tablet did not dissolve in acid but released >10%

release_properties.release_kinetics: Not modeled (zero order, first order, Hixson-Crowell, Korsmeyer-Peppas not reported)
release_properties.factors_influencing_release: pH (low release in acid due to protonation of remaining amino groups? Actually chitosan succinate solubility increases in alkali due to carboxylate ion formation; combination with HPMC improves acid resistance)

disintegration_time.106_F1: Dissolved in acid (within 1h), not tested in alkaline
disintegration_time.106_F2: Undissolved in acid, disintegrated in alkaline at 20.95 ± 1.88 min
disintegration_time.106_F3: Undissolved in acid, disintegrated in alkaline at 19.11 ± 0.12 min
disintegration_time.106_F4: Undissolved in acid, disintegrated in alkaline at 17.75 ± 1.29 min

experimental_data.key_results: Chitosan succinate was successfully synthesized with DS 3.65 mol/g. Enteric-coated tablets with chitosan succinate alone (106_F1, 106_F2) failed to limit drug release in acid (<10% required). Combination with HPMC (106_F3, 106_F4) achieved acid release <10% (8.53% and 6.74%) and alkaline release >80% (93.48% and 91.97%).
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Mean ± SD reported for n=3 in dissolution, n=10-20 for tablet properties. Coefficient of variation for uniformity <2%.
experimental_data.conclusion: Chitosan succinate alone insufficient as enteric coating; combination with HPMC at 3:1 or 2:1 (total polymer 3%) meets USP/Pharmacopeia requirements for enteric-coated tablets.
