key_bands.SA: 1621 cm⁻¹ (asymmetric COO⁻ stretch), 1423 cm⁻¹ (symmetric COO⁻ stretch)
key_bands.CS: 1659 cm⁻¹ (amide I), 1597 cm⁻¹ (amino group), 1486 cm⁻¹ (C-N amide)
key_bands.PEC_film: Single broad band at 1609 cm⁻¹ (shift from 1659 cm⁻¹), indicating ionic interaction between NH₃⁺ (CS) and COO⁻ (SA)

fourier_transform_infrared_spectral_meas.conclusion: Formation of CS-SA polyelectrolyte complex confirmed.

swelling_behavior.F5_0_1: Rapid descent after transfer to SIF (complete dissolution)

fd2P.Release_mechanism: Anomalous transport (diffusion + structural change) in SVF (n=0.6243); super case II transport in SVF/SSF (n=1.002)
fd2P.Kinetics_best_fit_AIC: Higuchi in SVF (AIC -45.16), Korsmeyer-Peppas in SVF/SSF (AIC -5.13)
fd2P.pH_dependent: Yes, faster release at higher pH

fd3P.Kinetics_best_fit_AIC: Higuchi in SVF (AIC -41.69), Korsmeyer-Peppas in SVF/SSF (AIC -18.13)

fd2C.pH_dependent: Not significant, but chitosan precipitates at high pH

fd3C.Release_mechanism: Diffusion + structural change in SVF (n=0.6158), diffusion in SVF/SSF (n=0.6718)

fd1H.Kinetics_best_fit_AIC: Korsmeyer-Peppas in SVF (AIC -31.79), Higuchi in SVF/SSF (AIC -35.23)

Tablet_Properties.Note: Not tablets; properties of freeze-dried bigels:

Experimental_Data.Key_results: Batch fd3P (3% pectin) showed highest hardness, good mucoadhesion, and pH-responsive drug release: faster release in SVF/SSF mixture (semen simulant) than in SVF alone. Pectin-based bigels are 'smart' systems for on-demand HIV prevention during intercourse.

Performance_metrics.Hardness_significance: fd3P significantly harder than all others (p<0.05, Student's t-test).
Performance_metrics.Mucoadhesion_significance: No significant differences between concentrations of same polymer; pectin and HPMC batches significantly more mucoadhesive than chitosan batches (p<0.05).
Performance_metrics.Drug_release_f2_similarity: fd2P and fd3P profiles similar in SVF (f2>50), not similar in SVF/SSF.

Experimental_Data.Zero_and_first_order_release: Not reported; only Korsmeyer-Peppas and Higuchi kinetics were fitted.

article_info.authors: Pornsit Chaiya, Siriporn Okonogi, Thawatchai Phaechamud

data.experimental_design: Direct compression method. Formulations: plain IBU, IP (ibuprofen-111_P407 eutectic 1:1.5), and IP with varying % of effervescent agents (sodium bicarbonate:citric acid 1:1.3 w/w) at 5%, 10%, 20%, 25%, 30% w/w. No factorial optimization design reported.
data.ingredients_identification: Not performed (no FTIR, DSC, or other identification data beyond composition).

wetting_gel_erosion_data.method: Stereomicroscope with image analysis (Olympus SZX10, magnification 15×), medium with 0.05% brilliant blue

experimental_data_summary.key_findings: Stereomicroscope with image analysis successfully measured wetting, gel formation, and erosion rates. Higher effervescent content increased wetting and erosion rates but decreased gel formation. Dissolution rate positively correlated with wetting rate (R²=0.9641) and erosion rate up to 0.16 mm/min. Disintegration time negatively correlated with wetting and erosion rates.
experimental_data_summary.statistical_analysis: Linear regression for correlations; model selection criteria (R², AIC, MSC) for release kinetics.
experimental_data_summary.zero_first_order_release: Not directly reported; Peppas-Sahlin model used (diffusion/erosion combined).

data.notes: The requested batch naming (1P,2P,3P,1C,2C,3C,1H,2H,3H) does not appear in the article. The actual formulation codes are IBU, IP, IP5E, IP10E, IP20E, IP25E, IP30E. All data above correspond to these. Pre-compression parameters, hardness, friability, thickness, weight variation, density, drug content, consolidation index, FTIR, lag time, floating time, swelling index were not reported in this study. Cumulative release vs time values are approximated from Figure 7 in the original article.

optimized_caplet.disintegration_time: Not applicable (extended release over 30 days, no disintegration)
optimized_caplet.stability: Not directly reported; X-ray imaging showed gradual erosion over 28 days in vivo

effect_of_Carbopol_content_on_swelling.1_percent_w_w: Dimensional increase: length, width, thickness (see Fig 2b; exact values not tabulated)
effect_of_Carbopol_content_on_swelling.3_percent_w_w: Greatest swelling; no significant effect of silk pouch (p<0.005)

in_vitro_results.swelling_study: Dimensional increase positively correlated with Carbopol 974P NF content; PBS caused more swelling than SVF

figure_1_approximated_release_data.description: Fractional AZT release from optimized caplet formulation in SVF (pH 4.5) and PBS (pH 7.4) over 30 days. Time points (days): 3, 7, 14, 21, 28.

in_vivo_results_pig_model.X_ray_imaging: Drug-loaded caplet showed gradual fading of contrast (erosion from outside inward); swelling observed

AZT_concentrations.plasma_range_ug_ml: 1.18 ± 0.01 to 1.13 ± 0.02 (decreased over time, Cmax on day 7)

in_vivo_results_pig_model.comparison_to_EC50_EC95: Plasma levels effective (above EC50/EC95) but low to limit toxicity; vaginal levels higher for site-specific action
in_vivo_results_pig_model.histopathology: Scored abnormalities: epithelial erosion/ulceration (minimal to moderate), epithelial proliferation (mild to moderate), subepithelial leukocyte influx (minimal to severe), stromal fibrosis (minimal to mild), perivascular cell cuffing (minimal to moderate), tissue necrosis (isolated case, pig B4). Some abnormalities attributed to device and silk pouch.

statistical_analysis.design_analysis: Box-Behnken with MINITAB; R² values: FMI 87.3%, Hardness 61.7%, FDR 77.9%
statistical_analysis.kinetic_model_fitting: Multiple models with R²_adj values ranging 0.885-0.997 (SVF) and 0.502-0.995 (PBS)

swelling_water_uptake_study.swelling_index_percent: Reported as percentage dimensional increase (length, width, thickness) for 1%,2%,3% Carbopol; exact values not tabulated but shown in Fig 2b

data.missing_data_note: The following requested data were not present in the article: FTIR spectra, friability for any batch, disintegration time, weight variation, thickness/diameter per batch, density, drug content per batch, consolidation index, pre-compression parameters, individual release kinetics for 113_F1-113_F15, and detailed swelling index values. Only the optimized formulation's properties and the Box-Behnken design matrix with three responses are reported.

materials_origin.losartan_potassium: Alkem Pharmaceutical Pvt. Limited, India (gift sample)
materials_origin.hpmc_k100m: Colorcon Asia Pvt Limited, India (gift sample)
materials_origin.affinisol_hpmc_as126g: Colorcon Asia Pvt Limited, India (gift sample)

experimental_design_optimization.objective: To develop sustained release matrix tablet of losartan potassium using HPMC K100M and Affinisol as release retarding polymers.
experimental_design_optimization.design: Nine trial formulations (114_F1-114_F9) prepared by varying polymer content from 50 mg to 100 mg each, total tablet weight 310 mg.
experimental_design_optimization.selection_criteria: In vitro drug release profile for 12 hours using USP Type II dissolution apparatus at 50 rpm in distilled water.
experimental_design_optimization.best_formulation: 114_F4 with 75 mg HPMC K100M and 100 mg Affinisol, extending drug release up to 12 hours.

uv_method.preparation: Standard stock 1000 µg/mL, secondary stock 100 µg/mL, diluted to concentrations 1,2,4,6,8,10,15,20 µg/mL

ftir_measurements.method: KBr disk, 1000 psig, scan at 4 mm/s, resolution 2 cm⁻¹, range 400-4000 cm⁻¹
ftir_measurements.drug_excipient_compatibility: No change or shift of peaks observed after mixing with excipients and storing at 40°C for two weeks.

process_parameters.equipment: Single punch compression machine (Amar Enterprises, Ambala Cantt, India; Model No.: AE-347-IV)

stability.physical_change: Some tablets stuck together due to moisture exposure

experimental_data.key_results: Formulation 114_F4 (HPMC K100M 75 mg, Affinisol 100 mg) achieved desired release: ≤30% at 2h, ≥50% at 6h, 100% at 12h. Zero-order release kinetics (R²=0.9892) with non-Fickian diffusion (n=0.692). Stable for 1 month at 60°C.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: All experiments repeated three times except in vitro dissolution (six times). Data expressed as mean ± SD. p < 0.05 considered significant. Similarity factor f2 calculated using equation: f2 = 50 * log{[1 + (1/n)Σ(Rt-Tt)^2]^{-0.5} * 100}.
experimental_data.zero_order_release_for_all_formulations: Calculated only for 114_F4 (R²=0.9892); not reported for others.
experimental_data.first_order_release_for_all_formulations: Calculated only for 114_F4 (R²=0.8512); not reported for others.
experimental_data.tablet_swelling_study: Not performed (no swelling index reported). Surface morphology by SEM showed non-porous surface becoming smooth non-porous over time, opposite to typical hydrophilic polymer behavior due to Affinisol.

article_title: The application of povidone in the preparation of modified release tablets

article_info.title: The application of povidone in the preparation of modified release tablets
article_info.authors: Regina Kasperek, Lukasz Zimmer, Maria Zun, Dorota Dwornicka, Katarzyna Wojciechowska, Ewa Poleszak
article_info.abstract: Tablets containing different types of povidone (Kollidon CL, Kollidon 30, Kollidon SR) and other excipients were prepared by direct compression. Fast-disintegrating tablets contained Kollidon CL, lactose, and Avicel; substitution with β-CD prolonged disintegration. Prolonged-release tablets (117_T5 and 117_T8) best fitted the Higuchi model, with release mechanism following Korsmeyer-Peppas anomalous (non-Fickian) transport.

article_title: The Effect of Acid pH Modifiers on the Release Characteristics of Weakly Basic Drug from Hydrophilic-Lipophilic Matrices

data.article_title: The Effect of Acid pH Modifiers on the Release Characteristics of Weakly Basic Drug from Hydrophilic-Lipophilic Matrices

sections.abstract: The solubility of weakly basic drugs within GI tract leads to pH-dependent release. The study aimed to prepare hydrophilic-lipophilic (hypromellose-montanyloyl wax) matrix tablets ensuring pH-independent delivery of verapamil-HCl by incorporating citric, fumaric, or itaconic acids. Dissolution studies used changing pH (2 h pH 1.2, then 10 h pH 6.8). Microenvironment pH was measured. Full factorial design and PLS-2 regression were used. Acidifiers increased drug release rate. Concentration and strength of acidifier were crucial (p<0.01); solubility did not influence effects except surface pH at 480 min.
sections.introduction: Discusses pH-dependent solubility of weakly basic drugs and strategies to achieve pH-independent release, including incorporation of organic acids. Focus on verapamil-HCl, HPMC-montanylcol wax matrices.
sections.materials_and_methods: Describes materials, granulation, tablet compression, dissolution testing, pH measurement, and statistical analysis.
sections.results_and_discussion: Presents dissolution profiles, similarity factors, release kinetics, gel layer pH, and statistical evaluation.
sections.conclusion: Incorporation of 100 mg citric or fumaric acid improved pH-independent release. Concentration and pKa were crucial; solubility not significant.

materials_origin.citric_acid: Sigma-Aldrich, GmbH, Steinheim am Albuch, Germany
materials_origin.fumaric_acid: Sigma-Aldrich, GmbH, Steinheim am Albuch, Germany
materials_origin.itaconic_acid: Sigma-Aldrich, GmbH, Steinheim am Albuch, Germany

experimental_design_for_optimization.method: Full factorial design and partial least squares PLS-2 regression
dependent_variables: surface pH of gel layer at 360 min
surface pH of gel layer at 480 min

ingredients_identification.particle_size_measurement: Optical microscope (DN 45, Lambda, Prague) with CCD camera (Alphaphot, Nikon), Ia32 software. Mean diameters: citric 200.1 μm, fumaric 179.3 μm, itaconic 190.6 μm.
ingredients_identification.drug_content_analysis: Spectrophotometric method at 278 nm (Lambda 25, Perkin Elmer, Wellesley, USA).
ingredients_identification.dissolution_analysis: UV spectrophotometer at 278 nm for VH.

formulation_details.note: All tablets also contain 0.5% colloid silica and 2.5% magnesium stearate. Total weight per tablet approximately 350 mg. Constant components per tablet: Verapamil-HCl 100 mg, HPMC K4M 60 mg, montanylcol wax 82.1 mg. Filler (acid + MCC) totals 100 mg.

pre_compression_parameters.weight_variation_overall: Average weight 345.7-354.9 mg, max SD 2.6 mg (n=20 per batch, not reported individually)

tablet_fabrication.granulation_method: Thermoplastic (melt) granulation using montanylcol wax as binder
tablet_fabrication.mixing: Turbula mixer TC2, Basel, Switzerland for 10 min before and after adding lubricants
tablet_fabrication.compression_device: Korsch EK 0 press, Korsch Pressen, Berlin, Germany
tablet_fabrication.compression_pressure: not specified, target similar hardness (111-127 N)

release_properties.dissolution_method: Paddle method, 100 rpm, 37°C, change of pH: 2 h in pH 1.2 (artificial gastric juice) then 10 h in pH 6.8 (adjusted with sodium triphosphate 18.7 g)
release_properties.korsmeyer_peppas_exponent_n: Ranged from 0.520 to 0.589 for samples containing acidifiers (anomalous transport, non-Fickian)
release_properties.release_mechanism: Fickian diffusion (Higuchi and Baker-Lonsdale best fit) with minor erosion contribution (anomalous transport)
release_properties.factors_influencing_release: pH, acidifier concentration and pKa (significant), acid solubility (not significant except surface pH at 480 min)

tablet_properties.weight_variation_percent: max SD 2.6 mg, average weight range 345.7-354.9 mg (approx 0.75% variability)

experimental_data.gel_layer_pH: Measured hourly for 8h using contact pH electrode. At pH1.2, all samples pH 1.23-1.65. After change to pH6.8, sample R pH 6.54-6.76; acid-containing samples maintained lower pH. Citric acid samples showed sharp pH increase after 360 min due to complete leaching.

statistical_analysis.full_factorial: Concentration of pH modifier (p<0.01) and type (p<0.05) significantly affected drug release and surface pH at 360 and 480 min.
statistical_analysis.PLS_2_regression: First two PLS components explained 75% (X-data) and 73% (Y-data) variation. Concentration (p<0.01) positively correlated with drug release, negatively with pH at 360 min. pKa (p<0.01) crucial for drug release and pH at 360 min. Solubility only affected pH at 480 min (p<0.05).

data.article_summary: Development of 5-aminosalicylic acid (5-ASA) colonic delivery pellets using alginate, calcium, and Eudragit® RS via extrusion-spheronization, with curing and coating to control drug release.

materials_origin.Eudragit_RS_PO: Evonic Degussa (India Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India), Mw ~150 kDa

experimental_design.optimization_approach: Variation of proportions of Eudragit RS, calcium acetate, and alginate while keeping 5-ASA and Avicel fixed at 20% each. Nine formulations (119_F1-119_F9) prepared and evaluated for morphology, mechanical properties, drug release. Curing (50°C, 5h) and coating (Eudragit RS, 5% and 10% weight gain) applied to selected formulations.

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Dry components mixed for 8 min, water added as granulating agent, extruded through 1mm die at 100 rpm, spheronized at 1000 rpm for 2 min. Half cured at 50°C for 5h, half dried at room temperature.

equations.mean_dissolution_time_MDT: MDT = Σ(T_i ΔM_i) / ΣM_i, where T_i = (t_i + t_{i+1})/2, ΔM_i = M_{i+1} - M_i

independent_variables.levels_percent: See formulation_details; ranges: Eudragit RS 0-50%, calcium acetate 0-45%, alginate 0-45%

ftir_info.sample_preparation: Homogeneous powder of raw materials and formulations
ftir_info.conclusion: No chemical interaction between drug and polymers in physical mixture, cured or uncured pellets.

equipment.extruder: Axial extruder (Malvern, UK), die 1 mm thickness, perforations 1 mm diameter, speed 100 rpm
equipment.spheronizer: Dorsa HC 732 (Iran), cross-hatched plate, 1000 rpm for 2 min

process_conditions.coating_solution: 30% w/v Eudragit RS PO in isopropyl alcohol:water (9:1), triethyl citrate 10% (w/w), talc 5% (w/w)

release_properties.release_mechanism: For uncoated pellets: diffusion and swelling/erosion. For coated pellets: diffusion through coating layer, with pH-dependent swelling of Ca-alginate creating channels.
release_properties.factors_influencing_release: pH (lower release at acidic pH, higher at pH 6.8), curing (reduces porosity and initial burst), coating level (higher coating reduces release), calcium and alginate content (higher amounts increase swelling and release at pH 6.8).

pellet_properties.hardness: Reported as crushing strength (N) in pre_compression_parameters table
pellet_properties.disintegration_time: Not reported; pellets are matrix systems, not disintegrating
pellet_properties.dissolution_profile: See Figures 5 and 6. Summarized: uncured and cured pellets show rapid release in pH 6.8; coated pellets (especially 119_F5 with 10% coating) show minimal release in pH 1.2 and sustained release in pH 6.8.
pellet_properties.thickness_diameter: Size range 500-1000 μm; individual dimensions not reported
pellet_properties.swelling_index: Qualitative observation via SEM; no quantitative index reported.
pellet_properties.lag_time: Not reported for coated pellets; in continuous dissolution, 119_F5 (10%) showed negligible release during first 2 h (acidic), then gradual release.

experimental_data.key_results: 119_F5 formulation (25% Eudragit RS, 17.5% calcium acetate, 17.5% alginate, 20% 5-ASA, 20% Avicel) with 10% coating level provided optimal colonic delivery: minimal drug release in pH 1.2 and sustained release in pH 6.8.

performance_metrics.release_in_alkaline_pH: Sustained release, suitable for colonic pH <7.0

experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Unpaired t-test for mechanical properties (cured vs uncured) at α=0.05; no significant difference for most formulations except 119_F4 and 119_F7 where curing increased hardness and elastic modulus (p<0.05).

figure_6a_release_in_0.1N_HCl.description: Release of 5-ASA from coated pellets in 0.1 N HCl over 120 minutes. Coating levels: 5% and 10% w/w.

figure_6b_release_in_phosphate_buffer_pH.description: Release of 5-ASA from coated pellets in phosphate buffer pH 6.8 over 120 minutes (as referenced in text; full profile up to 10h but key 120 min values provided).

figure_6c_continuous_release.description: Continuous dissolution test: first 2 hours in 0.1 N HCl (pH 1.2) followed by 10 hours in phosphate buffer pH 6.8. Total 12 hours.

formulations.notes: Acidic phase (0-2h): ~10% release; after switch to pH 6.8, rapid release reaching 84% at 2h into buffer (4h total), then plateau.
Acidic phase (0-2h): ~4% release; sustained release in pH 6.8, below 40% at 2h into buffer (4h total), reaching ~38% at 12h.
Acidic phase (0-2h): ~5% release; slow release in pH 6.8, only 36% total at end of colonic phase.
Acidic phase (0-2h): ~2% release; very slow release, ~30% total at 12h.

article_title: Use of hydrophilic and hydrophobic polymers for the development of controlled release tizanidine matrix tablets

data.article_title: Use of hydrophilic and hydrophobic polymers for the development of controlled release tizanidine matrix tablets

parameters.calculated_D_t: 30 mg tizanidine base, equivalent to 34 mg tizanidine hydrochloride per tablet

figure_1_hydration_data.description: Hydration behavior (swelling %) over time for tizanidine matrix formulations. Swelling (%) = (Wt - Wo)/Wo * 100. Data extracted from Figure 1.

figure_2_release_0.1N_HCl.description: Drug release profiles in 0.1 N HCl, USP apparatus II, 100 rpm, 37°C. Cumulative percent release over time.

figure_3_release_pH4.5.description: Drug release profiles in phosphate buffer pH 4.5, USP apparatus II, 100 rpm, 37°C. Cumulative percent release over time.

swelling_study.description: Swelling studies performed in distilled water at room temperature. Results shown in Figure 1. HPMC formulations showed highest hydration ability, followed by Kollidon SR, then Ethocel. Swelling increased with polymer concentration and with higher viscosity grade (K100M > K4M).

key_findings.E10ST_formulations: Unable to control release; nearly 100% released within 4 hours.
key_findings.low_polymer_formulations: Polymer ≤25% (K4M5, K100M5, E10FP3, 120_KSR5): >90% released in 4 hours.
key_findings.polymer_30%: K4M3, K100M3, KSR3: ~80% released; E10FP9: 76% released.
key_findings.controlled_release_formulations: K4M2 (50% polymer), K100M9 (40% polymer), E10FP2 (40% polymer), 120_KSR2 (55% polymer) showed: 45-53% in 4h, 82-85% in 8h, 94-99% in 24h.

process_parameters.compression_device: Manually operated single punch tablet press (Korsch Erweka, Frankfurt, Germany)
process_parameters.compression_pressure_range: Not specified, but tablet weights ranged 96-126 mg
process_parameters.blending_method: Tumbling method in a polybag for 8-10 minutes

data.conclusion: Controlled release tizanidine HCl tablets successfully prepared with HPMC K4M (50%), HPMC K100M (40%), Ethocel 10FP (40%), and Kollidon SR (55%). Formulation K100M9 (HPMC K100M 40%) was best with highest shelf life (30.35 months) and zero-order release kinetics via anomalous transport.

Vaginal Chitosan Tablets with Clotrimazole—Design and Evaluation of Mucoadhesive Properties Using Porcine Vaginal Mucosa, Mucin and Gelatine

article_summary.title: Vaginal Chitosan Tablets with Clotrimazole—Design and Evaluation of Mucoadhesive Properties Using Porcine Vaginal Mucosa, Mucin and Gelatine
article_summary.authors: Emilia Szymańska, Katarzyna Winnicka, Aleksandra Amelian, Urszula Cwalina
article_summary.objective: Develop mucoadhesive vaginal tablets with clotrimazole using chitosan to prolong drug residence time.

excipients.Prosolv_SMCC_50: silicified microcrystalline cellulose, JRS Pharma, Rosenberg, Germany
excipients.Pruv: sodium stearyl fumarate, JRS Pharma, Rosenberg, Germany
excipients.Tablettose_80: spray-dried lactose, Cardinal Pharma Ltd., Lodz, Poland

porcine_vaginal_mucosa.source: Large White Pigs (~200 kg) from Bost slaughterhouse (Turosn Koscielna, Poland)
porcine_vaginal_mucosa.preparation: Frozen at -20°C immediately after sacrifice, thawed at ambient temperature, cut into 2 cm long, 2 mm thick pieces

other_chemicals.buffers: sodium acetate, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, sodium hydroxide, disodium hydrogen phosphate from Chempur
other_chemicals.water: distilled, passed through Milli-Q Reagent Water System

experimental_design_for_optimization.method: Preliminary experiments with constant drug and chitosan amounts, varying excipient blends to determine suitable weight ratio. Direct compression used.
experimental_design_for_optimization.optimization_criteria: Mechanical stability, mucoadhesive properties, prolonged release.

ingredients_identification.preparation_of_tablets: Direct compression using single punch eccentric tablet press EP-1 with 12 mm flat-faced punches at 20 rpm. Blended powder mixed in mortar, sieved through 300 μm sieve.

HPLC.conditions: Isocratic, Zorbax Eclipse XDB-C18 column (4.6×150 mm, 5 μm), mobile phase methanol-phosphate buffer pH 7.4 (4:1 v/v), flow rate 1.0 mL/min, UV detection 210 nm, column temp 25°C, injection 20 μL, retention time 5.2 min, calibration 1-100 μg/mL

characterization_methods.SEM: Hirox SH-3000 mini-SEM, 15 kV, back-scattered electrons, low vacuum

pre_compression_parameters.note: Data not explicitly reported for powder blends; only post-compression tablet properties available.

fixed_parameters.excipient_ratio: kept unalterable relative to drug and chitosan

tablet_fabrications.equipment: Single punch eccentric tablet press EP-1 (Erweka, Heusenstamm, Germany)
tablet_fabrications.friability: Electrolab EF-1W friabilator, 25 rpm for 100 revolutions (4 min)
tablet_fabrications.drug_content: HPLC after extraction in methanol for 24h at 25±0.5°C, 250 rpm

process_parameters.compression_device: Single punch eccentric tablet press EP-1
process_parameters.compression_pressure: Not specified (adjusted to achieve target hardness)

batch_F1.release_profile: Almost 100% CLO released within first hour, similar to commercial control
batch_F1.release_mechanism: Not kinetically evaluated (out of targeted profile limits)
batch_F1.factors_influencing: Low chitosan (10%) leads to rapid erosion and drug dissolution

batch_F2.release_profile: Retarded up to 6 h, ~80% released at 4 h
batch_F2.release_mechanism: Anomalous diffusion (non-Fickian), combination of diffusion and swelling control
batch_F2.factors_influencing: Chitosan hydration and gel formation in acidic pH (4.5), slower disintegration

batch_F3.release_profile: Retarded up to 6 h, ~80% released at 5 h
batch_F3.factors_influencing: Higher chitosan (40%) forms compact gel layer, further retards release

batch_F1.disintegration_time: Not directly measured; swelling study showed 50% weight loss after 2h

batch_F2.disintegration_time: Not directly; swelling index max 5.45 at 30 min, compact during study

batch_F3.disintegration_time: Not directly; swelling index max 3.81 at 30 min, compact

swelling_study.method: Tablets placed in 5 mL 0.08 M acetic buffer pH 4.5 at 37±0.5°C, weighed at intervals

results.121_F1: Rapid initial swelling, 50% weight loss after 2h due to erosion
results.121_F2: Highest swelling index (SI) 5.45 at 30 min; compact, slight drop at end

residence_time.method: Modified USP disintegration tester, porcine vaginal mucosa, pH 4.5 buffer, 37°C
residence_time.results: 121_F2 and 121_F3 significantly longer than control and 121_F1; correlated with chitosan concentration (Fig. 5A)

mucoadhesive_properties.method: Texture analyser TA.XT.Plus, 5 kg load cell, contact force 1 N for 100 s, separation rate 0.1 mm/s
mucoadhesive_properties.substrates: Porcine vaginal mucosa, 30% gelatin discs, 10% mucin gel on cellulose

results.121_F2: Intermediate; plateau in mucoadhesion (not significantly different from 121_F3)
results.121_F3: Highest values: F_max = 0.747 ± 0.188 N (median 0.671), W_ad = 1021.7 ± 365 μJ (median 961.3) on porcine mucosa

mucoadhesive_properties.note: Gelatine discs showed better correlation to porcine mucosa and higher reproducibility than mucin gel.

statistical_analysis.key_findings: No significant difference in mucoadhesion between 121_F2 and 121_F3 (p > 0.05); 121_F2 and 121_F3 significantly better than control and 121_F1

performance_metrics.best_formulation: 121_F2 (25% chitosan) – best balance of mucoadhesion, sustained release, and mechanical properties.
performance_metrics.commercial_comparison: 121_F2 and 121_F3 provide prolonged residence time and retarded release vs. commercial vaginal tablets.

article_title: Variables Influencing Drug Release from Layered Matrix System Comprising Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose

data.article_title: Variables Influencing Drug Release from Layered Matrix System Comprising Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose
data.abstract: The purpose of this study was to prepare and evaluate layered matrix tablets of propranolol HCl containing HPMC and phytowax as matrix component using direct compression technique. Layering with this polymeric matrix could prolong the release of drug and shift the release pattern approach to zero order as described from the least square curve fitting. Increasing the amount of coating layer could apparently prolong the drug release. The longer lag time of drug release from one planar apparently when the amount of coating layer was increased. HPMC concentration and compression force did not affect the drug release from this three-layer tablet. The drug release from this three-layer tablet was influenced by hydrodynamic force. An increase in stirring rate was a corresponding increasing in the release rate. From photomage and SEM, gel mass of HPMC was increased with time during dissolution and covered the core surface, therefore dissolved drug molecules were allowed to diffuse out from the core through the polymer network of gel layer containing the porous structure. This suggested that HPMC and phytowax could be fabricated into the layered matrix tablet exhibiting sustained drug release.
data.introduction: Geometric modulation of matrix systems has been employed to achieve zero order drug release. Three-layer matrix tablets with hydrophilic barrier layers can provide linear release profiles. HPMC barriers are efficient in reducing drug delivery. Variables such as diluents, compression force, agitation intensity, hydrodynamic force, and pH influence drug release. This study evaluates the effect of coating barrier amount, polymer concentration, compression force, and pH on propranolol HCl release from three-layer tablets comprising HPMC K15M and phytowax.

materials_origin.Propranolol hydrochloride: China National Chemical Imp. & Exp., Shanghai, China (batch no. 941002)
materials_origin.Magnesium stearate: P.C. Drug Center Co. (lot no. MAF 07), passed through sieve no. 80 mesh
materials_origin.Phytowax (Phytowax Olive 14L 48, hydroge: Sophim, Parc de la Cassine, France, passed through sieve No. 60

independent_variables: Amount of coating barrier (100, 150, 200 mg per side)
pH of dissolution media (distilled water, HCl buffer pH 1.2, phosphate buffer pH 6.8, pH change from 1.2 to 6.8)

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Direct compression using hydraulic press. Single layer tablets (200 mg) with drug. Two-layer and three-layer tablets by adding barrier layers (without drug) on one or both sides of core.
ingredients_identification.results: Three-layer tablets exhibited prolonged release approaching zero order. Increased barrier amount increased lag time and decreased release rate. HPMC concentration and compression force had no significant effect. Higher stirring rate increased release rate. pH dependence observed (fastest in distilled water).
ingredients_identification.equation: Power law: M_t/M_inf = k * t^n; Zero order: M_t/M_inf = k0 * t

formulation_details.barrier_composition_base: Powder mixture without drug containing 2% magnesium stearate and variable HPMC/phytowax ratios. Total barrier weight per side varies (100, 150, 200 mg). For a given HPMC percentage, the remaining mass (98% minus HPMC%) is phytowax.

experimental_data.key_results: Fast release, not zero order
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Curve fitting to power law (r2=0.9995)

ingredients.core: Same as H35% 1L (200 mg, 80 mg drug)
ingredients.barrier_layer: One side, composition: 35% HPMC, 63% phytowax, 2% Mg stearate. Weight ~201 mg

experimental_data.key_results: Slower release than single layer

batches.description: Three-layer tablet with barriers 100 mg each side, core 200 mg, HPMC 35% in barriers

ingredients.core: Same as H35% 1L (200 mg, 80 mg drug)
ingredients.barrier_upper: 100 mg, composition: 35% HPMC (35 mg), 63% phytowax (63 mg), 2% Mg stearate (2 mg)

experimental_data.key_results: Zero-order release, lag time observed in one-planar study
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: High r2 for zero order (0.9994)
experimental_data.swelling_study: Gel formation increased with time, erosion after 26h

batches.description: Three-layer tablet with barriers 150 mg each side, core 200 mg, HPMC 35%

ingredients.barrier_upper: 150 mg (35% HPMC = 52.5 mg, 63% phytowax = 94.5 mg, 2% Mg stearate = 3 mg)

experimental_data.key_results: Increased barrier thickness further prolongs release

batches.description: Three-layer tablet with barriers 200 mg each side, core 200 mg, HPMC 35%, compression force 1.5 tons

ingredients.barrier_upper: 200 mg (70 mg HPMC, 126 mg phytowax, 4 mg Mg stearate)

experimental_data.key_results: Most prolonged release, influenced by stirring rate
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: r2=0.9955 (power law)
experimental_data.swelling_study: Gel formation, wax particles visible after erosion

batches.description: Three-layer tablet with barriers containing 0% HPMC (only phytowax and Mg stearate), core same (35% HPMC)

ingredients.barrier_upper: 100 mg (0% HPMC, 98% phytowax = 98 mg, 2% Mg stearate = 2 mg)

experimental_data.key_results: Lowest friability (0.22%), faster release due to no HPMC in barriers
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: r2=0.9983 power law

batches.description: Three-layer tablet with barriers containing 15% HPMC

ingredients.barrier_upper: 100 mg (15% HPMC = 15 mg, 83% phytowax = 83 mg, 2% Mg stearate = 2 mg)

experimental_data.key_results: Higher HPMC in barrier slows release

batches.description: Three-layer tablet with barriers containing 25% HPMC

ingredients.barrier_upper: 100 mg (25% HPMC = 25 mg, 73% phytowax = 73 mg, 2% Mg stearate = 2 mg)

release_properties.release_profile: Slower than H15%, faster than H35%

experimental_data.key_results: Increased HPMC decreases release rate

batches.description: Three-layer tablet with barriers 200 mg each, compression force 1 ton
batches.ingredients: Same as H35% 3L 2/2/2 (core 200 mg, barriers 200 mg each, 35% HPMC in barriers)

tablet_properties.dissolution_profile: Zero order, slightly higher rate than 1.5 tons

experimental_data.key_results: No significant difference from 1.5 tons

batches.description: Three-layer tablet with barriers 200 mg each, compression force 1.5 tons (duplicate of H35% 3L 2/2/2)
Three-layer tablet with barriers 200 mg each, compression force 1.75 tons

experimental_data.key_results: Increased hardness but no change in release

batches.description: Three-layer tablet with barriers 200 mg each, compression force 2 tons

tablet_properties.dissolution_profile: Slightly lower rate but not significant

experimental_data.key_results: Highest hardness, lowest friability, no effect on release

materials_and_methods.fourier_transform_infrared_spectral_meas: Not performed / not mentioned in the article.

tablet_fabrication_general.friability: Friabilitor (Yieheng Engineering, Bangkok, Thailand), 20 tablets, 100 revolutions in 4 min
tablet_fabrication_general.thickness_and_diameter: Thickness tester (Teclock, Japan), diameter 12 mm round flat punches
tablet_fabrication_general.drug_content: Not directly measured; theoretical 80 mg per tablet based on core weight

process_parameters_general.coating_method: Layering of barrier powder mixture without drug, compressed sequentially
process_parameters_general.equipment: 12 mm round flat punches, dwell time 10 s after target pressure
process_parameters_general.dissolution_apparatus: Basket method (type 1; Prolabo, France), rotational speeds 50, 100, 150 rpm
process_parameters_general.dissolution_medium: Distilled water (mainly), also HCl buffer pH 1.2, phosphate buffer pH 6.8, pH change

release_properties_general.release_mechanism: Case II transport or zero-order for three-layer tablets with HPMC; Fickian for single/double layer or H0% barriers
release_properties_general.release_profile: Sustained over 26 h for three-layer tablets, zero-order approach
release_properties_general.factors_influencing: Hydrodynamic force (increased rpm increases release), pH (fastest in distilled water due to common ion effect in HCl), barrier amount (increased barrier prolongs release and lag time). HPMC concentration and compression force have no significant effect.

tablet_properties_general.hardness_range_Kp: 3.21 to 12.54 depending on layers and compression force
tablet_properties_general.dissolution_profile: Zero order for three-layer with HPMC barriers

experimental_data_general.key_results: Three-layer HPMC/phytowax tablets provide near zero-order release of propranolol HCl. Increasing barrier thickness increases lag time and decreases release rate. HPMC concentration in barrier (0-35%) affects release rate but not zero-order linearity. Compression force (1-2 tons) has no effect. Release is pH-dependent and influenced by agitation speed.
experimental_data_general.performance_metrics: Best zero-order fit for H35% 3L 1/2/1 (r2=0.9994, rate 1.36%/h).
experimental_data_general.statistical_analysis: Power law and zero order fitting with Scientist for Windows, using r2 and MSC criteria.
experimental_data_general.zero_order_rates: See Table II and batch-specific data.
experimental_data_general.first_order: Not reported; zero order and power law used.
experimental_data_general.swelling_study: Photoimages and SEM show gel formation over time, erosion after 26 h, wax particles exposed. No quantitative swelling index.
experimental_data_general.lag_time_min: From one-planar study: For 100 mg barrier ~120-180 min, 150 mg ~300-360 min, 200 mg ~480-540 min.
experimental_data_general.total_floating_time_h: Not applicable (tablets sink? Not mentioned floating).

data.results_and_discussion: The physical properties of tablets showed increased hardness with more coating layers and higher compression force. Friability decreased with compression force. Drug release from single layer without HPMC was immediate; addition of HPMC slowed release. Two- and three-layer tablets further prolonged release. Increasing barrier amount increased lag time and decreased release rate. HPMC concentration and compression force did not significantly affect release. Higher stirring rate increased release due to hydrodynamic effects. Release was pH-dependent: fastest in distilled water, slower in HCl buffer (common ion effect) and phosphate buffer. SEM and photoimages showed gel layer formation and erosion over time. Curve fitting indicated zero-order or Case II release for three-layer tablets with HPMC barriers.
data.conclusion: Layering with HPMC and phytowax prolongs propranolol HCl release and shifts release to zero order. Increasing barrier amount prolongs release. Drug release is influenced by hydrodynamic force and pH. HPMC concentration and compression force do not affect release from three-layer tablets.

article_title: Design and evaluation of effervescent floating tablets based on hydroxyethyl cellulose and sodium alginate using pentoxifylline as a model drug

article_metadata.title: Design and evaluation of effervescent floating tablets based on hydroxyethyl cellulose and sodium alginate using pentoxifylline as a model drug
article_metadata.overall_conclusion: Effervescent floating matrix tablets containing pentoxifylline with HEC and sodium alginate were successfully prepared; formulations with 20% w/w sodium bicarbonate at 50–54 N hardness showed acceptable floating, swelling, and sustained release over 24 h in 0.1 N HCl (pH 1.2). [file:1]

sections_location.introduction: After abstract, describes gastroretentive systems and rationale for pentoxifylline and floating tablets. [file:1]

materials_and_methods.materials: Subsection 'Materials' lists origin of pentoxifylline, sodium alginate, sodium bicarbonate, Prosolv 90, hydroxyethyl cellulose (Natrosol 250-HHX), magnesium stearate. [file:1]
materials_and_methods.granulation_and_tablet_preparation: Subsections 'Granulation', 'Powder mixtures and granules evaluation', 'Tablets preparation', and 'Evaluation of tablets'. [file:1]
materials_and_methods.FTIR_DSC: Subsections 'Differential scanning calorimetry study' and 'Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy'. [file:1]
materials_and_methods.in_vitro_tests: Subsections on 'Tablet floating capacity', 'Swelling and erosion studies', 'In vitro drug release studies', 'Release data modeling and analysis', and 'Statistical analysis'. [file:1]

sections_location.results_and_discussion: From 'Results and discussion' onward: moisture content, Carr’s index, DSC, FTIR, physical properties, floating, swelling, erosion, dissolution, and modeling. [file:1]
sections_location.conclusion: Final 'Conclusion' section summarizing optimal formulation and behavior. [file:1]

properties_mentioned.half_life: Approximately 1–2 h (short half-life) used as model highly water-soluble, high-density drug
properties_mentioned.dose_per_tablet: 60 mg per tablet for both 123_F1 and 123_F2

sodium_alginate.particle_size_used: 350 µm cut to improve compression compared with 180 µm cut

sodium_bicarbonate.role: Effervescent gas-forming agent to generate CO2 for floating

experimental_design_for_optimization.objective: To design and evaluate effervescent floating gastro-retentive matrix tablets with sustained release using mixtures of hydroxyethyl cellulose and sodium alginate, and to study the effects of granulation, sodium bicarbonate level, and tablet hardness. [file:1]

independent_variables.name: tablet_hardness
levels_N: 59–64 (Level C; attainable only from powder, not from granules for 123_F1/123_F2)

independent_variables.name: production_route
independent_variables.purpose: To compare flow, density, floating capacity, and release behavior before and after granulation.

responses_measured: Moisture content and Carr’s index (flow properties)
Tablet hardness, friability, weight uniformity, content uniformity
Floating lag time and total floating duration
Swelling and erosion (% medium uptake, % mass loss)
Dissolution profile (% drug released vs time in 0.1 N HCl, pH 1.2, 24 h)
Release kinetics (Korsmeyer–Peppas model parameters n and Kp)
Statistical significance via paired t-test and ANOVA

tests: post hoc multiple comparisons when needed

powder_preparation.sieving: All powders passed through 180 µm sieve; sodium alginate used at 350 µm cut to mitigate compression problems. [file:1]
powder_preparation.mixing: Turbula mixer at 60 rpm for 10 min for powder blends. [file:1]

wet_granulation.binder: 0.5% w/w water added to powder mixture
wet_granulation.equipment: Kenwood Chef/Kneader mixer, 10 min mixing, mass passed through 1000 µm sieve
wet_granulation.drying: Drying oven at 60 °C overnight; then granules collected at 853 µm size fraction

granule_evaluation.moisture_content: Measured using Mettler Toledo HG53 halogen moisture analyzer; values reported as mean ± SD for 123_F1 and 123_F2 pre- and post-granulation. [file:1]
granule_evaluation.flow_properties: Carr’s index calculated from bulk and tapped densities using tapping apparatus (Copley JV1000). [file:1]

UV_calibration.method: UV–Vis spectrophotometry at 274 nm in 0.1 N HCl for pentoxifylline; calibration curve prepared with triplicate measurements. [file:1]

123_F1.description: Floating matrix tablet with 10% w/w sodium bicarbonate.

composition_per_tablet_mg.magnesium_stearate: small amount to reach total tablet weight; difference due to lubricant content

approximate_fraction_of_tablet_mass.sodium_bicarbonate_percent: 10% w/w relative to matrix composition as stated by authors

123_F2.description: Floating matrix tablet with 20% w/w sodium bicarbonate.

composition_per_tablet_mg.magnesium_stearate: small amount to reach total tablet weight; difference due to lubricant content

approximate_fraction_of_tablet_mass.sodium_bicarbonate_percent: 20% w/w relative to matrix composition as stated by authors

nonfloating_control.description: Similar HEC–sodium alginate matrix tablets without sodium bicarbonate (0% w/w) used as control in swelling, erosion, and release modeling.
nonfloating_control.detailed_composition: Not fully tabulated in text; assumed to contain same polymer–filler–drug ratios as 123_F1/123_F2 without gas-former.

carr_index_F1.granules: Improved to fair flow (~17%; significant improvement, P < 0.05)

design_matrix.floating: Floating formulations when NaHCO3 used; 123_F1 tablets floated for 8–12 h depending on route and hardness.
123_F2 tablets floated for 24 h at both hardness levels for granule-based and powder-based tablets.
Nonfloating, used for comparison of swelling and erosion.

ftir_measurements.instrument: Perkin Elmer FT-IR system, Spectrum BX series
ftir_measurements.sample_presentation: Small quantity placed on sample stage and compressed by twisting arm; spectra acquired via Spectrum BX software. [file:1]

compatibility_results.F1_F2_powder_and_granules: Pentoxifylline characteristic peaks appeared at similar wavenumbers in mixtures and granules, indicating no chemical incompatibility with excipients.
compatibility_results.conclusion: No new peaks or major shifts indicative of strong interactions; formulation components are compatible.

equipment.tablet_press: Single-punch tableting machine Type 3, Manesty Machines Ltd, UK, with flat-faced 9.60 mm punches.
equipment.dissolution_apparatus: USP Apparatus II, Erweka GmbH, Germany.

consolidation_index_porosity.apparent_density: Increased with hardness and decreased after granulation, depending on formulation and NaHCO3 level. [file:1]
consolidation_index_porosity.porosity_percent: Decreased with higher hardness and with granulation; higher NaHCO3 reduced porosity further.

compression_process.machine: Single-punch Manesty Type 3, flat-faced 9.60 mm tools.
compression_process.process: Automatic compression from granules and manual compression from powder blends to achieve desired hardness levels.
compression_process.hardness_control: Adjusted via compression force to reach levels A–C; level C not achievable post-granulation for 123_F1/123_F2 due to elastic recovery of sodium alginate.

coating.status: No tablet coating reported; tablets are uncoated matrices.

environment_conditions.FTIR_conditions: Room temperature; spectra collected from 4000–620 cm−1.
environment_conditions.dissolution_temperature: 37 ± 0.5 °C in 0.1 N HCl (pH 1.2), paddle speed 50 rpm.

medium_and_conditions.sink_conditions: Maintained; solubility of pentoxifylline is high relative to dose.

F1_granule_tablets.total_floating_duration_h: ≈8 h (123_F1 granule tablets sank before 24 h).

level_A.mechanism: Anomalous (non-Fickian) transport with contributions from diffusion and polymer relaxation.
Anomalous (non-Fickian) with more pronounced polymer relaxation.

release_kinetics_summary.overall: Most granule-based formulations (floating and nonfloating) exhibit non-Fickian (anomalous) release as per the Korsmeyer–Peppas model, indicating combined diffusion and polymer relaxation/erosion. Increasing hardness tends to reduce Kp for powder-based tablets; granulation generally lowers Kp and increases n. [file:1]
release_kinetics_summary.zero_first_order: The article focuses on Korsmeyer–Peppas modeling rather than fitting detailed zero-order/first-order parameters; numeric zero/first-order constants are not tabulated.

swelling_water_uptake.method: Determined by medium uptake (DMU) at time intervals up to 24 h; tablets withdrawn, blotted, weighed wet, then dried at 60 °C to constant weight.

results_summary.trend_F2: Higher NaHCO3 (20% w/w) produced lower net swelling but higher mass loss due to more effervescence and longer floating, compared with nonfloating tablets and F1.
results_summary.trend_F1: 123_F1 granule tablets showed higher medium uptake near 24 h than 123_F2 when they had sunk and all surfaces were exposed.
results_summary.nonfloating: Nonfloating tablets showed highest swelling but lowest mass loss, as they remained fully submerged without effervescence.

tablet_mechanical_properties.hardness: All 123_F1/123_F2 granule tablets at levels A and B showed hardness around 5–6 kg with low variability (SD < 0.5 kg). [file:1]
tablet_mechanical_properties.friability: All <1% weight loss, meeting BP requirements; 123_F2 slightly higher friability at level A due to higher NaHCO3 content.
tablet_mechanical_properties.weight_variation: Twenty tablets tested for each formulation; deviations within BP limits.
tablet_mechanical_properties.thickness_and_diameter: Diameter fixed at 9.60 mm; thickness decreased slightly with increased hardness.

dissolution_profile.qualitative_F1: Granule-based 123_F1 tablets at level A released drug more slowly than powder-based 123_F1 at same hardness; increasing hardness reduced release rate. 123_F1 provided sustained release up to 24 h but with incomplete floating duration.
dissolution_profile.qualitative_F2: Granule-based 123_F2 at level A showed slower release than 123_F1 and nonfloating tablets due to longer floating and lower swelling. Increasing NaHCO3 from 10 to 20% decreased release rate for granule-based tablets but increased it for powder-based tablets (due to pore formation from gas).

stability.formal_stability_study: Not reported; study focuses on immediate post-manufacture performance, not long-term stability.

examples: Granulation significantly reduced moisture content and Carr’s index (P < 0.05) for both 123_F1 and F2.
Granulation significantly affected apparent density, porosity, floating lag time, and release profile in many cases (P < 0.05).
Raising NaHCO3 from 10 to 20% significantly changed floating lag time and drug release profiles for powder-based tablets; effect was attenuated after granulation.
Korsmeyer–Peppas fits showed R² values mostly >0.98, supporting the chosen model.

article_title: Formulation and evaluation of sustained release matrix tablet of rabeprazole using wet granulation technique

article_metadata.title: Formulation and evaluation of sustained release matrix tablet of rabeprazole using wet granulation technique
article_metadata.journal: Journal of Pharmacy and Bioallied Sciences

api.name: Rabeprazole
api.supplier: Elder Pharmaceuticals Pvt Ltd, Dehradun, India
api.grade: Not explicitly stated; gift sample; assumed pharmaceutical grade

polymers.name: Hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC E-15)
polymers.supplier: Elder Pharmaceuticals Pvt Ltd, Dehradun, India

polymers.name: Carbopol 934
polymers.supplier: Elder Pharmaceuticals Pvt Ltd, Dehradun, India

polymers.name: Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (Sodium CMC)
polymers.supplier: Elder Pharmaceuticals Pvt Ltd, Dehradun, India

excipients.name: Talc
excipients.supplier: S.D. Fine Chem. Ltd., Mumbai, India

excipients.name: Magnesium stearate
excipients.supplier: S.D. Fine Chem. Ltd., Mumbai, India

excipients.name: Lactose
excipients.grade: Analytical grade (implied: 'All the chemicals were of analytical grade')

materials_origin.general_note: All chemicals used were of analytical grade.

experimental_design_for_optimization.description: Six sustained-release matrix tablet formulations (124_F1–124_F6) were prepared using wet granulation, varying type and concentration of hydrophilic polymers (HPMC E-15, Carbopol 934, Sodium CMC) and lactose filler to study their effect on rabeprazole release over 10 h.
experimental_design_for_optimization.design_type: Simple comparative formulation study (no formal factorial/RSM optimization reported).
independent_variables: Polymer type: HPMC E-15, Carbopol 934, Sodium CMC
dependent_variables: Flow properties (angle of repose, densities, Carr's index)
experimental_design_for_optimization.optimization_note: No statistical optimization (e.g., design of experiments, regression models) is described; conclusions are based on comparative release profiles and qualitative assessment.

ftir_identification.purpose: Identification of pure rabeprazole and compatibility with polymers.
ftir_identification.method_summary: FT-IR spectrum of rabeprazole was recorded and compared with reference spectrum; mixture spectra (drug + polymers) were examined for interaction.
ftir_identification.results_summary: Wave numbers of pure drug and drug–excipient mixtures were reported as almost similar, indicating no significant interaction and compatibility.
section_sources: Results and Discussion – FT-IR compatibility paragraph

uv_vis_assay_and_calibration.assay_medium: Phosphate buffer pH 7.2 (for estimation of rabeprazole sodium)
uv_vis_assay_and_calibration.assay_description: Rabeprazole sodium dissolved in phosphate buffer pH 7.2 to obtain 10 µg/mL solution, further diluted and scanned between 200–400 nm on a double-beam UV–VIS spectrophotometer using buffer as blank to determine λmax.

calibration_curve_water.stock_solution: 25 mg rabeprazole in 100 mL volumetric flask, volume made up with distilled water.
calibration_curve_water.regression_equation_units: A: absorbance at 287 nm; C: concentration in µg/mL

drug_content_assay_in_tablets.procedure_summary: Three tablets from each batch were placed in 100 mL volumetric flask, volume made up with 0.1 N HCl, kept for 48 h, 1 mL withdrawn, filtered, suitably diluted and analyzed spectrophotometrically (wavelength not re-stated, implied use of same assay).
drug_content_assay_in_tablets.calculation_details: Not fully specified; assumes use of calibration curve/standard absorbance at 287 nm.

formulation_details_per_batch.note: Values are per tablet in mg as given in Table 2; '-' entries in the article are treated as 0 mg.

pre_compression_parameters.note: The article reports angle of repose, bulk density, tapped density, and Carr’s index for granules/tablets by batch; no standard deviations are provided.

independent_variables_and_levels.description: Independent formulation variables are primarily polymer type and polymer amount per tablet; lactose serves as diluent.

sample_types: Physical mixtures of rabeprazole with HPMC E-15, Carbopol 934, Sodium CMC (exact ratios not specified)
key_observations: Characteristic peaks of rabeprazole were retained in mixtures.
No significant shifts or disappearance of major peaks, indicating absence of strong drug–polymer interaction.

fabrication_process.particle_sizing: All polymers and active ingredients passed through sieve no. 80; wet granules passed through sieve no. 20.
fabrication_process.drying_conditions: Granules dried in hot air oven at 45 °C.
fabrication_process.lubrication: Dried granules mixed with magnesium stearate and talc.

compression.tablet_shape_and_size: Not specified; described only as 'desired hardness, shape, and size'.
compression.coating: No tablet coating described; matrix tablets appear to be uncoated.

friability.method: Roche friabilator, approximately 6 g dedusted tablets, 100 free falls of 6 inches at 25 rpm.

weight_variation.method: 20 tablets weighed individually, average weight calculated; deviation expressed as % weight variation.

thickness.method: Crown-to-crown thickness of ten tablets measured using slide calipers.

density_of_formulation.description: Apparent density of cylindrical tablets computed from mass and geometric volume (V = π r^2 h).
density_of_formulation.reported_as: Bulk density and tapped density (g/cm3) in Table 3.

drug_content.method: Three tablets per batch dissolved in 0.1 N HCl, kept for 48 h; aliquot filtered, diluted, and analyzed by UV spectrophotometry.

consolidation_index.definition: Carr's index % = (tapped density - bulk density) / tapped density × 100.
consolidation_index.reported_as: Carr’s index % per batch in Table 3.

compression_process.equipment: Automatic tablet punching machine (model not specified).
compression_process.coating: No coating mentioned; tablets treated as uncoated matrices.

granulation.sieving_before_granulation: All ingredients passed through sieve no. 80.
granulation.sieving_after_granulation: Wet granules passed through sieve no. 20.
granulation.binder: Not explicitly specified; water or polymer dispersion assumed but not described.

medium_stage_1.pH: Acidic (exact pH not numerically stated; typical 1.2).

medium_stage_2.pH: Not explicitly stated for dissolution; phosphate buffer pH 7.2 used elsewhere for UV assay.

overview.main_observation: Most formulations released more than 80–90% of rabeprazole over 10 h, with higher Carbopol 934 concentration (124_F4) showing stronger sustaining effect; combinations with HPMC and NaCMC also produced sustained release.
overview.release_mechanism_conclusion: The article presents zero-order and first-order plots but does not provide explicit model-fitting parameters (e.g., R² values) or a definitive mechanistic classification (Higuchi, Korsmeyer–Peppas).

zero_order_cumulative_percent_release.description: Cumulative percent of drug released over time as reported in Table 4.

first_order_release_%ARA.description: Values labelled '% ARA' in Table 5; these likely correspond to log-transformed or residual amounts but exact definition is not given.

release_kinetics_summary.higuchi_model_evaluated: Mentioned in table heading ('Zero order and Higuchi'); detailed Higuchi-specific parameters not listed.
release_kinetics_summary.mechanism_of_drug_release: Not explicitly analysed; generally consistent with diffusion-controlled release from hydrophilic polymer matrices but not quantified.

environmental_factors.pH_effect: Tablets were exposed to acidic medium first, then to phosphate buffer, mimicking gastric then intestinal pH; no enzyme presence described.
environmental_factors.agitation: Paddle stirring in USP-II apparatus (speed not specified).

studies: Dissolution testing in acidic medium for 2 h followed by phosphate buffer for 10 h using USP-II apparatus.
Flow property measurements (angle of repose, densities, Carr’s index).
Physical evaluations (hardness, thickness, weight variation, drug content).
in_vitro.statistical_analysis: No formal statistical comparisons or ANOVA reported; results presented as mean values without SD or p-values.
key_performance_findings: All physicochemical tablet parameters were within acceptable limits for hardness, weight variation, and drug content.
Formulations generally achieved 80–90% drug release within 10 h, indicating sustained-release behaviour.
Higher Carbopol 934 concentration (124_F4) produced more prolonged release compared to HPMC-only formulation (124_F1).
Combinations of HPMC and Sodium CMC (e.g., 124_F5) also showed sustained release comparable to Carbopol-containing formulations.

notes_on_missing_or_non_applicable_items: Percent of pellets is not applicable because dosage form is a matrix tablet, not pellets.
Exact compression pressure/force, punch size, and machine settings are not reported.
Numerical friability values, standard deviations for any measurement, disintegration times, swelling index, floating/lag time, and stability study details are not provided in the article.
FT-IR data are summarized qualitatively (compatibility) without specific peak positions or spectra.

ingredients_identification.preparation: Samples of MTG, optimized floating tablet, polymers, and pure drug were analyzed using FTIR with KBr pellets, scanned over 4000-400 cm^-1.
ingredients_identification.results: Compatibility confirmed; peaks of MTG maintained in formulation.

absorbance.M3_formulation_peaks: Similar peaks with slight change in intensity, no shifting

data.pre_compression_parameters: Not reported per batch; powder blends were assessed but data not provided.

ftir_measurements.method: KBr pellet method, scan range 4000-400 cm^-1
ftir_measurements.results: Compatibility confirmed; no new peaks or shifting observed.

in_vivo_radiographic_study.findings: Tablets remained afloat in gastric fluid for up to 12 hours

stability_study.result: No significant changes in physical properties, buoyancy, and drug release pattern; similarity >90%

article_title: Preparation, Characterization and Evaluation of Flavonolignan Silymarin Effervescent Floating Matrix Tablets for Enhanced Oral Bioavailability

article_citation.title: Preparation, Characterization and Evaluation of Flavonolignan Silymarin Effervescent Floating Matrix Tablets for Enhanced Oral Bioavailability
article_citation.authors: Ahmad S., Khan J.A., Kausar T.N., Mahnashi M.H., Alasiri A., Alqahtani A.A., Alqahtani T.S., Walbi I.A., Alshehri O.M., Elnoubi O.A., et al.

experimental_design_optimization.levels: Varying amounts as per Table 7 (see formulations)

ftir_info.instrument: Thermo Fisher FTIR, Waltham, MA, USA
ftir_info.key_peaks_drug_silymarin: OH at 3300, C-H at 2900, C=O at 1635, aromatic C=C at 1500 and 1456 cm⁻¹
ftir_info.key_peaks_carbopol: OH at 3300, alkyl at 3000, OH at 2372, COOH at 1697, carbonyl at 1234 cm⁻¹
ftir_info.key_peaks_HPMC_K4: OH at 3400, C-H at 2850, C=C at 1600, -CO and -CH at 1053 and 947 cm⁻¹
ftir_info.key_peaks_HPMC_K15: OH at 3400, C-H at 2900, C=C at 1600, -CO and -CH at 1053 and 947 cm⁻¹
ftir_info.compatibility_conclusion: No significant spectral shift in physical mixture and optimized formulation, indicating no chemical interaction

tablet_fabrication_process.mixing: Ingredients sieved, mixed thoroughly for 15 min, then Mg stearate added and mixed for 2-3 min

process_parameters.equipment: Single punch tablet press (Emmy Enterprise), USP Type II dissolution apparatus, UV spectrophotometer (Lambda 21, PerkinElmer), DSC (Perkin Elmer), HPLC (Sykam, Germany)

swelling_index.note: Figure 3 shows swelling index for formulations; optimized formulation (126_SF10) exhibited excellent swelling pattern. No numerical values per batch provided in text.
